title: Comment on "Extension of the adiabatic theorem"
summary: Phys. Rev. B 113, 165102 (2026) proposed the conjecture that, for quantum quenches within the same phase, the overlap between the initial ground state and postquench eigenstates is maximal for the postquench ground state. We show that this conjecture is not valid in general. An explicit local, translationally invariant, gapped free-fermion counterexample exists even though the pre- and postquench Hamiltonians are connected by a symmetry-preserving gapped path and the thermodynamic-limit spectrum is continuous.

AI.tldr: The paper disproves a conjecture about quantum quenches, showing that the overlap between initial ground states and postquench eigenstates is not necessarily maximal for the postquench ground state, even under favorable conditions.
AI.motivation: To test the validity of a conjecture proposed in Phys. Rev. B 113, 165102 (2026) regarding quantum quenches within the same phase, which claimed that the overlap between initial ground states and postquench eigenstates is maximal for the postquench ground state.
AI.method: Constructs an explicit counterexample using a local, translationally invariant, gapped free-fermion system where the pre- and postquench Hamiltonians are connected by a symmetry-preserving gapped path with continuous thermodynamic-limit spectrum.
AI.result: Demonstrates that the conjecture is not generally valid by showing that even under the specified favorable conditions (same phase, symmetry-preserving gapped path, continuous spectrum), the maximal overlap does not necessarily occur with the postquench ground state.
AI.conclusion: The conjecture about quantum quenches within the same phase is disproven, establishing that the overlap between initial ground states and postquench eigenstates can be non-maximal for the postquench ground state even in well-behaved systems.

title: Thermodynamic Curvature and the Widom Ridge in Interacting Spin Systems
summary: We develop a geometric formulation of thermodynamic response in the classical Ising model by defining a curvature field over the control manifold spanned by inverse temperature $β$ and magnetic field $h$. We show that the existence of nontrivial curvature depends sensitively on the choice of control variables: while the $(J,h)$ manifold at fixed temperature is integrable and exhibits zero curvature, the $(β,h)$ manifold supports a finite curvature field arising from variations of the statistical ensemble. This curvature is given by a mixed derivative of the free energy and can be expressed directly as the covariance between energy and magnetization fluctuations. We evaluate the curvature field using Monte Carlo sampling and demonstrate that it develops a pronounced ridge structure extending from the critical point into the supercritical regime. This identifies the Widom line as a geometric feature of control space, corresponding to a locus of maximal thermodynamic response. More generally, the formulation provides a direct connection between geometric thermodynamics, critical phenomena, and experimentally accessible observables, and suggests that thermodynamic curvature may be probed through measurements of work performed under cyclic driving protocols.

AI.tldr: Geometric thermodynamics approach reveals curvature in Ising model control space, with Widom line emerging as ridge of maximal curvature connecting to critical point.
AI.motivation: To develop a geometric formulation of thermodynamic response in classical systems, connecting geometric thermodynamics with critical phenomena and experimentally accessible observables.
AI.method: Define curvature field over control manifold (β,h) using mixed derivative of free energy; evaluate via Monte Carlo sampling; analyze ridge structure in curvature field.
AI.result: (β,h) manifold exhibits finite curvature (zero in (J,h) manifold); curvature forms pronounced ridge extending from critical point into supercritical regime, identifying Widom line as geometric feature; curvature equals covariance between energy and magnetization fluctuations.
AI.conclusion: Thermodynamic curvature provides geometric characterization of critical phenomena, connects to experimental observables, and may be probed through work measurements in cyclic driving protocols.

title: Quantum many-body operator cascade as a route to chaos
summary: Dynamical properties of classical chaotic systems, for instance relaxation, can be understood as emerging from the time evolution of initially smooth long-wavelength densities to ever finer short-wavelength densities with fractal structure. Whether there is any analogous fractality by which one could characterize quantum many-body chaos is not known. By studying the spectral properties of the truncated operator propagator, we provide such structures. Namely, we show that the slowest-decaying operators, i.e., the leading Ruelle-Pollicott eigenvectors, have a nontrivial fractal dimension quantifying their non-locality, visible also in the divergence of their condition numbers. Furthermore, we find that unitarity imposes a constraint, i.e., an (approximate) equality, between the temporal decay rate of local correlations and this spatial operator fractal dimension. With this insight, a scenario for many-body quantum chaos becomes clear: over time, local operators evolve towards increasingly non-local ones with a quantifiable fractal structure, thereby naturally leading to effective non-unitary relaxation on the subspace of local operators - a kind of many-body Kolmogorov cascade in the space of operators. Our predictions are demonstrated in various quantum circuits: the kicked Ising model, brickwall circuits with a random 2-qubit gate, and dual-unitary circuits, where our results are exact.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that quantum many-body chaos exhibits fractal structures in operator space, analogous to classical chaos, with Ruelle-Pollicott eigenvectors having nontrivial fractal dimensions that constrain relaxation rates.
AI.motivation: To understand whether quantum many-body chaos exhibits analogous fractal structures to classical chaotic systems, where relaxation emerges from the evolution of smooth densities to fractal ones.
AI.method: Study spectral properties of truncated operator propagator, analyze Ruelle-Pollicott eigenvectors' fractal dimensions via condition numbers, and examine unitarity constraints on decay rates and fractal dimensions across various quantum circuits.
AI.result: Leading Ruelle-Pollicott eigenvectors have nontrivial fractal dimensions quantifying non-locality; unitarity imposes equality between temporal decay rates and spatial fractal dimensions; local operators evolve toward fractal non-local operators.
AI.conclusion: Quantum many-body chaos involves a fractal cascade in operator space where local operators evolve into non-local fractal structures, explaining effective non-unitary relaxation as a many-body Kolmogorov cascade in operator space.

title: Solution of the Ising model with Brascamp-Kunz boundary conditions by the transfer matrix method
summary: The square lattice Ising model under the Brascamp-Kunz boundary conditions is a well-known exactly solvable lattice model. The exact solution of this system has been derived within the framework of Pfaffian-type method. In this paper we provide a derivation for the solution by the Schultz-Mattis-Lieb method in the transfer matrix formalism. We set special interactions on the boundaries and take certain limit of these interactions, so that the system under the Brascamp-Kunz boundary conditions is transformed into another system under the toroidal boundary conditions. The Schultz-Mattis-Lieb method is applied to the mapping system and the partition function is exactly solved in the fermionic representation. The Fisher zeros are analytically calculated and the physical critical point is identified. We also discuss the difference between the transfer matrix approaches to the Brascamp-Kunz and to the toroidal boundary conditions. Our work introduces a member to the family of transfer-matrix-based studies for Ising model under various boundary conditions.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an alternative derivation of the exact solution for the square lattice Ising model under Brascamp-Kunz boundary conditions using the Schultz-Mattis-Lieb transfer matrix method, transforming it to toroidal boundary conditions via boundary interaction limits.
AI.motivation: To provide an alternative derivation of the exact solution for the square lattice Ising model under Brascamp-Kunz boundary conditions using the Schultz-Mattis-Lieb transfer matrix method, complementing existing Pfaffian-type approaches and expanding the family of transfer-matrix-based studies for Ising models under different boundary conditions.
AI.method: The Schultz-Mattis-Lieb method in transfer matrix formalism is applied. Special interactions are set on boundaries and taken to certain limits, transforming the Brascamp-Kunz boundary system into a toroidal boundary system. The method is then applied to this mapped system, solving the partition function exactly in fermionic representation.
AI.result: The partition function is exactly solved in fermionic representation. Fisher zeros are analytically calculated, and the physical critical point is identified. The difference between transfer matrix approaches for Brascamp-Kunz and toroidal boundary conditions is discussed.
AI.conclusion: The work successfully demonstrates an alternative derivation using transfer matrix methods, introduces a new member to the family of transfer-matrix-based studies for Ising models under various boundary conditions, and provides analytical insights into Fisher zeros and critical behavior.

title: Late Breaking Results: Hardware-Aware Compilation Reshapes Trainability in Variational Quantum Circuits
summary: Variational quantum circuits (VQCs) are typically evaluated at the logical design level when analyzing trainability. However, execution on real quantum devices requires hardware-aware compilation (transpilation) to satisfy qubit connectivity and native gate-set constraints. In this paper, we examine how transpilation can alter the gradient statistics. Using parameter-shift differentiation and gradient variance estimation, we compare logical and transpiled circuits across three representative ansatz families: EfficientSU2 (dense entanglement), TTN (tree tensor network), and RealAmplitudes (linear entanglement). We observe architecture-dependent trainability shifts where densely entangling circuits exhibit pronounced gradient reshaping in shallow regimes, structured tensor-network circuits remain comparatively robust, and linear architectures show mixed behavior. Deep circuits across all families display minimal sensitivity to hardware-aware compilation. These findings demonstrate that transpilation acts as an implicit structural transformation of the optimization landscape, motivating compilation-aware analysis and co-design for VQCs.

AI.tldr: Transpilation of variational quantum circuits alters gradient statistics in architecture-dependent ways, affecting trainability differently across ansatz families.
AI.motivation: While VQCs are typically analyzed at logical design level, real quantum hardware requires transpilation to satisfy connectivity and gate constraints, which may affect gradient statistics and trainability.
AI.method: Used parameter-shift differentiation and gradient variance estimation to compare logical vs. transpiled circuits across three ansatz families: EfficientSU2 (dense entanglement), TTN (tree tensor network), and RealAmplitudes (linear entanglement).
AI.result: Densely entangling circuits show pronounced gradient reshaping in shallow regimes; structured tensor-network circuits remain robust; linear architectures show mixed behavior; deep circuits across all families show minimal sensitivity to transpilation.
AI.conclusion: Transpilation acts as implicit structural transformation of optimization landscape, motivating compilation-aware analysis and co-design for VQCs.

title: Comment on "Specific heat of an ideal Bose gas above the Bose condensation temperature," [Am. J. Phys. 72(9), 1193--1194 (2004)]
comment: Submitted to the American Journal of Physics on April 20, 2026. Comment on https://doi.org/10.1119/1.1764562
summary: We examine the English translation of Albert Einstein's groundbreaking 1925 paper on Bose-Einstein condensation. We guide readers to execute the calculations Einstein outlined for the specific heat above the condensation temperature, correct some numerical errors, and compare his formula with a different one published in the American Journal of Physics in 2004. The history of the acceptance of Einstein's theory will be summarized.

AI.tldr: Analysis of Einstein's 1925 Bose-Einstein condensation paper translation, including calculation guidance, error correction, formula comparison with 2004 work, and historical context of theory acceptance.
AI.motivation: To provide a comprehensive analysis of Einstein's seminal 1925 paper on Bose-Einstein condensation through English translation, clarify his calculations for specific heat above condensation temperature, correct numerical errors, and compare his theoretical approach with modern interpretations.
AI.method: Translation and analysis of Einstein's original 1925 paper, execution of his outlined calculations for specific heat above condensation temperature, correction of numerical errors, comparison with 2004 American Journal of Physics formula, and historical research on theory acceptance.
AI.result: Successful English translation of Einstein's paper, execution of his specific heat calculations with corrected numerical values, identification of differences between Einstein's 1925 formula and 2004 alternative, and documentation of the historical trajectory of Bose-Einstein condensation theory acceptance.
AI.conclusion: Einstein's 1925 paper provides foundational theoretical framework for Bose-Einstein condensation, with some numerical errors that can be corrected through modern analysis. The comparison with 2004 formula reveals different mathematical approaches to the same physical phenomenon, while historical analysis shows the gradual acceptance of this quantum statistical theory over decades.

title: Fundamental temperature in the superstatistical description of non-equilibrium steady states
summary: Among the statistical mechanical frameworks able to describe systems in non-equilibrium steady states such as collisionless plasmas, self-gravitating systems and other complex systems, superstatistics have gained recent attention. Superstatistics postulates a superposition of canonical systems with inverse temperatures $β$ described by a probability distribution depending on the external conditions. Unfortunately, the uncertainty about $β$ cannot be attributed to fluctuations of a phase space function, and this suggests that the distribution of $β$ is purely of statistical nature and must be inferred rather than measured. This lack of direct observability of the superstatistical temperature then becomes a conceptual issue in need of resolution. In this work we address this issue, showing that a mapping exists between functions of the superstatistical temperature and functions of the recently proposed fundamental temperature, a model-dependent function of the energy, in such a way that their expectation values coincide. We illustrate the use of this mapping by computing the conditional distribution of inverse temperature given energy for the $q$-canonical ensemble, as well as the full inverse temperature distribution, without the use of Laplace inversion.

AI.tldr: Superstatistics describes non-equilibrium systems via superposition of canonical ensembles with varying inverse temperatures β, but β's statistical nature (not directly observable) creates conceptual issues. This work resolves this by mapping superstatistical temperature functions to fundamental temperature functions, enabling computation of conditional distributions without Laplace inversion.
AI.motivation: Superstatistics provides a framework for non-equilibrium steady states (collisionless plasmas, self-gravitating systems), but the inverse temperature β is not directly observable as it cannot be attributed to phase space fluctuations. This creates a conceptual problem where β's distribution must be inferred rather than measured, requiring resolution.
AI.method: The authors establish a mapping between functions of the superstatistical temperature and functions of the fundamental temperature (a model-dependent energy function). This mapping ensures their expectation values coincide, allowing computation of conditional distributions without Laplace inversion techniques.
AI.result: The mapping enables direct computation of the conditional distribution of inverse temperature given energy for the q-canonical ensemble, as well as the full inverse temperature distribution, bypassing the need for Laplace inversion methods.
AI.conclusion: The conceptual issue of superstatistical temperature's lack of direct observability is resolved through a mapping to the fundamental temperature, providing a mathematically consistent framework for computing temperature distributions in non-equilibrium statistical mechanics.

title: Momentum reconstruction from Unruh-deWitt detectors
summary: We investigate momentum reconstruction for particle processes observed by Unruh-deWitt detector setups. In particular, we derive the probability distributions for particle momenta conditioned on detector clicks in three spatial dimensions. We investigate the statistical properties of such detector setups and discuss their use as models of measurement devices in particle physics.

AI.tldr: Derivation of momentum reconstruction probability distributions from Unruh-deWitt detector clicks in 3D, analyzing statistical properties and applications as particle physics measurement models.
AI.motivation: To develop theoretical framework for reconstructing particle momenta from Unruh-deWitt detector measurements, addressing how detector clicks can be used to infer momentum distributions in particle processes.
AI.method: Derivation of probability distributions for particle momenta conditioned on detector clicks in three spatial dimensions, using Unruh-deWitt detector setups as measurement models.
AI.result: Obtained momentum reconstruction probability distributions from detector measurements, analyzed statistical properties of such detector setups for particle momentum inference.
AI.conclusion: Unruh-deWitt detector setups provide viable models for particle momentum measurement devices, with derived probability distributions enabling momentum reconstruction from detector click data.

title: What Do Black Holes Teach Us About Wigner's Friend?
comment: Forthcoming in European Journal for Philosophy of Science
summary: Recently, Hausmann and Renner have pointed out that several famous paradoxes relating to black holes have a similar character to various Extended Wigner's Friend paradoxes. In this paper I consider what the connection between these things could teach us about the Wigner's Friend scenarios. I argue that if we take the analogy between these cases seriously, the black hole paradoxes appear to favour a certain class of response to the Wigner's Friend scenario - specifically, those which posit intrinsic relationality, rather than effective and emergent relationality, and also those which posit some kind of retrocausality.

AI.tldr: The paper explores connections between black hole paradoxes and Extended Wigner's Friend scenarios, arguing that black hole paradoxes favor intrinsic relationality and retrocausal interpretations in quantum mechanics.
AI.motivation: To investigate the connection between black hole paradoxes and Extended Wigner's Friend scenarios, and determine what insights from black hole physics can inform interpretations of quantum measurement scenarios.
AI.method: Analogical reasoning and conceptual analysis comparing the structural similarities between black hole paradoxes (like information paradox, firewall paradox) and Extended Wigner's Friend scenarios, examining what consistent solutions to black hole paradoxes imply for quantum measurement interpretations.
AI.result: The analysis suggests that taking the analogy seriously leads to favoring interpretations with intrinsic relationality (rather than emergent relationality) and some form of retrocausality as more consistent solutions to both sets of paradoxes.
AI.conclusion: Black hole paradoxes provide evidence supporting quantum interpretations featuring intrinsic relationality and retrocausality, offering new constraints on viable solutions to Wigner's Friend scenarios based on insights from gravitational physics.

title: Activation and Avalanche Length Scales in the Finite-Temperature Creep of an Elastic Interface
summary: We investigate the creep dynamics of a driven elastic line at finite temperature, well below the depinning threshold. We show that creep is governed by two distinct length scales. The first, $\ell_{\mathrm{opt}}$, corresponds to the optimal activated rearrangements that control the dynamics' bottleneck and remains essentially temperature-independent. The second, $\ell_{\mathrm{av}}$, characterizes the spatial extent of thermally activated avalanches and grows as temperature decreases. By combining structural and dynamical observables, we show that $\ell_{\mathrm{av}}$ governs both the crossover in the structure factor and the growth of the four-point dynamical susceptibility, while the relaxation time remains controlled by activation over large barriers associated with $\ell_{\mathrm{opt}}$. We find that the avalanche scale follows $\ell_{\mathrm{av}}(T)\sim T^{-ν_{\mathrm{dep}}}$, thereby selecting a unique scenario among competing theoretical predictions. These results establish a unified picture of finite-temperature creep in which activation controls temporal scales while depinning criticality governs spatial correlations.

AI.tldr: Creep dynamics of driven elastic line below depinning threshold reveals two distinct length scales: temperature-independent optimal activation scale ℓ_opt controlling relaxation time, and temperature-dependent avalanche scale ℓ_av governing spatial correlations and growing as ℓ_av(T)∼T^{-ν_dep}.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental mechanisms of creep dynamics in driven elastic systems at finite temperature below the depinning threshold, particularly how thermal activation interacts with depinning criticality to govern both temporal and spatial aspects of the dynamics.
AI.method: Investigation of creep dynamics through analysis of two distinct length scales: ℓ_opt for optimal activated rearrangements and ℓ_av for thermally activated avalanches. Combined structural and dynamical observables including structure factor, four-point dynamical susceptibility, and relaxation time measurements.
AI.result: ℓ_opt remains essentially temperature-independent and controls relaxation time via activation over large barriers. ℓ_av grows as temperature decreases following ℓ_av(T)∼T^{-ν_dep}, governs crossover in structure factor and growth of four-point dynamical susceptibility, and selects a unique scenario among competing theoretical predictions.
AI.conclusion: Establishes unified picture of finite-temperature creep where activation controls temporal scales while depinning criticality governs spatial correlations, with avalanche scale following specific temperature scaling that resolves theoretical debates.

title: Verifying random matrix product states with autoregressive local measurements
summary: Matrix product states (MPS) are a central language for one-dimensional quantum matter and a practical target for near-term quantum simulators and variational algorithms. Yet, while substantial effort has focused on preparing MPS with shallow circuits, scalable methods to \emph{verify} that a many-body device has actually produced the intended state remain underdeveloped. Direct fidelity estimation (DFE) relies only on local Pauli measurements, but in many-body settings it suffers an exponential classical overhead from the preprocessing needed to sample Pauli strings. We eliminate this obstacle by introducing an \emph{autoregressive} importance sampler that draws Pauli strings sequentially from efficiently computable conditional distributions, reducing the per-shot classical overhead to linear scaling in the number of qubits. We further develop a grouped extension that constructs qubit-wise commuting measurement settings via a \emph{sorting string} and simultaneously estimates the entire commuting group from a single setting, significantly reducing estimator variance while preserving efficient postprocessing. Our approach extends naturally to matrix product operators (MPO), enabling scalable verification of tensor-network states and observables in long one-dimensional quantum systems. We utilize random MPS as a natural benchmark for generic 1D entangled states.

AI.tldr: Autoregressive importance sampling for efficient direct fidelity estimation of matrix product states using local Pauli measurements with linear classical overhead.
AI.motivation: Scalable verification methods for MPS preparation in quantum devices are underdeveloped; existing DFE suffers exponential classical overhead from Pauli string sampling preprocessing.
AI.method: Autoregressive importance sampler draws Pauli strings sequentially from efficiently computable conditional distributions, plus grouped extension using sorting strings to construct qubit-wise commuting measurement settings.
AI.result: Reduces per-shot classical overhead to linear scaling in qubits, significantly reduces estimator variance while preserving efficient postprocessing, extends naturally to MPOs.
AI.conclusion: Enables scalable verification of tensor-network states and observables in long 1D quantum systems, with random MPS as benchmark for generic 1D entangled states.

title: Self-averaging parameter estimation for coarse-grained particle models
summary: We introduce a parameter estimation method that utilizes microscopic data, specifically averages and correlations of selected microscopic observables, to determine the parameters of a stochastic differential equation governing coarse-grained degrees of freedom. The method is not limited to static parameters found in the reversible part of the coarse-grained dynamics, such as those in the free energy function or potential of mean force, but also extends to dynamic parameters, including friction coefficients. The method couples the stochastic differential equation with free parameters to dynamic equations for the parameters. The coupled system self-averages, according to Anosov-Kifer's theorem, in such a way that the final state of the parameters gives coincidence between the microscopic and mesoscopic averages and correlations of selected observables. The method is validated in two examples: a Brownian particle in a harmonic potential, and a set of Brownian particles interacting hydrodynamically with the Rotne-Prager-Yamakawa mobility tensor. This latter case illustrates how the method can be used not only to determine coefficients but also state dependent transport properties - in this case, the position dependent form of the mobility tensor. The parameter estimation for these two models yields excellent results. Subsequently we use the methodology to study a bimodal-mass Lennard-Jones fluid for which we infer both the potential of mean force between the heavy particles and its hydrodynamic mobility tensor.

AI.tldr: A parameter estimation method using microscopic data (averages and correlations) to determine parameters of stochastic differential equations for coarse-grained systems, including both static (free energy) and dynamic (friction coefficients) parameters.
AI.motivation: To develop a method that can estimate both static and dynamic parameters in coarse-grained stochastic models from microscopic data, overcoming limitations of existing methods that typically focus only on static parameters like free energy functions.
AI.method: The method couples the stochastic differential equation with free parameters to dynamic equations for the parameters themselves. This coupled system self-averages according to Anosov-Kifer's theorem, ensuring that the final parameter state yields coincidence between microscopic and mesoscopic averages and correlations of selected observables.
AI.result: The method is validated on two examples: (1) Brownian particle in harmonic potential, and (2) Brownian particles with Rotne-Prager-Yamakawa hydrodynamic interactions. Both yield excellent parameter estimation results. The method is further applied to a bimodal-mass Lennard-Jones fluid to infer both potential of mean force and hydrodynamic mobility tensor.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method successfully estimates both static and dynamic parameters from microscopic data, extending beyond traditional approaches limited to static parameters. It enables determination of state-dependent transport properties and shows promise for complex systems like the bimodal-mass Lennard-Jones fluid.

title: GreenPeas: Unlocking Adaptive Quantum Error Correction with Just-in-Time Decoding Hypergraphs
summary: Circuit-level decoders are essential for the realisation of low-overhead fault-tolerant quantum computing. However, they rely on complex hypergraphs that are traditionally compiled ahead-of-time. This static approach introduces a significant bottleneck for an emerging class of adaptive circuits, where the structure is modified during execution based on mid-circuit measurement outcomes. Pre-compiling hypergraphs for all possible circuit branches would incur an exponential memory cost, rendering current tools impractical for these workloads. Hence, we introduce GreenPeas, a C++/CUDA toolchain for the high-speed, just-in-time compilation of decoding hypergraphs. By lowering the circuit to a space-time error propagation graph, we show how Stim's backtracking algorithm can be mapped efficiently onto massively parallel GPU architectures, decomposing the O(nl) workload for a circuit with n qubits and l gate layers across thousands of concurrent threads. Our implementation achieves a greater than 10x average speedup over the Stim baseline across two of the leading fault-tolerant architectures: the surface and bivariate bicycle codes. As a key use case, we demonstrate that this speedup enables circuit-level decoding of adaptive syndrome measurement circuits, unlocking a regime previously restricted to less accurate phenomenological decoders. We aim to open-source GreenPeas to support the research of future adaptive circuit protocols.

AI.tldr: GreenPeas is a GPU-accelerated JIT compiler for quantum error correction decoding hypergraphs, enabling efficient circuit-level decoding of adaptive quantum circuits that was previously impractical.
AI.motivation: Traditional static compilation of decoding hypergraphs creates a bottleneck for adaptive quantum circuits where circuit structure changes based on mid-circuit measurements, making current tools impractical due to exponential memory costs for pre-compiling all possible circuit branches.
AI.method: GreenPeas is a C++/CUDA toolchain that lowers quantum circuits to space-time error propagation graphs and maps Stim's backtracking algorithm onto massively parallel GPU architectures, decomposing the O(nl) workload across thousands of concurrent threads.
AI.result: Achieves >10x average speedup over Stim baseline for surface and bivariate bicycle codes, enabling circuit-level decoding of adaptive syndrome measurement circuits previously restricted to less accurate phenomenological decoders.
AI.conclusion: GreenPeas unlocks efficient circuit-level decoding for adaptive quantum circuits, supporting future adaptive circuit protocol research and addressing a critical bottleneck in fault-tolerant quantum computing.

title: Stability and breakdown of chiral motion in non-reciprocal flocking
summary: We study a two-species Vicsek model with intra-species alignment and asymmetric inter-species couplings, where one species aligns with the other while the latter anti-aligns. Motivated by recent results showing that globally coherent chiral motion is not a generic large-scale state of finite-range non-reciprocal flocking, we ask whether a chiral state can nevertheless be stabilized in the discrete-time, metric, non-reciprocal two-species Vicsek model, and if so, under what conditions. For equal populations and motilities, we show that such a state exists only within a restricted window characterized by high density, very low self-propulsion speed, and small system size relative to the interaction range. Within this window, we also find that chirality appears primarily when aligning interactions dominate over anti-alignment, whereas stronger anti-alignment leads to species segregation and suppresses chirality. Conversely, introducing species asymmetry through population imbalance drives transitions from chiral states to porous parallel-flocking or anti-parallel-flocking liquids; motility imbalance induces asynchronous oscillations and, in extreme cases, leads to segregation into moving clusters of the faster species within a more dispersed background of slower particles. Overall, these results indicate that chirality in the non-reciprocal two-species Vicsek model arises within a restricted regime set by density, motility, inter-species coupling, and system size, rather than being a generic outcome of non-reciprocal interactions.

AI.tldr: Chiral motion in non-reciprocal two-species Vicsek model requires specific conditions: high density, low speed, small system size relative to interaction range, and aligning-dominated interactions.
AI.motivation: To determine whether chiral states can be stabilized in discrete-time, metric, non-reciprocal two-species Vicsek models despite previous findings that globally coherent chiral motion is not generic for finite-range non-reciprocal flocking.
AI.method: Study of two-species Vicsek model with intra-species alignment and asymmetric inter-species couplings (one species aligns with the other while the latter anti-aligns), analyzing conditions for chiral state emergence.
AI.result: Chiral states exist only within restricted window: high density, very low self-propulsion speed, small system size relative to interaction range. Chirality appears primarily when aligning interactions dominate over anti-alignment. Species asymmetry through population imbalance drives transitions to porous parallel/anti-parallel flocking liquids; motility imbalance induces asynchronous oscillations and segregation.
AI.conclusion: Chirality in non-reciprocal two-species Vicsek model arises within a restricted regime determined by density, motility, inter-species coupling, and system size, rather than being a generic outcome of non-reciprocal interactions.

title: Entropy Moduli and BKM Coercivity for Rank-Deficient Non-Commutative Markov Semigroups
summary: We study entropy-based certification for non-commutative Markov semigroups with rank-deficient stationary states. We introduce a boundary-activation functional that quantifies deviation from the support of the stationary state and prove a logarithmic lower bound on relative entropy in terms of this quantity, based on a BKM coercivity argument and an exact block-decomposition identity. Combining this estimate with dynamical bounds for Davies semigroups under a modified logarithmic Sobolev inequality, we show that, under explicit local conditions, entropy-based certification of convergence can exhibit a slowdown near the boundary regime, with a rate of order e^{-alpha t}/sqrt(alpha t). The mechanism is identified as a coherence-population effect and disappears for incoherent initial states, where classical Pinsker-type bounds are recovered.

AI.tldr: Entropy-based certification for non-commutative Markov semigroups with rank-deficient stationary states shows slowdown near boundary regime with rate e^{-αt}/√(αt) due to coherence-population effect.
AI.motivation: To study entropy-based certification for non-commutative Markov semigroups with rank-deficient stationary states, particularly understanding convergence behavior near the boundary regime where classical bounds may not apply.
AI.method: Introduce boundary-activation functional to quantify deviation from stationary state support; prove logarithmic lower bound on relative entropy using BKM coercivity argument and exact block-decomposition identity; combine with dynamical bounds for Davies semigroups under modified logarithmic Sobolev inequality.
AI.result: Under explicit local conditions, entropy-based certification exhibits slowdown near boundary regime with rate order e^{-αt}/√(αt); mechanism identified as coherence-population effect; classical Pinsker-type bounds recovered for incoherent initial states.
AI.conclusion: Coherence in initial states causes convergence slowdown near boundary regime in non-commutative Markov semigroups with rank-deficient stationary states, requiring modified entropy-based certification bounds that account for quantum coherence effects.

title: Density Profiles and Direct Correlation Functions from Density Functional Theory in Binary Hard-Sphere Crystals: Substitutional Solid and Interstitial Solid Solution
summary: We determine the fully resolved equilibrium density profiles for two binary hard-sphere crystal structures using classical density functional theory through the White Bear II functional from fundamental measure theory. While for the substitutional crystal, in which some hard spheres are replaced by spheres of slightly smaller diameter, the density profiles are rather similar to the single-component case (narrow Gaussian peaks centered at fcc lattice sites), we observe a more complex behavior for the case of interstitial solid solutions, where the small species is fairly delocalized in the unit cell. Further, we compute the species-resolved inhomogeneous two-body direct correlation functions for these two types of binary crystals. The large-large components are mainly determined by the vacancy concentration $n_\text{vac}$ and show a characteristic magnitude $~1/n_\text{vac}$. Based on this observation, we propose a simple geometric picture of this six-dimensional function. The components of the direct correlation function involving the small spheres substantially differ in interstitial solid solutions from those of the substitutional crystal.

AI.tldr: Binary hard-sphere crystal equilibrium density profiles and two-body direct correlation functions analyzed using classical density functional theory with White Bear II functional.
AI.motivation: To understand and characterize the equilibrium density profiles and species-resolved two-body direct correlation functions for two types of binary hard-sphere crystal structures: substitutional crystals and interstitial solid solutions.
AI.method: Classical density functional theory using the White Bear II functional from fundamental measure theory to compute fully resolved equilibrium density profiles and species-resolved inhomogeneous two-body direct correlation functions for binary hard-sphere crystals.
AI.result: Substitutional crystals show narrow Gaussian peaks similar to single-component cases, while interstitial solid solutions exhibit more complex behavior with small species being fairly delocalized. Large-large direct correlation components scale as ~1/n_vac and are determined by vacancy concentration, enabling a simple geometric picture. Components involving small spheres differ substantially between the two crystal types.
AI.conclusion: Binary hard-sphere crystals exhibit fundamentally different structural properties depending on whether they are substitutional or interstitial, with significant implications for their direct correlation functions and microscopic organization.

title: Non-Associativity Induced Modifications of Open-System Quantum Dynamics: General Master Equation and a Two-Qubit Ising Case Study
summary: Nonassociative deformations of phase-space structures arise naturally in the presence of magnetic charge, where the Jacobi identity for momentum components fails and the corresponding Moyal product becomes nonassociative. While such structures are well understood at the level of single-particle kinematics, their implications for open-system quantum dynamics remain largely unexplored. Here we derive a Born-Markov master equation for a system coupled to a bath when the underlying operator product is weakly nonassociative. The deformation enters through associators appearing in the second-order kernel, while pairwise operator products and dissipators retain their standard form. The resulting correction is dispersive and modifies the Liouville-von Neumann part of the generator without introducing additional dissipative channels. We then embed this structure into a two-qubit transverse-field Ising model using a Stratonovich-Weyl representation and an Ising-aligned twisted Poisson structure. In the zero-temperature limit, the nonassociative terms produce a nonlinear correction in which the instantaneous population imbalance of each qubit feeds back into the dynamics as a state-dependent longitudinal field. Numerical simulations in the weak-coupling regime, where the Born-Markov derivation is quantitatively controlled, show that increasing the nonassociativity parameter suppresses steady-state entanglement by up to 59%, reduces purity, and increases entropy, while leaving the relaxation timescale set by the dissipative rates unchanged. These results demonstrate that weak nonassociativity manifests as a coherent, population-dependent deformation of open-system dynamics rather than an additional dissipative mechanism.

AI.tldr: Nonassociative phase-space deformations from magnetic charge lead to dispersive corrections in open quantum systems, suppressing entanglement and increasing entropy without affecting relaxation times.
AI.motivation: Nonassociative structures from magnetic charge are understood for single-particle kinematics but their effects on open quantum systems remain unexplored, motivating investigation of how weak nonassociativity modifies open-system dynamics.
AI.method: Derived Born-Markov master equation for systems with weakly nonassociative operator products, embedding structure into two-qubit transverse-field Ising model using Stratonovich-Weyl representation and Ising-aligned twisted Poisson structure, with numerical simulations in weak-coupling regime.
AI.result: Nonassociative terms produce dispersive correction modifying Liouville-von Neumann part without additional dissipative channels; increasing nonassociativity parameter suppresses steady-state entanglement by up to 59%, reduces purity, increases entropy, while leaving relaxation timescale unchanged.
AI.conclusion: Weak nonassociativity manifests as coherent, population-dependent deformation of open-system dynamics rather than additional dissipative mechanism, demonstrating how magnetic-charge-induced phase-space structures affect quantum information processing.

title: Fractional motions of an active particle on the quantum vortex
summary: We analytically investigate the diffusive motion inferred from experimental observations of active particles driven by quantum vortices on the surface of superfluid helium. We first study the dynamical behavior of an active particle subject to a viscoelastic memory effect characterized by a power-law kernel. We then analyze the dynamics of an active particle under a uniform vortex force, thermal noise, and viscous dissipation subject to a power-law kernel. Next, by including a harmonic confining force, we obtain analytical solutions for the joint probability density in two distinct time regimes.

AI.tldr: Analytical study of diffusive motion of active particles driven by quantum vortices on superfluid helium surface, examining viscoelastic memory effects, vortex forces, and confinement.
AI.motivation: To understand the diffusive behavior of active particles on superfluid helium surfaces, particularly how quantum vortices drive their motion and how viscoelastic memory effects influence their dynamics.
AI.method: Analytical investigation using mathematical modeling: 1) Study active particle with viscoelastic memory effect (power-law kernel), 2) Analyze dynamics under uniform vortex force, thermal noise, and viscous dissipation with power-law kernel, 3) Include harmonic confining force and obtain analytical solutions for joint probability density in two time regimes.
AI.result: Obtained analytical solutions for the joint probability density function describing particle motion in two distinct time regimes when including harmonic confinement, providing mathematical framework for understanding diffusive behavior.
AI.conclusion: The analytical framework successfully models complex active particle dynamics on superfluid helium surfaces, capturing viscoelastic memory effects, vortex-driven motion, and confinement effects through mathematical solutions applicable to different time regimes.

title: Resource-Efficient Quantum-Enhanced Compressive Imaging via Quantum Classical co-Design
summary: Quantum sensing can enhance imaging performance by reducing measurement noise below the classical limit, thereby improving the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of acquired data. In conventional quantum imaging schemes, squeezing is applied independently to each pixel or spatial mode, leading to a quantum resource cost that scales linearly with image dimension. This approach implicitly separates quantum enhancement from classical post-processing, treating them as independent layers. In this work, we demonstrate that integrating quantum resource allocation with the guidance from classical compressive imaging, via co-design between the quantum hardware layer and the classical software layer, substantially reduces the required quantum resources. We employ principal component analysis (PCA) to identify a low-dimensional principal component subspace for measurement and apply squeezing selectively to the most informative spatial modes corresponding to these principal components. Our numerical experiments show that high-accuracy image classification and high-fidelity image reconstruction can be achieved with significantly fewer squeezed modes compared to pixel-wise squeezing. Our results establish a joint quantum classical co-design framework for resource-efficient quantum-enhanced imaging.

AI.tldr: Quantum-classical co-design framework reduces quantum resource requirements for imaging by selectively squeezing only the most informative spatial modes identified via PCA, rather than applying squeezing pixel-wise.
AI.motivation: Conventional quantum imaging schemes apply squeezing independently to each pixel, leading to linear scaling of quantum resource cost with image dimension. This approach treats quantum enhancement and classical post-processing as separate layers, missing opportunities for resource optimization.
AI.method: Integrate quantum resource allocation with classical compressive imaging guidance through co-design between quantum hardware and classical software layers. Use principal component analysis (PCA) to identify low-dimensional principal component subspace for measurement, then apply squeezing selectively only to the most informative spatial modes corresponding to these principal components.
AI.result: Numerical experiments demonstrate that high-accuracy image classification and high-fidelity image reconstruction can be achieved with significantly fewer squeezed modes compared to pixel-wise squeezing, substantially reducing required quantum resources.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a joint quantum-classical co-design framework for resource-efficient quantum-enhanced imaging that optimizes quantum resource allocation based on classical compressive imaging principles.

title: Emergent nonreciprocity in open thermodynamically-consistent chemical reaction networks
summary: Nonreciprocity, a hallmark of nonequilibrium systems, can generate dynamics not possible near thermodynamic equilibrium, including oscillatory and rotating patterns. The onset of temporal oscillations is often evident in linearized dynamics, where nonreciprocity appears as complex eigenvalues of an asymmetric Jacobian. Here, we show that the topology of open, thermodynamically-consistent chemical reaction networks can result in oscillatory instabilities near nonequilibrium steady states. These instabilities arise from chemostat-induced breaking of Onsager reciprocity, while the local equilibrium hypothesis preserves the variational structure of the dissipative part of the dynamics. Numerical results confirm that such nonreciprocity in reaction-diffusion systems produces oscillatory dynamics that nevertheless minimize a free energy.

AI.tldr: Nonreciprocity in chemical reaction networks enables oscillatory instabilities near nonequilibrium steady states through chemostat-induced breaking of Onsager reciprocity while preserving variational structure.
AI.motivation: To understand how nonreciprocity, a hallmark of nonequilibrium systems, can generate oscillatory dynamics in thermodynamically-consistent chemical reaction networks, particularly how network topology enables such instabilities near nonequilibrium steady states.
AI.method: Analysis of open chemical reaction network topology showing that chemostat-induced breaking of Onsager reciprocity creates oscillatory instabilities while preserving the variational structure of dissipative dynamics through local equilibrium hypothesis.
AI.result: Numerical results confirm that nonreciprocity in reaction-diffusion systems produces oscillatory dynamics that nevertheless minimize a free energy, demonstrating that oscillatory instabilities can arise from network topology near nonequilibrium steady states.
AI.conclusion: The topology of open chemical reaction networks can generate oscillatory instabilities through chemostat-induced nonreciprocity while maintaining thermodynamic consistency and variational structure, providing a mechanism for oscillatory patterns in nonequilibrium systems.

title: Effective Trace Framework for Self-Similar Casimir Systems
summary: The interaction of quantum fields with fractal and self-similar geometries encompasses multiple distinct physical regimes, including spectral geometry on intrinsic fractals, macroscopic self-similar Casimir configurations, and bounded Euclidean cavities with fractal boundaries. While the thermal equations of state and spectral asymptotics for these systems are well established, a cohesive treatment of the vacuum trace frequently conflates rigorous mathematical bounds with phenomenological models. In this manuscript, we systematically decouple these regimes and advance a unified effective framework combining the rigorous thermal trace of fractal radiation with a zero-temperature integrated vacuum trace for plate-like self-similar geometries. We demonstrate that for systems governed by a scale-dependent Casimir coefficient $C(d_s, \ln(d/\ell_*))$, the anisotropic stress-energy tensor produces an integrated vacuum trace proportional to its logarithmic running, $\partial_{\ln d}C$. We strictly differentiate this effective macroscopic backreaction from first-principles local trace anomalies on genuine fractal boundaries. Finally, we analyze finite-level ($n$) prefractal realizations, establishing the analytical prerequisites necessary to transition this effective formalism into a quantitatively predictive electromagnetic theory amenable to experimental verification.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a unified framework for quantum field interactions with fractal geometries, distinguishing between different physical regimes and providing an effective macroscopic treatment of vacuum trace effects.
AI.motivation: To address the conflation of rigorous mathematical bounds with phenomenological models in treatments of vacuum trace for quantum fields interacting with fractal and self-similar geometries, and to provide a systematic decoupling of different physical regimes.
AI.method: Systematically decouples three regimes: spectral geometry on intrinsic fractals, macroscopic self-similar Casimir configurations, and bounded Euclidean cavities with fractal boundaries. Combines rigorous thermal trace of fractal radiation with zero-temperature integrated vacuum trace for plate-like self-similar geometries.
AI.result: For systems with scale-dependent Casimir coefficient C(d_s, ln(d/ℓ_*)), the anisotropic stress-energy tensor produces an integrated vacuum trace proportional to its logarithmic running, ∂_{ln d}C. The framework distinguishes effective macroscopic backreaction from first-principles local trace anomalies on genuine fractal boundaries.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a unified effective framework that differentiates between mathematical rigor and phenomenological approaches, provides analytical prerequisites for finite-level prefractal realizations, and enables transition to quantitatively predictive electromagnetic theory for experimental verification.

title: Causality from Projection and Hardy-Space Analyticity of Non-Markovian Memory Kernels
comment: 8 pages, 2 figures; establishes Kramers-Kronig relations for non-Markovian memory kernels via Hardy-space theory; connects to Gavassino's causality-from-coarse-graining framework
summary: The Kramers-Kronig (KK) dispersion relations connect the real and imaginary parts of a causal response function through a Hilbert transform. While standard for linear-response theory, their validity for non-Markovian memory kernels -- the central object governing reduced dynamics in open quantum systems -- has not been rigorously established. We prove that the Nakajima-Zwanzig memory kernel belongs to the vector-valued Hardy space H+p(B) for every p>1, provided the initial system-bath state is factorized and the bath has a continuous spectral density. This membership yields rigorous KK reconstruction formulas. We establish three new results, including (i) a CP-Hardy obstruction theorem showing that upper-half-plane poles in approximate kernels imply non-physical (non-CPTP) dynamics; (ii) a passivity-analyticity theorem connecting dissipative kernels to the Herglotz-Nevanlinna class, realizing Gavassino's stability-causality chain for open quantum systems; and (iii) a moment-based Carleman criterion guaranteeing that Pade-reconstructed kernels from Memory Kernel Coupling Theory automatically satisfy KK. We validate the framework on solvable models and construct a counterexample demonstrating that correlated initial states break the Hardy property -- the quantum analogue of Gavassino's mechanism for how acausal macroscopic equations emerge from causal microscopic dynamics.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes rigorous Kramers-Kronig relations for non-Markovian memory kernels in open quantum systems, proving they belong to Hardy spaces under factorized initial conditions, with implications for causality, complete positivity, and reconstruction methods.
AI.motivation: While Kramers-Kronig relations are standard for linear response theory, their validity for non-Markovian memory kernels in open quantum systems has not been rigorously established. The authors aim to provide mathematical foundations for causality and analyticity properties of memory kernels governing reduced quantum dynamics.
AI.method: The authors prove that Nakajima-Zwanzig memory kernels belong to vector-valued Hardy spaces H+p(B) for p>1 under factorized initial states and continuous bath spectral densities. They develop three key theorems: (1) CP-Hardy obstruction theorem linking upper-half-plane poles to non-physical dynamics, (2) passivity-analyticity theorem connecting dissipative kernels to Herglotz-Nevanlinna class, and (3) moment-based Carleman criterion ensuring Padé-reconstructed kernels satisfy KK relations. Validation includes solvable models and construction of counterexamples for correlated initial states.
AI.result: The paper establishes rigorous Kramers-Kronig reconstruction formulas for memory kernels, demonstrates that Hardy space membership yields causality constraints, shows how approximate kernels with upper-half-plane poles lead to non-CPTP dynamics, connects dissipative kernels to mathematical classes ensuring stability-causality chains, and proves that Padé reconstruction from Memory Kernel Coupling Theory automatically satisfies KK relations. Counterexamples show correlated initial states break the Hardy property.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for causality in open quantum systems, establishing that memory kernels satisfy Kramers-Kronig relations under physically reasonable conditions (factorized initial states, continuous spectral densities). The results bridge microscopic causality with macroscopic dynamics, showing how acausal equations can emerge from correlated initial conditions, analogous to Gavassino's mechanism in classical thermodynamics.

title: Gigahertz-rate thin-film lithium niobate receiver for time-bin quantum communication
summary: Time-bin encoded quantum states of light are crucial for quantum technology applications. The integration of manipulation functionalities into chip-scale devices is essential for deploying scalable, high-performance, and cost-effective quantum networks. Here we develop a fully integrated, high-throughput quantum receiver based on the thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) platform, capable of high-speed electro-optic manipulation of time-bin encoded quantum states. The device's novel architecture enables active switching of time-bin quantum states with an electro-optic bandwidth exceeding 30 Ghz, while supporting real-time arbitrary projective measurements with a bandwidth of over 1 GHz. We showcase its versatility and performance through several applications, including the certification of entanglement with Bell's inequality violation by 38 standard deviations and with >95% visibility. We then apply it to a fiber-based quantum communication scenario, where we experimentally demonstrate an entanglement-based quantum key distribution (QKD) protocol, achieving stable finite-size secure key rates exceeding 25 kbit/s over 12 hours of continuous operation. By leveraging a high-speed active switching scheme, the system overcomes the need for temporal post-selection, eliminating a fundamental loophole that compromises the security of time-bin entanglement-based QKD protocols and relaxes the temporal resolution requirements of single-photon detectors. Moreover, it enables active selection of the projection basis, increasing the flexibility for communication parties. This approach establishes a versatile and scalable architecture for time-bin encoded quantum communication, enabling practical protocols on industry-grade photonic technology.

AI.tldr: Researchers developed a fully integrated quantum receiver on thin-film lithium niobate that enables high-speed manipulation of time-bin encoded quantum states, demonstrated through Bell inequality violation and entanglement-based QKD with 25 kbit/s secure key rates.
AI.motivation: Time-bin encoded quantum states are essential for quantum technologies, but scalable deployment requires chip-scale integration of manipulation functionalities for practical quantum networks.
AI.method: Developed a fully integrated quantum receiver on the thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) platform with novel architecture enabling active switching of time-bin quantum states with >30 GHz electro-optic bandwidth and real-time arbitrary projective measurements with >1 GHz bandwidth.
AI.result: Demonstrated Bell's inequality violation by 38 standard deviations with >95% visibility, and achieved stable finite-size secure key rates exceeding 25 kbit/s over 12 hours in entanglement-based QKD, eliminating temporal post-selection loopholes.
AI.conclusion: The TFLN-based quantum receiver establishes a versatile and scalable architecture for time-bin encoded quantum communication, enabling practical protocols on industry-grade photonic technology with enhanced security and flexibility.

title: Quantum many-body scars in random unitary circuits
summary: Quantum many-body scars are rare exceptions to thermalization: they sustain non-thermal stationary states without the protection of any local conservation law, and are generally expected to be fragile. Here we construct an analytically tractable random unitary circuit hosting a single scar, and derive from first principles the thermalization mechanism governing perturbations thereof - described by a picture of fluctuating interfaces. Surprisingly, despite being thermodynamically irrelevant for local observables, the scar leaves a sharp fingerprint in the entanglement dynamics, driving a transition as a function of perturbation strength that is not probed by any local measurement.

AI.tldr: Analytical random unitary circuit with single quantum scar shows scar leaves entanglement fingerprint despite being thermodynamically irrelevant for local observables.
AI.motivation: Quantum many-body scars are rare exceptions to thermalization that lack local conservation law protection and are generally expected to be fragile. Understanding their robustness and thermalization mechanisms under perturbations is crucial.
AI.method: Construct analytically tractable random unitary circuit hosting a single scar, derive thermalization mechanism from first principles using fluctuating interface picture.
AI.result: Scar leaves sharp fingerprint in entanglement dynamics despite being thermodynamically irrelevant for local observables. Perturbation strength drives entanglement transition not probed by any local measurement.
AI.conclusion: Quantum scars can have measurable effects on entanglement dynamics even when invisible to local measurements, revealing hidden structure in thermalization processes.

title: Enabling Lie-Algebraic Classical Simulation beyond Free Fermions
comment: 22 pages main text, 15 pages appendices, 10 figures
summary: Efficient classical simulation has matured to a critical component of the quantum computing stack, driving hardware validation, algorithm design, and the study of structured quantum dynamics. Lie-algebraic simulation ($\mathfrak{g}$-sim) is a compelling approach: it replaces exponentially large Hilbert-space evolution by dynamics in a reduced adjoint space whose dimension is set by the dynamical Lie algebra (DLA) of the circuit, enabling efficient simulation whenever the DLA grows only polynomially with system size. In practice, however, existing applications of $\mathfrak{g}$-sim have been confined to free-fermionic (matchgate) regimes, and it has been unclear how to extend the paradigm to other structured circuits whose generators may have large Pauli expansions. Here we enable Lie-algebraic classical simulation beyond free fermions by identifying additional non-trivial families of polynomial-dimensional DLAs and introducing symmetry-adapted basis representations that make the adjoint space mapping tractable. In particular, we develop an explicit Pauli orbit basis for permutation-equivariant dynamics, supporting cubic-dimensional algebras despite exponential Pauli support, and a subspace-adapted (modified) generalized Gell-Mann basis for bounded Hamming-weight ($U(1)$-equivariant) dynamics, yielding polynomial costs on fixed excitation sectors. Together with streamlined routines for free-fermionic Pauli algebras and translation-invariant variants thereof, these constructions significantly broaden the practical scope of $\mathfrak{g}$-sim as a unifying simulation tool for structured quantum dynamics. Numerical benchmarks confirm favorable preprocessing scaling and validate large-scale proof-of-concept simulations.

AI.tldr: The paper extends Lie-algebraic simulation beyond free-fermionic regimes by identifying new polynomial-dimensional dynamical Lie algebras and developing symmetry-adapted basis representations, enabling efficient classical simulation of structured quantum circuits with large Pauli expansions.
AI.motivation: Existing Lie-algebraic simulation (𝔤-sim) has been limited to free-fermionic (matchgate) regimes, leaving unclear how to extend the paradigm to other structured circuits whose generators may have large Pauli expansions, despite the potential for efficient simulation when dynamical Lie algebras grow polynomially with system size.
AI.method: Developed symmetry-adapted basis representations: 1) explicit Pauli orbit basis for permutation-equivariant dynamics supporting cubic-dimensional algebras despite exponential Pauli support, and 2) subspace-adapted modified generalized Gell-Mann basis for bounded Hamming-weight (U(1)-equivariant) dynamics yielding polynomial costs on fixed excitation sectors. Also streamlined routines for free-fermionic Pauli algebras and translation-invariant variants.
AI.result: Numerical benchmarks confirm favorable preprocessing scaling and validate large-scale proof-of-concept simulations, demonstrating the practical feasibility of extending 𝔤-sim beyond free-fermionic regimes to broader classes of structured quantum dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The developed symmetry-adapted basis representations and identification of new polynomial-dimensional dynamical Lie algebras significantly broaden the practical scope of 𝔤-sim as a unifying simulation tool for structured quantum dynamics, enabling efficient classical simulation of circuits previously inaccessible to the Lie-algebraic approach.

title: Adversarial quantum teleportation
summary: Claims of successful quantum teleportation are backed up by showing that fidelity exceeds some specified threshold, but whether fidelity is the performance metric and what the threshold should be has been a subject of vigorous debate. We construct adversarial models for quantum teleportation, i.e., involving cheating parties, and show that fidelity thresholds can be justified in the context of the type of adversary trying to prove unsuccessful quantum teleportation has been successful. In particular we show how previously established average-fidelity thresholds of 1/2 and 2/3 arise from our adversarial approach. Mathematically, we describe adversarial quantum teleportation as a multi-partite protocol with explicit quantum-logic circuits in both honest and cheating settings, and our methods are relevant beyond quantum teleportation to other quantum-information gadgets.

AI.tldr: Adversarial models justify fidelity thresholds for quantum teleportation by analyzing cheating scenarios, showing how established thresholds of 1/2 and 2/3 arise from different adversary types.
AI.motivation: To resolve debates about appropriate fidelity thresholds for verifying quantum teleportation success by constructing adversarial models that justify specific thresholds based on the type of cheating adversary.
AI.method: Construct adversarial models for quantum teleportation with cheating parties, describe protocols as multi-partite quantum-logic circuits in both honest and cheating settings, and analyze fidelity thresholds through adversarial approach.
AI.result: Shows how previously established average-fidelity thresholds of 1/2 and 2/3 arise naturally from the adversarial approach, justifying these thresholds based on the type of adversary trying to prove unsuccessful teleportation as successful.
AI.conclusion: Fidelity thresholds for quantum teleportation can be rigorously justified through adversarial modeling, with specific thresholds (1/2, 2/3) corresponding to different cheating scenarios, providing a security-based foundation for teleportation verification.

title: Enhance Quantum Teleportation with Multi-Axis Measurement
summary:
Quantum teleportation is a cornerstone of quantum information processing, enabling the nonlocal transmission of quantum states across arbitrary distances using shared entanglement and classical communication. While the standard protocol typically employs Z-basis Bell-state measurements, this fixed-basis approach limits flexibility in practical quantum networks, where dynamic operations, hardware variability, and advanced communications demand alternative measurement bases.
  In this work, we introduce a multi-axis quantum teleportation protocol that generalizes the measurement process by allowing arbitrary basis choices. We provide a formal derivation and self-contained mathematical proof demonstrating that the input quantum state can be faithfully reconstructed under basis-adaptive restoration operations. By establishing a rigorous algorithmic and analytical foundation, this work validates the generalized teleportation protocol and paves the way toward advanced strategies for quantum communication. The demonstrations of the proposed protocol are available at: https://github.com/JJJayyyy/Multi-Axis-Quantum-Teleportation.

AI.tldr: Generalized quantum teleportation protocol allowing arbitrary measurement bases with basis-adaptive restoration operations, enabling flexible quantum network operations.
AI.motivation: Standard quantum teleportation uses fixed Z-basis Bell-state measurements, limiting flexibility for practical quantum networks with dynamic operations, hardware variability, and advanced communication requirements.
AI.method: Multi-axis quantum teleportation protocol that generalizes measurement process by allowing arbitrary basis choices, with formal derivation and self-contained mathematical proof demonstrating faithful state reconstruction using basis-adaptive restoration operations.
AI.result: Established rigorous algorithmic and analytical foundation validating the generalized teleportation protocol, enabling advanced strategies for quantum communication.
AI.conclusion: The work paves the way toward more flexible quantum communication strategies by removing the constraint of fixed measurement bases in teleportation protocols.

title: Complex Quaternionic Formulations of Dirac, Electrodynamic, and Electroweak Fields and Interactions
summary: A simple translation between a standard representation of $\mathfrak{sl}_2\mathbb{C}$ and the complex-quaternions ($\mathbb{H}\otimes_\mathbb{R}\mathbb{C}$) is established and exploited to construct a novel hyper-complex description of the Dirac theory, electrodynamics, and ultimately the electroweak sector of the standard model. We find that coupling the constructed Dirac spinors to electromagnetism yields the correct magnetic moment for charged spin-1/2 particles. Extending electrodynamics to electroweak theory necessitates an algebraic distinction between the structures of the leptonic and Higgs fields not present in the standard model. The conditions of spontaneous symmetry breaking are explored using an alternative representation of weak isospin and hypercharge equivalent to an irreducible representation of $\mathfrak{su}(2)\oplus\mathfrak{u}(1)$ on $\mathbb{C}^4$. This alternative representation disagrees with the standard model on the overall signs of weak neutral currents.

AI.tldr: A novel hyper-complex formulation of Dirac theory, electrodynamics, and electroweak theory using complex-quaternions, yielding correct magnetic moments but differing from standard model in neutral current signs.
AI.motivation: To establish a simpler algebraic framework for fundamental physics using complex-quaternions, potentially offering new insights into the structure of Dirac theory, electrodynamics, and electroweak interactions.
AI.method: Develop translation between sl₂ℂ and complex-quaternions (ℍ⊗ℝℂ), construct hyper-complex description of Dirac theory, extend to electrodynamics and electroweak sector using alternative representation of weak isospin and hypercharge as irreducible representation of su(2)⊕u(1) on ℂ⁴.
AI.result: Successfully reproduces correct magnetic moment for charged spin-1/2 particles when coupling Dirac spinors to electromagnetism. Electroweak extension requires algebraic distinction between leptonic and Higgs fields not present in standard model. Alternative representation yields different overall signs for weak neutral currents compared to standard model.
AI.conclusion: Complex-quaternions provide viable algebraic framework for fundamental physics, reproducing key results but revealing structural differences in electroweak sector and predicting modified neutral current signs that could be experimentally tested.

title: Q-SINDy: Quantum-Kernel Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics with Provable Coefficient Debiasing
summary: Quantum feature maps offer expressive embeddings for classical learning tasks, and augmenting sparse identification of nonlinear dynamics (SINDy) with such features is a natural but unexplored direction. We introduce Q-SINDy, a quantum-kernel-augmented SINDy framework, and identify a specific failure mode that arises: coefficient cannibalization, in which quantum features absorb coefficient mass that rightfully belongs to the polynomial basis, corrupting equation recovery. We derive the exact cannibalization-bias formula Delta xi_P = (P^T P)^{-1} P^T Q xi_Q and prove that orthogonalizing quantum features against the polynomial column space at fit time eliminates this bias exactly. The claim is verified numerically to machine precision (<10^-12) on multiple systems. Empirically, across six canonical dynamical systems (Duffing, Van der Pol, Lorenz, Lotka-Volterra, cubic oscillator, Rossler) and three quantum feature map architectures (ZZ-angle encoding, IQP, data re-uploading), orthogonalized Q-SINDy consistently matches vanilla SINDy's structural recovery while uncorrected augmentation degrades true-positive rates by up to 100%. A refined dynamics-aware diagnostic, R^2_Q for X-dot, predicts cannibalization severity with statistical significance (Pearson r=0.70, p=0.023). An RBF classical-kernel control across 20 hyperparameter configurations fails more severely than any quantum variant, ruling out feature count as the cause. Orthogonalization remains robust under depolarizing hardware noise up to 2% per gate, and the framework extends without modification to Burgers' equation.

AI.tldr: Q-SINDy introduces quantum feature maps to SINDy for nonlinear dynamics identification, but suffers from coefficient cannibalization where quantum features absorb polynomial basis coefficients, corrupting equation recovery. Orthogonalizing quantum features against polynomial basis eliminates this bias exactly.
AI.motivation: Quantum feature maps offer expressive embeddings for classical learning tasks, but their integration with sparse identification of nonlinear dynamics (SINDy) remains unexplored. The authors aim to leverage quantum computing advantages for dynamical systems identification while addressing potential pitfalls.
AI.method: Q-SINDy framework augments SINDy with quantum-kernel features. Identifies coefficient cannibalization failure mode and derives exact bias formula Δξ_P = (P^T P)^{-1} P^T Q ξ_Q. Proposes orthogonalization of quantum features against polynomial column space at fit time to eliminate bias. Validates on six dynamical systems with three quantum feature map architectures.
AI.result: Orthogonalized Q-SINDy matches vanilla SINDy's structural recovery across all tested systems, while uncorrected augmentation degrades true-positive rates by up to 100%. Cannibalization severity predicted by R²_Q for X-dot diagnostic (Pearson r=0.70, p=0.023). Orthogonalization remains robust under 2% per gate depolarizing noise and extends to Burgers' equation. RBF classical-kernel control fails more severely than quantum variants.
AI.conclusion: Quantum feature augmentation of SINDy requires careful handling of coefficient cannibalization. Orthogonalization provides exact bias elimination, enabling quantum-enhanced dynamical systems identification without corrupting polynomial basis recovery. The framework demonstrates quantum advantage over classical kernels and robustness to hardware noise.

title: Learning Non-Markovian Noise via Ensemble Optimal Control
summary:
We study the estimation of parameters pertaining to non-Markovian quantum open systems, such as the dissipation rate and environmental memory time. A key challenge is identifying the optimal measurement time, which must allow sufficient time to acquire information about the environment, yet be short enough to avoid dissipation that erases the information.
  Using machine learning approaches, we develop an optimized control scheme trained over a representative ensemble to fix the optimal measurement time at a prescribed runtime. The protocol is robust to errors in the training process, enhances precision by exploiting non-Markovian memory effects, and achieves measurement uncertainties approaching the quantum limits set by the Cramér-Rao bound.

AI.tldr: Machine learning optimized control protocol for estimating non-Markovian quantum system parameters with optimal measurement timing approaching quantum limits.
AI.motivation: The challenge in estimating parameters of non-Markovian quantum open systems (dissipation rate, environmental memory time) lies in identifying optimal measurement time - long enough to acquire environmental information but short enough to avoid dissipation erasing that information.
AI.method: Developed machine learning optimized control scheme trained over representative ensemble to determine optimal measurement time at prescribed runtime. The protocol is designed to be robust to training errors.
AI.result: Protocol enhances precision by exploiting non-Markovian memory effects and achieves measurement uncertainties approaching quantum limits set by the Cramér-Rao bound.
AI.conclusion: Machine learning approach successfully addresses the optimal measurement timing challenge in non-Markovian quantum parameter estimation, achieving near-quantum-limit precision while maintaining robustness.

title: Engineering magnetically insensitive qubits in metastable electronic D-states of trapped ions
summary: Ion trap quantum computers often store qubits on field-sensitive S_1/2 ground state Zeeman levels of the valence electron, such as in 40Ca+, 88Sr+, and 138Ba+ atomic systems. We experimentally synthesize magnetically insensitive qubit states in multiple metastable electronic D_3/2 Zeeman levels in such an atomic system. We demonstrate coherent operations within the D_3/2 manifold of 138Ba+, including coherent flopping between the synthesized qubit states, and our results agree with theory. Such an encoding may allow for more flexible use of atomic levels for photonic interfaces, and with a measured improvement in the qubit coherence time T2* by a factor of 3, this lays the foundation for further improvement for quantum computing and network applications.

AI.tldr: Experimental synthesis of magnetically insensitive qubit states in D₃/₂ manifold of ¹³⁸Ba⁺ ion trap quantum computer, achieving 3× improvement in coherence time T₂*
AI.motivation: Ion trap quantum computers typically use field-sensitive S₁/₂ ground state Zeeman levels for qubit storage, which are susceptible to magnetic field noise. The motivation is to create magnetically insensitive qubit states to improve coherence times and enable more flexible use of atomic levels for photonic interfaces in quantum computing and networking applications.
AI.method: Experimental synthesis of magnetically insensitive qubit states in multiple metastable electronic D₃/₂ Zeeman levels in ¹³⁸Ba⁺ atomic system.
