The method involves coherent operations within the D₃/₂ manifold, including coherent flopping between the synthesized qubit states, with experimental results validated against theoretical predictions.
AI.result: Successful demonstration of coherent operations within the D₃/₂ manifold of ¹³⁸Ba⁺, including coherent flopping between synthesized qubit states. Measured improvement in qubit coherence time T₂* by a factor of 3 compared to conventional S₁/₂ ground state encoding. Experimental results agree with theoretical predictions.
AI.conclusion: The magnetically insensitive qubit encoding in D₃/₂ manifold provides significant coherence time improvement (3× T₂*) and enables more flexible use of atomic levels for photonic interfaces. This work lays foundation for enhanced quantum computing and network applications by reducing susceptibility to magnetic field noise.

title: Entanglement Dynamics with a Stochastic Non-Hermitian Hamiltonian away from Exceptional Points
summary: Although non-Hermitian dynamics near exceptional points (EPs) provide a route to accelerated entanglement generation, entanglement can also be generated far from EPs at comparable or even higher rates. However, the behavior of such entanglement in open systems remains largely unexplored, rendering it highly susceptible to environmental noise. Here, we study entanglement dynamics in two coupled qubits described by a non-Hermitian Hamiltonian, where the dissipative rates are subject to stochastic noise. We focus on the regime away from EPs. Our approach demonstrates that, even in the presence of classical noise, rich dynamical control can be achieved, enabling highly efficient entanglement generation with a timescale that is significantly shorter than that of both Hermitian systems and EP-based non-Hermitian protocols. Additionally, the timescale is independent of the number of qubits, highlighting favorable scalability for multipartite entanglement generation and facilitating integration into future photonic quantum processors.

AI.tldr: Non-Hermitian dynamics away from exceptional points can generate entanglement faster than both Hermitian systems and EP-based protocols, with noise-resistant scalability independent of qubit count.
AI.motivation: While non-Hermitian dynamics near exceptional points (EPs) can accelerate entanglement generation, entanglement can also be generated far from EPs at comparable or higher rates. However, the behavior of such entanglement in open systems with environmental noise remains largely unexplored.
AI.method: Study entanglement dynamics in two coupled qubits described by a non-Hermitian Hamiltonian where dissipative rates are subject to stochastic noise, focusing on the regime away from exceptional points.
AI.result: Even with classical noise, rich dynamical control enables highly efficient entanglement generation with timescales significantly shorter than both Hermitian systems and EP-based non-Hermitian protocols. The timescale is independent of the number of qubits, showing favorable scalability.
AI.conclusion: Non-Hermitian dynamics away from exceptional points offer noise-resistant, scalable entanglement generation with faster timescales than existing approaches, facilitating integration into future photonic quantum processors.

title: Scalable Quantum Error Mitigation with Physically Informed Graph Neural Networks
summary: Quantum error mitigation (QEM) provides a practical route for estimating reliable observables on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices. Traditional QEM strategies, including zero-noise extrapolation (ZNE) and Clifford data regression (CDR), rely on noise scaling or global regression, and their performance is constrained by the exponential growth of the system degrees of freedom. We construct a graph-enhanced mitigation (GEM) framework, which incorporates physical information into the model representation. In this work, quantum circuits are encoded as attributed graphs. Hardware-level physical information is mapped to node and edge features: local noise parameters such as calibration parameters $T_1$, $T_2$, and readout errors are encoded at nodes, while coupling-related information such as two-qubit gate errors is encoded as edge features. Graph neural networks are used to model how errors propagate along the physical coupling structure and build up into non-local correlations. This allows the model to capture local interactions and part of the resulting non-local correlations across qubits. A dual-branch affine correction is applied to maintain consistency with physical constraints. Experiments on 10-qubit and 16-qubit random circuits executed on superconducting quantum processors show that GEM provides a level of accuracy comparable to CDR at small scales, while yielding lower mean absolute error and improved stability in zero-shot transfer to larger systems. Results of the traditional QEM strategy indicate that global regression methods remain effective in low-dimensional settings but become less reliable as system degrees of freedom grow. In contrast, GEM makes use of local physical structures to show better scalability and generalization, while preserving the overall error propagation patterns. This work provides a practical scalable approach to QEM for NISQ devices.

AI.tldr: Graph-enhanced mitigation (GEM) framework uses graph neural networks with hardware-level physical information to model error propagation in quantum circuits, showing better scalability than traditional QEM methods like CDR and ZNE.
AI.motivation: Traditional QEM strategies (ZNE, CDR) suffer from exponential growth constraints with system degrees of freedom and rely on noise scaling or global regression, limiting their scalability for NISQ devices.
AI.method: Encode quantum circuits as attributed graphs with hardware-level physical information mapped to node features (T1, T2, readout errors) and edge features (two-qubit gate errors). Use graph neural networks to model error propagation along physical coupling structure, with dual-branch affine correction for physical consistency.
AI.result: GEM achieves accuracy comparable to CDR at small scales (10-16 qubit random circuits on superconducting processors) with lower mean absolute error and improved stability in zero-shot transfer to larger systems. Shows better scalability and generalization than global regression methods.
AI.conclusion: GEM provides a practical scalable approach to QEM for NISQ devices by leveraging local physical structures to capture error propagation patterns while maintaining better scalability than traditional methods.

title: Universal Quartic Scaling Law for Kerr-Type Interactions: Projection-Law Factorization Across Nonlinear Quantum Platforms
summary: We present a rigorous derivation and numerical validation of a universal projection-law factorization for quartic nonlinear coupling rates across physically distinct platforms. The central result is that observable Kerr-type interactions -- self-Kerr, cross-Kerr, and cross-phase modulation -- factorize into a dimensionless projection coefficient and an intrinsic quartic energy scale. This structure follows from canonical quantization of a quartic interaction projected onto a finite normal-mode basis. We state this result as a formal proposition with clearly enumerated assumptions, provide a proof sketch based on second quantization of the anharmonic potential, and enumerate the domain of validity. We then implement the scaling law as a lightweight computational toolkit (UEFT-Designer) with platform-specific kernels for superconducting circuits, photonic microcavities, and epsilon-near-zero (ENZ) structures. A complete, step-by-step worked example for a superconducting quarton device -- with full uncertainty propagation -- predicts a cross-Kerr rate $χ/2π= 361\pm 13\,\mathrm{MHz}$, agreeing with the independently measured value of $366\pm 0.5\,\mathrm{MHz}$ \cite{Ye2025} to within 1.4\%. A formal error-propagation analysis establishes that the dominant uncertainty is the Josephson-energy extraction uncertainty ($\sim\!2\%$), not the geometric projection factor. Cross-platform validation against five independent experiments spanning eight orders of magnitude in coupling strength confirms the universality of the factorization to within reported experimental uncertainties. The ghost-sector spectral correction introduced in earlier versions is reframed as an optional phenomenological self-energy model with no mandatory role in the validated scaling law.

AI.tldr: A universal factorization law for quartic nonlinear coupling rates (Kerr-type interactions) is derived and validated, showing they factor into a dimensionless projection coefficient and intrinsic quartic energy scale, with cross-platform validation across eight orders of magnitude.
AI.motivation: To establish a universal scaling law for Kerr-type nonlinear interactions across different physical platforms (superconducting circuits, photonic microcavities, ENZ structures) that enables accurate prediction of coupling rates without full numerical simulations.
AI.method: Canonical quantization of quartic interaction projected onto finite normal-mode basis, formal proposition with proof sketch, implementation as computational toolkit (UEFT-Designer) with platform-specific kernels, and comprehensive validation against experimental data.
AI.result: The factorization law accurately predicts cross-Kerr rates (within 1.4% for superconducting quarton device), with validation across five independent experiments spanning eight orders of magnitude in coupling strength, establishing universality within experimental uncertainties.
AI.conclusion: Kerr-type interactions universally factorize into projection coefficient and intrinsic energy scale, enabling accurate predictions across platforms; dominant uncertainty is parameter extraction (e.g., Josephson energy) rather than geometric projection factors.

title: Simultaneous cooling of degenerate mechanical modes in unresolved sideband regime via optical and mechanical nonlinearities
summary: We propose a scheme to simultaneously cool multiple degenerate mechanical modes in optomechanical systems beyond the resolved sideband regime. In general, one of the main obstacles for cooling degenerate mechanical modes is the so-called dark-mode effect. The Duffing nonlinearities (mechanical nonlinearities) can be used to overcome the dark-mode effect of degenerate mechanical modes. A second-order nonlinear medium (optical nonlinearity) is introduced to accomplish the ground-state cooling of degenerate mechanical modes beyond the resolved sideband regime. We find the dark mode of degenerate mechanical modes can be broken when the mechanical nonlinearities of different mechanical modes are not very close. Our scheme paves the way toward the implementation of simultaneous ground-state cooling of degenerate mechanical modes of optomechanical systems beyond the resolved sideband regime in experiments.

AI.tldr: Simultaneous cooling of multiple degenerate mechanical modes in optomechanical systems beyond resolved sideband regime by breaking dark-mode effect using mechanical and optical nonlinearities.
AI.motivation: The dark-mode effect prevents simultaneous cooling of degenerate mechanical modes in optomechanical systems, especially beyond the resolved sideband regime where conventional cooling methods fail.
AI.method: Utilizes Duffing nonlinearities (mechanical nonlinearities) to overcome dark-mode effect and introduces a second-order nonlinear medium (optical nonlinearity) to enable ground-state cooling beyond resolved sideband regime.
AI.result: Dark mode of degenerate mechanical modes can be broken when mechanical nonlinearities of different modes are sufficiently distinct, enabling simultaneous ground-state cooling.
AI.conclusion: The scheme enables experimental implementation of simultaneous ground-state cooling for degenerate mechanical modes in optomechanical systems beyond resolved sideband regime.

title: Nonnormality and Dissipation in Markovian Quantum Dynamics: Implications for Quantum Simulation
summary: Understanding the structure and stability of open quantum dynamics is increasingly important for both fundamental studies of nonequilibrium quantum systems and the development of quantum simulation algorithms. In this work, we introduce a structural framework for Markovian open quantum systems that characterizes Lindbladian generators in terms of two scalar quantities: the dissipative strength and the nonnormality. We show that normal generators admit an exact decoupling between dissipative and norm-preserving dynamics, leading to purely exponential behavior governed by the dissipative scale. In contrast, nonnormality is an intrinsically dissipative feature: it vanishes in the absence of dissipation but is not implied by it. Moreover, it is structurally constrained by the interplay between the Hermitian and anti-Hermitian components of the generator. For generic Markovian open quantum systems, we identify parametric regimes controlled by a dimensionless ratio between nonnormality and dissipative strength, governing the onset of transient amplification. These structural features have direct implications for quantum simulation. While Hamiltonian and normal dissipative dynamics exhibit stable evolution with standard scaling behavior, nonnormal generators can induce transient growth that amplifies numerical errors and increases simulation cost. Our results provide a unified generator-level perspective on irreversibility, stability, and quantum simulation of open quantum systems.

AI.tldr: A structural framework for Markovian open quantum systems characterizes Lindbladian generators using dissipative strength and nonnormality, revealing how nonnormality causes transient amplification and affects quantum simulation stability.
AI.motivation: Understanding open quantum dynamics is crucial for fundamental nonequilibrium studies and quantum simulation development. Current approaches lack a unified structural perspective on generator properties and their implications for stability and simulation.
AI.method: Introduces a structural framework analyzing Lindbladian generators through two scalar quantities: dissipative strength and nonnormality. Examines normal vs. nonnormal generators, their decoupling properties, and parametric regimes governed by the ratio between nonnormality and dissipative strength.
AI.result: Normal generators exhibit exact decoupling between dissipative and norm-preserving dynamics with purely exponential behavior. Nonnormality is an intrinsically dissipative feature that vanishes without dissipation but is not implied by it. Nonnormal generators can induce transient amplification that amplifies numerical errors and increases simulation costs.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a unified generator-level perspective connecting irreversibility, stability, and quantum simulation of open quantum systems, highlighting how nonnormality fundamentally affects dynamics and simulation requirements.

title: PAPUS: Pauli-Space-Based Multiclass Quantum Classification
summary: Quantum classification faces two key challenges. First, the difficulty of distinguishing between different classes varies: some class pairs are easy to separate, while others are more challenging. Second, practical execution is affected by noise, finite sampling, and measurement overhead. To address these issues, we propose PAPUS, a framework for pair-adaptive quantum classification in Pauli space. The method evaluates candidate upload circuits using low-weight Pauli features and formulates upload design as a structured model selection problem based on discriminative representations. By dynamically adjusting circuit complexity according to class-pair difficulty, the framework achieves a better balance between classification accuracy and resource efficiency. Experiments on 9 data sets with 474 tasks show that PAPUS achieves a favorable balance between predictive performance and execution cost. Specifically, PAPUS attains classification accuracies above 90% in both local noiseless simulation and the IonQ noisy simulator, while requiring substantially lower measurement and circuit cost (fewer total measurement shots and fewer quantum gates for data upload). Compared with the two conventional baselines, template_cv and kta_exact, PAPUS also shows much stronger robustness under noise: accuracy decreases by only 1.67% in the noisy setting, whereas both baselines degrade by 9.44%.

AI.tldr: PAPUS is a pair-adaptive quantum classification framework that dynamically adjusts circuit complexity based on class-pair difficulty, achieving better accuracy-resource balance with strong noise robustness.
AI.motivation: Quantum classification faces two key challenges: varying difficulty in distinguishing different class pairs, and practical limitations from noise, finite sampling, and measurement overhead. Existing methods don't adaptively address these issues.
AI.method: PAPUS evaluates candidate upload circuits using low-weight Pauli features and formulates upload design as a structured model selection problem based on discriminative representations. It dynamically adjusts circuit complexity according to class-pair difficulty.
AI.result: Experiments on 9 datasets with 474 tasks show PAPUS achieves >90% accuracy in both local noiseless simulation and IonQ noisy simulator, with substantially lower measurement and circuit costs. PAPUS shows much stronger noise robustness (only 1.67% accuracy decrease) compared to baselines (9.44% degradation).
AI.conclusion: PAPUS provides an effective framework for pair-adaptive quantum classification that balances predictive performance with execution cost, demonstrating superior noise robustness and resource efficiency compared to conventional approaches.

title: Anomalous nonlocality of information masked in quantum correlations
summary: Although information, strictly speaking, is not a physical entity, it generally requires physical entities as its carriers, e.g., writing it down on paper, encoding it with quantum particles, or transmitting it using electro-magnetic fields. And it seems natural that these carriers cannot travel faster than light. Here we reveal that if we use quantum correlations as the carrier of information (either quantum or classical), then it can display a kind of nonlocality, which bears both similarities to and distinctions from the nonlocality of physical particles. Notably, though superluminal signaling is still not allowed so that the special relativity is not violated, it is possible to select at our will whether to decode the information at one location, or to dispatch it to another location far away (i.e., to give up the chance of decoding the information and let it be decodable in somewhere else only) without needing the assistance of classical information, so that it occurs instantaneously without being limited by the speed of light. This phenomenon differs sharply from the nonlocality of physical particles that we once knew, where whether a particle can be detected in one location or another is governed by quantum uncertainty, which cannot be chosen freely.

AI.tldr: Quantum correlations can serve as information carriers enabling instantaneous, choice-dependent information dispatch between distant locations without superluminal signaling, revealing a new type of nonlocality distinct from particle nonlocality.
AI.motivation: To explore how quantum correlations as information carriers can exhibit novel nonlocal properties that differ from the nonlocality of physical particles, while maintaining compatibility with special relativity by avoiding superluminal signaling.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of using quantum correlations as carriers for information transmission, examining how information can be selectively decoded at one location or dispatched to another distant location instantaneously without classical assistance.
AI.result: Reveals a new form of nonlocality where information can be either decoded locally or dispatched to a remote location at will, occurring instantaneously without violating special relativity, as no superluminal signaling occurs.
AI.conclusion: Quantum correlations as information carriers exhibit a unique type of nonlocality that allows controlled, instantaneous information dispatch between locations, fundamentally different from particle nonlocality governed by quantum uncertainty.

title: Hybrid Quantum Neural Networks for Enhanced Breast Cancer Thermographic Classification: A Novel Quantum-Classical Integration Approach
comment: Published in: 2025 IEEE International Biomedical Instrumentation and Technology Conference (IBITeC)
summary: Breast cancer diagnosis through thermographic image analysis remains a critical challenge in medical AI, with classical deep learning approaches facing limitations in complex thermal pattern classification tasks. This paper presents a novel Hybrid Quantum Neural Network (HQNN) architecture that integrates quantum computing principles with classical convolutional neural networks for enhanced breast cancer classification. Our approach employs parameterized quantum circuits with multi-head attention mechanisms for quantum-aware feature encoding, coupled with classical convolutional layers for comprehensive pattern recognition. The quantum component utilizes a 4qubit variational circuit with strongly entangling layers, while the classical component incorporates advanced attention mechanisms for feature fusion. Experimental validation on breast cancer thermographic data demonstrates substantial performance improvements over state-of-the-art classical architectures, with the quantum-enhanced approach exhibiting superior convergence dynamics and enhanced feature representation capabilities. Our findings provide evidence for quantum advantage in medical image classification through classical simulation, establishing a framework for quantum-classical hybrid systems in healthcare applications. The methodology addresses key challenges in quantum machine learning deployment while maintaining computational feasibility on near-term quantum devices.

AI.tldr: Hybrid Quantum Neural Network (HQNN) combining quantum circuits with classical CNNs achieves superior breast cancer classification from thermographic images compared to classical deep learning approaches.
AI.motivation: Classical deep learning approaches face limitations in complex thermal pattern classification for breast cancer diagnosis from thermographic images, necessitating more advanced architectures that can capture intricate patterns.
AI.method: Proposes a Hybrid Quantum Neural Network (HQNN) architecture integrating parameterized quantum circuits with multi-head attention mechanisms for quantum-aware feature encoding, coupled with classical convolutional layers. Uses 4-qubit variational circuits with strongly entangling layers and advanced attention mechanisms for feature fusion.
AI.result: Experimental validation on breast cancer thermographic data demonstrates substantial performance improvements over state-of-the-art classical architectures, with superior convergence dynamics and enhanced feature representation capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Provides evidence for quantum advantage in medical image classification through classical simulation, establishing a framework for quantum-classical hybrid systems in healthcare applications while maintaining computational feasibility on near-term quantum devices.

title: Fundamental Limits of Eavesdropper Detection and Localization in Optical Fiber via Stimulated Brillouin Scattering
summary: Recent work investigated the use of Stimulated Brillouin Scattering (SBS) to measure changes in fiber parameters, thereby enhancing the security of a Quantum Key Distribution(QKD) system. In this work, we cast the problem into a binary hypothesis testing scenario, with the goal of comparing the state of the art with potential future quantum technology-based detection methods. We derive an effective input-output model for the Stimulated Brillouin Scattering (SBS) interaction, and utilize it to compare three detection methods: First, the established state of the art. Second, a photon-counting based method which will likely be available in the near future. Finally, we compare to the ultimate quantum limit, which is given by the quantum error exponent of asymmetric hypothesis testing.

AI.tldr: Comparison of three detection methods for SBS-based fiber parameter monitoring in QKD security: current state-of-the-art, near-future photon-counting, and ultimate quantum limit via quantum error exponent analysis.
AI.motivation: To enhance Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) system security by comparing different detection methods for Stimulated Brillouin Scattering (SBS) fiber parameter monitoring, assessing current capabilities against potential future quantum technologies.
AI.method: Formulated as binary hypothesis testing problem; derived effective input-output model for SBS interaction; compared three detection approaches: 1) established state-of-the-art method, 2) photon-counting based method (near-future technology), 3) ultimate quantum limit using quantum error exponent of asymmetric hypothesis testing.
AI.result: The paper provides comparative analysis of detection methods, establishing performance benchmarks and quantifying the gap between current practical implementations and theoretical quantum limits for SBS-based fiber monitoring in QKD security applications.
AI.conclusion: The study enables systematic evaluation of detection method improvements for SBS-based QKD security, identifying performance gaps between current technology, near-future photon-counting methods, and ultimate quantum theoretical limits.

title: Heteronuclear Polarization Transfers Between Spin-locked and Anti-Longitudinal Spin States in the NMR of Liquids and Spinning Solids
summary: Recently, Pang et al reported a novel polarization transfer scheme applicable to three-spin systems, whereby a rotating-frame NMR analogue of the cross effect could transfer polarization between; e.g., two 13Cs and an 15N in a single crystal. The present work furthers this scheme to the case of powder NMR under magic angle spinning (MAS) conditions, as well as to solution NMR. It is found that in all such cases a second-order average Hamiltonian can transfer polarization between non-equivalent, coupled abundant spins (e.g., two 1Hs) prepared in anti-longitudinal magnetization states, and the spin-locked magnetization of a rare spins (e.g., one 13C). The average Hamiltonian for such three-spin (S1-S2) to I transfer was derived for both liquids and solids, and found in good quantitative agreement with numerical simulations and experiments. At an optimal transfer condition whereby an I-spin RF irradiation field matches the S1-S2 chemical-shift-difference, a maximum polarization enhancement equal to the ratio of gyromagnetic ratios is achieved; as explained and demonstrated in the study, ca. half of this can be effectively obtained for I = 13C in powdered solids and in multi-spin systems in solutions. All such processes display an oscillatory nature, meaning that the transverse spin-locked polarization of a rare spin can become anti-longitudinal magnetization of abundant spins -without ever pulsing on the latter. The roles played by many-body interactions, RF inhomogeneities, and interferences of other coherences during the execution of these novel forms of cross-polarization were investigated, and are exemplified with experiments and simulations.

AI.tldr: Extension of a three-spin polarization transfer scheme from single crystals to powder MAS and solution NMR, enabling polarization transfer between abundant spins and rare spins via second-order average Hamiltonian effects.
AI.motivation: To generalize a recently reported three-spin polarization transfer scheme (rotating-frame NMR analogue of cross effect) from single crystal conditions to more practical powder magic angle spinning (MAS) and solution NMR environments.
AI.method: Derived average Hamiltonian for three-spin (S1-S2 to I) polarization transfer in both liquids and solids, validated with numerical simulations and experiments. Investigated optimal transfer conditions where I-spin RF irradiation matches S1-S2 chemical shift difference.
AI.result: Maximum polarization enhancement equal to gyromagnetic ratio ratio achieved at optimal conditions; approximately half of this enhancement effectively obtained for 13C in powdered solids and multi-spin solution systems. Transfer processes display oscillatory nature enabling conversion between transverse spin-locked rare spin polarization and anti-longitudinal abundant spin magnetization.
AI.conclusion: The study successfully extends three-spin polarization transfer to practical NMR conditions, demonstrating robust transfer mechanisms that operate without pulsing on abundant spins, with applications in both solid-state and solution NMR spectroscopy.

title: Time evolution of quantum gates and the necessity of complex numbers
summary: As physical systems, qubits must evolve from input to output state. We describe a simple scheme in which the effect of a quantum gate is described by the action of an effective Hamiltonian acting for some characteristic time. This model shows that the action of common unary gates is to induce Bloch sphere trajectories along lines of latitude relative to an eigenvector of the gate. Such trajectories would immediately move a `rebit', initially confined to a line of latitude, off this line and acquire a complex phase. The role of the complex phase in bringing about the entanglement of two qubits is also highlighted. It is then asked whether such dynamics could be modelled using real QM. It is shown that the continuous evolution required for such dynamics can only be provided by members of the special orthogonal group of the vector space. Since the matrices representing many quantum gates of interest have determinant -1, no real special orthogonal operators can model their evolution if the dimension of the real vector space is the same as that of the complex space. Next we look at the mapping from a complex vector space of dimension $N$ to a real space of dimension $2N$ that is often used to construct `real' QM. It is shown that this is just an isomorphic mapping from the scalar representation of complex numbers to their $2\times2$ matrix equivalents, so that the resulting matrices are actually represent complex matrices. Finally, we investigate the endomorphism of real vector spaces of dimension $N = 2^{n}$, where $n \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$, suitable for the modelling of $n-1$ qubits. We confirm that the mapping from $\mathbb{C}^{2^{n-1}} \to \mathbb{R}^{2^{n}}$ only maps elements of $\mathrm{End}(\mathbb{C}^{2^{n-1}})$ to a restricted subspace of $\mathrm{End}(\mathbb{R}^{2^{n}})$ that reproduces the `real' representation of complex matrices.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes whether quantum gate dynamics can be modeled using real quantum mechanics, showing that real representations of complex quantum mechanics are actually isomorphic to complex matrices and cannot properly model quantum gates with determinant -1.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether quantum gate evolution and entanglement dynamics can be faithfully modeled using real quantum mechanics rather than complex quantum mechanics, examining the limitations of real representations of quantum systems.
AI.method: The authors analyze quantum gate dynamics through effective Hamiltonian evolution, examine Bloch sphere trajectories for unary gates, investigate real special orthogonal group representations, and study the mapping from complex vector spaces to real spaces of doubled dimension.
AI.result: The paper shows that: 1) Quantum gates with determinant -1 cannot be modeled by real special orthogonal operators in same-dimensional spaces; 2) The standard mapping from ℂ^N to ℝ^{2N} is isomorphic to representing complex numbers as 2×2 matrices; 3) The resulting "real" quantum mechanics actually reproduces complex matrix representations.
AI.conclusion: Real quantum mechanics cannot faithfully model quantum gate dynamics because the required continuous evolution can only be provided by special orthogonal group members, and the standard real representations are actually isomorphic to complex representations, failing to provide a truly real alternative to complex quantum mechanics.

title: Thermodynamics of Coherence-Selective Quantum Reset Protocols
summary: We develop an exact theory of coherence-selective stroboscopic resetting for quadratic open quantum systems within the single-particle density-matrix formalism. We focus on the survival of coherences and the associated thermodynamic cost at the stroboscopic fixed point. To this end, we introduce a one-parameter family of reset channels that continuously interpolates between complete coherence erasure and complete coherence preservation. This unifies the reset-map description, the repeated-interaction and evolving-correlation endpoint channels, and the thermodynamic cost of environmental reinitialization. For a single fermionic level coupled to a structured semi-infinite tight-binding bath, we derive the exact affine stroboscopic map, solve for its unique fixed point, and compute the retained coherence spectrum, the post-reset occupation, and the reset heat current. We find that retained coherence increases monotonically with the retention parameter, whereas the reset heat current is generically nonmonotonic and is maximized at an intermediate operating point. Thus the protocol that stores the most coherence is not the one that dissipates the most heat. Exact operating diagrams further show that coherence-optimal and coherence-per-cost-optimal protocols are both driven toward the coherence-preserving endpoint, while the heat-optimal protocol depends strongly on the reset interval. We also show that this coherence-cost geometry survives at nonzero chemical potential as a filling-biased deformation of the same fixed-point tradeoff, rather than as an independent particle-current optimization problem. These results establish coherence-selective resetting as a distinct control principle for structured-bath open quantum systems and provide an exactly solvable benchmark for memory engineering and thermodynamic optimization under repeated environmental reinitialization.

AI.tldr: Exact theory of coherence-selective stroboscopic resetting for quadratic open quantum systems shows nonmonotonic relationship between retained coherence and thermodynamic cost, with optimal protocols not maximizing heat dissipation.
AI.motivation: To understand the survival of coherences and associated thermodynamic costs in open quantum systems under stroboscopic resetting, and to establish coherence-selective resetting as a control principle for structured-bath systems.
AI.method: Develop exact theory within single-particle density-matrix formalism for quadratic open quantum systems. Introduce one-parameter family of reset channels interpolating between complete coherence erasure and preservation. Derive exact affine stroboscopic map for single fermionic level coupled to structured semi-infinite tight-binding bath, solve for unique fixed point, and compute coherence spectrum, occupation, and reset heat current.
AI.result: Retained coherence increases monotonically with retention parameter, but reset heat current is nonmonotonic and maximized at intermediate operating point. Coherence-optimal and coherence-per-cost-optimal protocols both driven toward coherence-preserving endpoint, while heat-optimal protocol depends strongly on reset interval. Coherence-cost geometry survives at nonzero chemical potential as filling-biased deformation.
AI.conclusion: Coherence-selective resetting establishes a distinct control principle for structured-bath open quantum systems, providing an exactly solvable benchmark for memory engineering and thermodynamic optimization under repeated environmental reinitialization.

title: Dynamic locking of an interacting spin system via periodic driving
summary: Periodic driving plays a central role in quantum control, but its application in interacting spin systems is often restricted to near-resonant conditions, where standard averaging techniques remain valid. Here we investigate how detuning from resonance can be used to dynamically spin-lock a dipolar-coupled ensemble. We show that the combination of offset and pulse structure generates an effective Rabi field with sharply structured amplitude and tilt. This behavior - supported by a semi-analytical framework, numerical simulations and experiment - enables new approaches to many-body system control, here exemplified via offset-induced reversible interconversion of Zeeman and dipolar order, and heterospin polarization transfer away from rf-field matching conditions. Further, we leverage artificial-intelligence-assisted sequence design to explore regimes with long control cycles - where average Hamiltonian theory breaks down, but effective locking persists - opening pathways to rich offset-dependent responses. These findings position offset-enabled dynamic locking as a promising tool for quantum sensing, energy transfer, and spin-order manipulation beyond traditional approaches.

AI.tldr: Periodic driving with detuning enables dynamic spin-locking in dipolar-coupled ensembles, creating structured effective fields for novel many-body control, demonstrated via order interconversion and polarization transfer beyond traditional resonance conditions.
AI.motivation: Traditional quantum control in interacting spin systems is limited to near-resonant conditions where averaging techniques work. The paper investigates how detuning from resonance can be exploited for dynamic spin-locking in dipolar-coupled ensembles, enabling control beyond standard approaches.
AI.method: Combination of offset and pulse structure generates effective Rabi fields with structured amplitude and tilt. The approach uses semi-analytical framework, numerical simulations, and experimental validation. AI-assisted sequence design explores regimes with long control cycles where average Hamiltonian theory breaks down but effective locking persists.
AI.result: Demonstrates offset-induced reversible interconversion of Zeeman and dipolar order, and heterospin polarization transfer away from rf-field matching conditions. Shows effective locking persists in regimes where traditional averaging techniques fail, enabling rich offset-dependent responses.
AI.conclusion: Offset-enabled dynamic locking provides a promising tool for quantum sensing, energy transfer, and spin-order manipulation beyond traditional approaches, opening pathways to new control strategies in many-body quantum systems.

title: Entanglement and Quantum Coherence in Coupled Double Quantum Dots under Markovian and Non-Markovian Noisy Channels
summary: Quantum dots are nanometer-scale semiconductor particles that exhibit size-dependent quantum mechanical properties. In this work, we investigate the dynamics of quantum correlations, quantified by the concurrence and the quantum coherence, in a bipartite system of coupled double quantum dots. The analysis is carried out within both Markovian and non-Markovian regimes, and further extended to different noisy quantum channels, including amplitude damping, phase flip, and phase damping. Our results show that environmental memory plays a crucial role in the preservation of quantum correlations, leading to oscillatory behavior and partial revivals in the non-Markovian regime, in contrast to the monotonic decay observed under Markovian dynamics. Moreover, distinct decoherence mechanisms induce qualitatively different effects: dissipative channels rapidly suppress correlations, while phase-based channels lead to either redistribution or gradual degradation. A key finding is that quantum coherence exhibits a higher robustness compared to entanglement under all considered conditions, highlighting its relevance as a reliable quantum resource in noisy environments. These results provide valuable insights into the control and protection of quantum correlations in realistic solid-state systems.

AI.tldr: Quantum correlation dynamics in coupled double quantum dots show non-Markovian memory effects preserve correlations via oscillations/revivals, with quantum coherence more robust than entanglement across various noise channels.
AI.motivation: To investigate how quantum correlations (entanglement and coherence) evolve in realistic solid-state quantum dot systems under different environmental conditions, comparing Markovian vs. non-Markovian regimes and various noise channels.
AI.method: Analysis of bipartite coupled double quantum dots using concurrence (for entanglement) and quantum coherence measures, studied within both Markovian and non-Markovian regimes, and extended to amplitude damping, phase flip, and phase damping noisy channels.
AI.result: Environmental memory in non-Markovian regime causes oscillatory behavior and partial revivals of quantum correlations, contrasting with monotonic decay in Markovian dynamics. Dissipative channels rapidly suppress correlations while phase-based channels cause redistribution or gradual degradation. Quantum coherence shows higher robustness than entanglement across all conditions.
AI.conclusion: Quantum coherence is a more reliable quantum resource than entanglement in noisy environments, with environmental memory playing crucial role in preserving quantum correlations. These insights are valuable for controlling and protecting quantum correlations in realistic solid-state quantum systems.

title: Map-Dependent Quantum Characteristic Functions and CP-Divisibility in Non-Markovian Quantum Dynamics
summary: We introduce map-dependent quantum characteristic functions constructed from the normalized Choi operator of quantum dynamical maps. We prove a Bochner--Choi positivity theorem establishing that the positive-type condition of the associated Gram matrix is equivalent to complete positivity of the underlying quantum channel. Applying the construction to intermediate dynamical maps, we obtain a characterization of CP-divisibility in terms of positivity of two-time characteristic functions. Numerical examples for amplitude damping and pure dephasing models demonstrate that negativity of the Gram matrix coincides with the breakdown of CP-divisibility and the emergence of information backflow. The proposed framework provides a new bridge between characteristic-function methods in quantum statistics and structural properties of quantum dynamical maps.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces map-dependent quantum characteristic functions from Choi operators, proves a Bochner-Choi positivity theorem linking Gram matrix positivity to channel complete positivity, characterizes CP-divisibility via two-time characteristic functions, and demonstrates applications to quantum dynamical maps.
AI.motivation: To establish a connection between characteristic-function methods in quantum statistics and structural properties of quantum dynamical maps, specifically to develop tools for analyzing CP-divisibility and information flow in open quantum systems.
AI.method: Construct map-dependent quantum characteristic functions from normalized Choi operators of quantum dynamical maps. Prove a Bochner-Choi positivity theorem relating Gram matrix positivity to complete positivity. Apply the construction to intermediate dynamical maps to characterize CP-divisibility via two-time characteristic functions. Validate with numerical examples for amplitude damping and pure dephasing models.
AI.result: The positive-type condition of the Gram matrix is equivalent to complete positivity of the underlying quantum channel. CP-divisibility can be characterized by positivity of two-time characteristic functions. Numerical examples show that negativity of the Gram matrix coincides with breakdown of CP-divisibility and emergence of information backflow.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a new bridge between characteristic-function methods in quantum statistics and structural properties of quantum dynamical maps, offering tools to analyze CP-divisibility and information flow in open quantum systems.

title: Coherence Transfer in Quantum Networks
summary: Detecting coherence transfer in complex quantum networks can be challenging due to uncharacterized experimental conditions and limited system access. Here, we use static and dynamic coherence features to introduce a nonlinear criterion for identifying coherence transfer. The criterion requires only two measurement settings for network-state populations in an experimental state basis, regardless of the network's size. It remains valid even when the verification capabilities of checkpoint nodes are uncharacterized. The principle and method are general, encompassing networks with different access levels and scenarios, from those requiring no input changes to those involving coherence dynamics in the time domain. Experimentally, using remote state preparation and entanglement swapping, we transfer single polarization qubits and polarization-entangled pairs in four- and six-photon entanglement networks. The criterion provides experimental evidence of coherence transfer in multi-photon entanglement networks. Our findings offer a practical tool for coherence transfer in quantum information and open quantum systems in networks.

AI.tldr: A nonlinear criterion using static/dynamic coherence features detects coherence transfer in quantum networks with only two measurement settings, applicable to various network types and validated experimentally with multi-photon entanglement.
AI.motivation: Detecting coherence transfer in complex quantum networks is challenging due to uncharacterized experimental conditions and limited system access, necessitating a practical verification method.
AI.method: Introduces a nonlinear criterion based on static and dynamic coherence features requiring only two measurement settings for network-state populations, applicable regardless of network size and even with uncharacterized checkpoint nodes.
AI.result: Experimental validation using remote state preparation and entanglement swapping with four- and six-photon entanglement networks successfully transfers single polarization qubits and polarization-entangled pairs, providing evidence of coherence transfer.
AI.conclusion: The criterion offers a practical tool for verifying coherence transfer in quantum information and open quantum systems, applicable to networks with different access levels and scenarios.

title: Double Descent in Quantum Kernel Ridge Regression
summary: Various classical machine learning models, including linear regression, kernel methods, and deep neural networks, exhibit double descent, in which the test risk peaks near the interpolation threshold and then decreases in the overparameterized regime. However, this phenomenon has received less attention in the quantum setting. In this work, we investigate the double descent phenomenon in quantum kernel ridge regression (QKRR). By applying deterministic equivalents from random matrix theory (RMT), we derive an asymptotic expression for the test risk of QKRR in the high-dimensional limit. Our analysis rigorously characterizes the interpolation peak and reveals how explicit regularization can effectively suppress it. We corroborate our theoretical results with numerical simulations, demonstrating close agreement even for finite-size quantum systems.

AI.tldr: Quantum kernel ridge regression exhibits double descent phenomenon similar to classical ML models, with test risk peaking near interpolation threshold then decreasing in overparameterized regime, which can be suppressed by explicit regularization.
AI.motivation: Double descent phenomenon is well-studied in classical machine learning (linear regression, kernel methods, deep neural networks) but has received less attention in quantum settings. The authors aim to investigate whether this phenomenon occurs in quantum kernel ridge regression and characterize its behavior.
AI.method: Apply deterministic equivalents from random matrix theory to derive asymptotic expression for test risk of quantum kernel ridge regression in high-dimensional limit. Use rigorous mathematical analysis to characterize interpolation peak and study effects of explicit regularization. Validate theoretical results with numerical simulations on finite-size quantum systems.
AI.result: Quantum kernel ridge regression exhibits double descent phenomenon with test risk peaking near interpolation threshold. The analysis provides rigorous characterization of this peak and demonstrates that explicit regularization can effectively suppress it. Numerical simulations show close agreement with theoretical predictions even for finite-size systems.
AI.conclusion: Double descent phenomenon extends to quantum kernel ridge regression, with similar behavior to classical models. The theoretical framework using random matrix theory successfully characterizes this phenomenon and shows regularization's effectiveness in mitigating interpolation peak, providing insights for quantum machine learning model design.

title: Exponentially-enhanced Weak-field Sensing with Quantum Stark Localization
summary: Stark-localized quantum probes have recently been shown to enable quantum-enhanced weak-field sensing with polynomial or super-polynomial scaling. In this paper, we show that the spatial geography of the encoded field can elevate this advantage to a genuine exponential scaling. We study a one-dimensional Stark probe subject to an exponential gradient profile, \(V_j=e^{aj}\), and analyze its metrological performance in both equilibrium and non-equilibrium regimes, for single-particle and interacting many-body settings. In the equilibrium single-particle case, we derive an analytical lower bound showing that the quantum Fisher information grows exponentially with system size, and confirm numerically that this enhancement persists throughout the extended phase and at the localization transition. We further show that the same exponential scaling survives for mid-spectrum eigenstates and in the interacting many-body regime. This advantage remains intact under a fair resource analysis because the relevant preparation gap closes only algebraically, so the polynomial preparation overhead cannot offset the exponential gain in sensitivity. In the non-equilibrium regime, a simple product-state initialization followed by free evolution already retains exponential enhancement, eliminating the need for cooling, adiabatic preparation, or operation within a narrowly tuned sensing window. Finally, we outline a superconducting implementation based on flux-tunable transmon qubits with graded mutual inductive coupling to a common sensing bus. Our results identify exponentially graded Stark potentials as a distinct and experimentally plausible route to weak-field sensing with exponentially improving precision.

AI.tldr: Exponential gradient Stark potentials enable quantum-enhanced weak-field sensing with exponential scaling in system size, persisting across equilibrium/non-equilibrium regimes, single-particle/many-body settings, and remaining robust under fair resource analysis.
AI.motivation: While Stark-localized quantum probes have shown polynomial/super-polynomial scaling for weak-field sensing, the spatial geography of encoded fields could potentially elevate this advantage to genuine exponential scaling, offering dramatically improved sensing precision.
AI.method: Study 1D Stark probes with exponential gradient profile V_j = e^{aj}; analyze metrological performance in equilibrium/non-equilibrium regimes for single-particle and interacting many-body settings; derive analytical lower bounds; perform numerical verification; conduct fair resource analysis; propose superconducting implementation with flux-tunable transmon qubits and graded mutual inductive coupling.
AI.result: Quantum Fisher information grows exponentially with system size in equilibrium single-particle case; enhancement persists throughout extended phase and at localization transition; exponential scaling survives for mid-spectrum eigenstates and interacting many-body regime; advantage remains under fair resource analysis as preparation gap closes algebraically; non-equilibrium regime with product-state initialization retains exponential enhancement without cooling/adiabatic preparation.
AI.conclusion: Exponentially graded Stark potentials provide a distinct, experimentally plausible route to weak-field sensing with exponentially improving precision, offering robust advantages across various operational regimes and eliminating demanding preparation requirements.

title: Efficient characterization of general Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill qubits
summary: Practical utilization of Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) qubits requires not only the preparation of logical basis states, but also the ability to prepare and evaluate arbitrary logical qubit superpositions. Currently, this is typically done via quantum state tomography, which is resource-intensive. We introduce a family of positive semidefinite Hermitian operators, one for each point on the logical Bloch sphere, whose unique zero-eigenvalue ground states are the corresponding ideal GKP qubit states. We show that the expectation value of each operator serves as a witness of non-Gaussianity, and corresponds to twice the logical infidelity for states in the ideal logical GKP subspace. Furthermore, the truncated finite-dimensional counterparts of these operators yield physical approximations of arbitrary logical GKP states as their ground states. The evaluation of the proposed operators requires only three quadrature measurements, making this framework practical for both the experimental characterization and numerical optimization of GKP state preparation circuits.

AI.tldr: A framework using positive semidefinite operators to characterize and prepare arbitrary GKP qubit states with only three quadrature measurements, avoiding resource-intensive tomography.
AI.motivation: Current methods for preparing and evaluating arbitrary logical GKP qubit superpositions rely on resource-intensive quantum state tomography, creating a need for more practical characterization and optimization approaches.
AI.method: Introduces a family of positive semidefinite Hermitian operators corresponding to points on the logical Bloch sphere, whose unique zero-eigenvalue ground states are ideal GKP qubit states. These operators serve as witnesses of non-Gaussianity and measure logical infidelity.
AI.result: The expectation values of these operators correspond to twice the logical infidelity for states in the ideal logical GKP subspace. Truncated finite-dimensional versions yield physical approximations of arbitrary logical GKP states as ground states. Evaluation requires only three quadrature measurements.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a practical approach for experimental characterization and numerical optimization of GKP state preparation circuits, significantly reducing measurement requirements compared to tomography.

title: Unified adiabatic and diabatic excited-state description via the ensemble-variational quantum eigensolver
summary: Within the present noisy intermediate-scale quantum-computing era, hybrid quantum-classical-processor algorithms have emerged as promising avenues for tackling electronic-structure eigenproblems. Among them, the so-called ensemble-variational quantum eigensolver has been designed to treat ground and excited states on an equal footing and proven effective in capturing features such as conical intersections and avoided crossings between two electronic states, as we recently demonstrated for formaldimine. We also showed on that example how the underlying ensemble-variational principle was prone to provide a quasi-diabatic representation "for free". To date, this method has been limited to computing only two eigenstates of a Hamiltonian. The aim of the present paper is to show how and under what conditions this can be generalized to models that involve three coupled electronic states or more. Our approach relies on designing a parameterized basis transformation that can directly be implemented on a quantum computer for further post-treatment. This nontrivial step is accompanied by the development of quantum circuits specifically adapted to the several states of interest. An algebraic optimization strategy for the parameters of the basis transformation is formulated to obtain the target eigenstates as well as the optimally diabatic states under various objective flavors of the ensemble-variational principle. Our approach was tested for addressing the first three coupled electronic states of the H$_4^+$ molecular ion as a proof of principle, with three electrons in four spatial orbitals, along various geometries.

AI.tldr: The paper extends the ensemble-variational quantum eigensolver (E-VQE) from two to three or more coupled electronic states, developing quantum circuits and optimization strategies for multi-state diabatic representations.
AI.motivation: Current ensemble-variational quantum eigensolver methods are limited to computing only two eigenstates of a Hamiltonian, but many chemical systems involve three or more coupled electronic states that need to be treated simultaneously for accurate modeling of complex electronic phenomena.
AI.method: Develops a parameterized basis transformation implementable on quantum computers, designs specialized quantum circuits for multiple states, formulates algebraic optimization strategies for basis transformation parameters, and tests on the H4+ molecular ion with three electrons in four spatial orbitals.
AI.result: Successfully demonstrated the approach on the first three coupled electronic states of H4+ molecular ion across various geometries, showing the method can handle three-state systems and potentially more.
AI.conclusion: The ensemble-variational quantum eigensolver can be generalized to treat three or more coupled electronic states through parameterized basis transformations and specialized quantum circuits, enabling more comprehensive modeling of complex electronic structure phenomena in quantum computing.

title: Hierarchical Progressive Pauli Noise Modeling with Residual Compensation for Multi-Qubit Quantum Circuits
summary: Quantum Noise Characterization (QNC) is indispensable for benchmarking and mitigating errors in Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) devices. However, traditional Quantum Process Tomography (QPT) suffers from an exponential parameter explosion $O(4^N)$, severely hindering its scalability. In this paper, we propose a Hierarchical Progressive Optimization (HPO) framework to efficiently extract high-order spatial crosstalk in multi-qubit systems. By introducing a mathematically rigorous combinatorial projection mask, the HPO framework strategically freezes foundational low-weight topologies and exclusively isolates high-weight Pauli correlations. This progressive masking mechanism effectively reduces the optimization complexity from $O(4^N)$ to a scalable $O(N \cdot 4^w)$, successfully mitigating the barren plateau phenomenon. Simulations show that our method achieves a remarkable parameter compression rate of 96.3% on a 5-qubit system while maintaining machine precision convergence. Furthermore, to validate its practical utility, we apply the extracted spatial crosstalk model to perform Quantum Error Mitigation (QEM) on a deep-circuit 10-qubit Harrow-Hassidim-Lloyd (HHL) algorithm. Compared to the traditional global depolarizing baseline, the HPO-guided mitigation scheme breaks the unmitigated crosstalk bottleneck, achieving an unprecedented state fidelity recovery from 0.7431 to 0.9381 ($ΔF \approx 19.5\%$). Our work provides a scalable, highly accurate, and indispensable blueprint for modeling and mitigating complex multi-body errors in large-scale quantum algorithms.

AI.tldr: HPO framework efficiently extracts high-order spatial crosstalk in multi-qubit systems using combinatorial projection masks, reducing complexity from O(4^N) to O(N·4^w) and achieving 96.3% parameter compression with machine precision convergence.
AI.motivation: Traditional Quantum Process Tomography suffers from exponential parameter explosion (O(4^N)), severely hindering scalability for quantum noise characterization in NISQ devices, necessitating more efficient methods for modeling complex multi-body errors.
AI.method: Hierarchical Progressive Optimization (HPO) framework using mathematically rigorous combinatorial projection masks that strategically freeze foundational low-weight topologies and exclusively isolate high-weight Pauli correlations, enabling progressive masking to reduce optimization complexity.
AI.result: Achieves 96.3% parameter compression on 5-qubit system with machine precision convergence; when applied to QEM on 10-qubit HHL algorithm, recovers state fidelity from 0.7431 to 0.9381 (ΔF ≈ 19.5%), breaking unmitigated crosstalk bottleneck.
AI.conclusion: HPO provides a scalable, highly accurate blueprint for modeling and mitigating complex multi-body errors in large-scale quantum algorithms, addressing the exponential scaling limitations of traditional quantum noise characterization methods.

title: Fault-Tolerant Cut-Cat State Syndrome Extraction for Quantum Codes
summary: Reliable quantum computation requires fault-tolerant protocols to prevent errors from propagating during syndrome extraction in quantum error correction. We present a novel fault-tolerant syndrome extraction technique for CSS codes, which we refer to as the cut-cat state scheme. While each ancilla qubit interacts non-fault-tolerantly with a pair of data qubits, we introduce additional cat stabilizer measurements to identify and correct the resulting hook errors. Our approach maintains the key benefit of cat-based extraction, i.e., parallelized data qubit interactions, while reducing the number of simultaneous qubits required by more than half. Compared to flag-based state-of-the-art protocols, the cut-cat scheme offers a notable advantage in terms of two-qubit gate count as the code distance increases.

AI.tldr: A fault-tolerant syndrome extraction technique for CSS codes called cut-cat state scheme that reduces qubit requirements while maintaining parallel data qubit interactions.
AI.motivation: Reliable quantum computation requires fault-tolerant protocols to prevent error propagation during syndrome extraction in quantum error correction, addressing the need for efficient fault-tolerant methods.
AI.method: Cut-cat state scheme for CSS codes where ancilla qubits interact non-fault-tolerantly with data qubit pairs, supplemented by cat stabilizer measurements to identify and correct hook errors.
AI.result: Maintains parallelized data qubit interactions while reducing simultaneous qubit requirements by more than half, offering notable advantage in two-qubit gate count as code distance increases compared to flag-based protocols.
AI.conclusion: The cut-cat scheme provides an efficient fault-tolerant syndrome extraction method that balances resource requirements with error correction capability for CSS codes.

title: Quantum channel tomography: optimal bounds and a Heisenberg-to-classical phase transition
comment: 82 pages, 7 figures. This paper subsumes prior papers (arXiv:2512.13614, arXiv:2601.04180, arXiv:2601.10683), including new bounds in the near boundary regime and improved presentation
summary:
How many black-box queries to a quantum channel are needed to learn its full classical description? This question lies at the heart of quantum channel tomography (also known as quantum process tomography), a fundamental task in the characterization and validation of quantum hardware. Despite extensive prior work, the optimal query complexity for quantum channel tomography is far from fully understood.
  In this paper, we study tomography of an unknown quantum channel with input dimension $d_1$, output dimension $d_2$, and Kraus rank at most $r$, to within error $\varepsilon$. We identify the dilation rate $τ= r d_2 / d_1$ (which always satisfies $τ\geq 1$ due to the trace preservation of quantum channels) as a key parameter, and establish that the optimal query complexity of channel tomography exhibits distinct scaling laws across three regimes of $τ$.
  - In the boundary regime ($τ= 1$): we show that the query complexity is $Θ(r d_1 d_2/\varepsilon)$ for Choi trace norm error $\varepsilon$, and is upper bounded by $O(\min\{r d_1^{1.5} d_2/\varepsilon, r d_1 d_2/\varepsilon^2\})$ and lower bounded by $Ω(r d_1 d_2/\varepsilon)$ for diamond norm error $\varepsilon$.
  - In the away-from-boundary regime ($τ\geq 1+Ω(1)$): we show that the query complexity is $Θ(r d_1 d_2/\varepsilon^2)$ for both Choi trace norm and diamond norm errors $\varepsilon$.
  Our results uncover a sharp Heisenberg-to-classical phase transition in the query complexity of quantum channel tomography: at $τ=1$, the optimal query complexity exhibits Heisenberg scaling $1/\varepsilon$, whereas for $τ\geq 1+Ω(1)$, it exhibits classical scaling $1/\varepsilon^2$. In addition, we show that in the near-boundary regime ($1< τ< 1+o(1)$), the query complexity exhibits a mixture of Heisenberg and classical scaling behaviors.

AI.tldr: Quantum channel tomography query complexity exhibits a sharp phase transition: Heisenberg scaling (1/ε) at dilation rate τ=1, classical scaling (1/ε²) for τ≥1+Ω(1), with mixed scaling in the near-boundary regime.
AI.motivation: To determine the optimal query complexity for quantum channel tomography, which is fundamental for characterizing quantum hardware but whose optimal complexity remains poorly understood despite extensive prior work.
AI.method: Study tomography of unknown quantum channels with input dimension d₁, output dimension d₂, and Kraus rank ≤ r, using the dilation rate τ = r d₂ / d₁ as a key parameter. Analyze query complexity across three regimes: boundary (τ=1), away-from-boundary (τ≥1+Ω(1)), and near-boundary (1<τ<1+o(1)).
AI.result: For τ=1: Θ(r d₁ d₂/ε) query complexity for Choi trace norm error, and O(min{r d₁¹·⁵ d₂/ε, r d₁ d₂/ε²}) upper bound with Ω(r d₁ d₂/ε) lower bound for diamond norm error. For τ≥1+Ω(1): Θ(r d₁ d₂/ε²) for both Choi trace norm and diamond norm errors.
AI.conclusion: Reveals a sharp Heisenberg-to-classical phase transition in quantum channel tomography query complexity, with Heisenberg scaling at τ=1 and classical scaling for τ≥1+Ω(1), providing fundamental limits for quantum hardware characterization.

title: Theory of Quantum Imaginary-Time Mpemba Effect
summary: Quantum imaginary-time evolution (QITE) is a fundamental framework for preparing ground and thermal states, yet its computational cost scales significantly with the evolution duration $τ$. Reducing this duration is critical for practical quantum advantage. Here, we establish a unified theoretical framework for the Mpemba effect in QITE -- a counterintuitive phenomenon where a state initially farther from the ground state relaxes to it faster than one initially closer. We derive a remarkably simple necessary and sufficient condition for the occurrence of this effect, showing it is uniquely determined by the population ratios of excited states to the ground state. For practical state preparation, we introduce a rigorous sufficient condition for the finite-time Mpemba effect, ensuring the crossing occurs before reaching a prescribed proximity threshold. Furthermore, we unveil unique dynamical features, including a multiple-crossing phenomenon in multi-level systems and simultaneous intersections for collinear initial states. Our results provide criteria for identifying favorable initial states in QITE and offer deep insights into the speed limit of quantum state preparation.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes a theoretical framework for the Mpemba effect in quantum imaginary-time evolution (QITE), deriving conditions for when states farther from the ground state relax faster than closer ones, with practical applications for quantum state preparation.
AI.motivation: QITE is fundamental for preparing ground and thermal states, but its computational cost scales significantly with evolution duration τ. Reducing this duration is critical for practical quantum advantage, motivating investigation of the Mpemba effect where states farther from equilibrium can relax faster.
AI.method: Developed a unified theoretical framework for the Mpemba effect in QITE, deriving necessary and sufficient conditions based on population ratios of excited states to the ground state. Introduced rigorous sufficient conditions for finite-time Mpemba effect and analyzed dynamical features including multiple-crossing phenomena in multi-level systems.
AI.result: Derived a simple necessary and sufficient condition for Mpemba effect occurrence determined solely by excited state population ratios. Established sufficient conditions for finite-time crossing before reaching proximity thresholds. Discovered multiple-crossing phenomena in multi-level systems and simultaneous intersections for collinear initial states.
AI.conclusion: The results provide criteria for identifying favorable initial states in QITE that exhibit faster relaxation despite being farther from equilibrium, offering deep insights into quantum state preparation speed limits and practical guidance for reducing computational costs in quantum algorithms.

title: Low-dimensional platforms for single photon detection
summary: A Single-Photon Detector (SPD) can detect extremely low intensity of electromagnetic wave - down to a single photon. Driven by the rapid developments in quantum information science and an increasing demand for ultra-low-light sensing across various domains, there is a need for transformative advancements in the design and development of SPDs. In this context, low-dimensional platforms, including quantum dots, superconducting nanowires and layered materials have emerged as crucial frontiers of research. This review explores the state-of-the-art of different low-dimensional SPD platforms, focusing on the engineering physics across their device architectures, performance parameters and application potential. By critically comparing the performance and addressing current challenges inherent to each low-dimensional platform, the review aims to outline future research directions to advance next-generation SPD technologies.

AI.tldr: Review paper analyzing low-dimensional platforms for single-photon detection, comparing quantum dots, superconducting nanowires, and layered materials for advancing next-generation SPD technologies.
AI.motivation: Driven by rapid developments in quantum information science and increasing demand for ultra-low-light sensing across various domains, there is a need for transformative advancements in single-photon detector design and development.
AI.method: Comprehensive review exploring state-of-the-art low-dimensional SPD platforms, focusing on engineering physics across device architectures, performance parameters, and application potential.
AI.result: Critical comparison of performance metrics and current challenges inherent to each low-dimensional platform (quantum dots, superconducting nanowires, layered materials).
AI.conclusion: Outlines future research directions to advance next-generation SPD technologies based on comparative analysis of low-dimensional platforms.

title: Continuous-time quantum-walk centrality for protein residue interaction networks
summary: We present a quantum-dynamical framework for identifying structurally important residues in proteins based on continuous time quantum walks (CTQWs) on weighted residue interaction networks constructed from experimentally resolved structures. By mapping the weighted adjacency matrix to a Hamiltonian, residue importance emerges from the long-time averaged occupation probability, confirmed analytically through its spectral decomposition. Across a dataset of approximately 150 proteins spanning diverse structural and functional classes, CTQW centrality exhibits consistently strong agreement with classical eigenvector centrality in identifying central residues, while extending beyond it through incorporating signatures of quantum interference. Analyzing the time-averaged quantum transition matrix reveals consistently larger spectral gaps than the classical random-walk operator. Furthermore, biological relevance is confirmed through recovery of experimentally established functional residues in proteins kinase A and oxytocin. CTQW-derived centrality rankings are accessible on near-term intermediate-scale quantum hardware, as we demonstrate through a proof-of-principle implementation on IBM superconducting quantum hardware. These results establish continuous-time quantum walks as a computationally tractable framework for protein network analysis, that connects network theoretical treatments of protein structural biology to continuous-time quantum walk dynamics.

AI.tldr: Quantum-dynamical framework using continuous-time quantum walks on protein residue networks identifies structurally important residues, shows strong agreement with classical centrality measures, and demonstrates feasibility on near-term quantum hardware.
AI.motivation: To develop a quantum-dynamical approach for identifying structurally important residues in proteins that extends beyond classical network analysis methods by incorporating quantum interference effects and demonstrating feasibility on emerging quantum hardware.
AI.method: Construct weighted residue interaction networks from experimentally resolved protein structures, map weighted adjacency matrix to Hamiltonian for continuous-time quantum walks, compute long-time averaged occupation probabilities to identify important residues, and implement proof-of-principle on IBM superconducting quantum hardware.
AI.result: CTQW centrality shows strong agreement with classical eigenvector centrality across ~150 diverse proteins, reveals larger spectral gaps than classical random walks, successfully recovers experimentally established functional residues in proteins kinase A and oxytocin, and demonstrates implementation feasibility on near-term quantum hardware.
AI.conclusion: Continuous-time quantum walks provide a computationally tractable quantum-dynamical framework for protein network analysis that bridges network theory with quantum dynamics and is accessible on current intermediate-scale quantum hardware.

title: Approximate Hamiltonian Simulation Algorithm for Efficient Fluid Quantum Simulations
summary: This work aims to address the bottleneck issues of hardware resource limitation and decoherence error in the Hamiltonian simulation of quantum fluids, which are caused by the standard quantum Fourier transform and the evolution of momentum operators, resulting in excessively deep circuits and excessive two-qubit gates. We propose an approximate operator optimization scheme aimed at reducing the circuit depth in Hamiltonian evolution. The proposed scheme successfully reduces the depth of analog circuits from $O(n^2)$ to $O(nlogn)$ or even $O(n)$ by eliminating $O(n^2)$ redundant two-qubit entangling gates. In this work, the numerical experiments are implemented on a supercomputing-oriented quantum simulator, simulating two-dimensional unsteady divergent flow. Experimental results demonstrate that although the truncation of high-frequency qubit coupling terms introduces deterministic theoretical errors, scaling at $O(n)$ for AQFT and $O(n^2)$ for momentum truncation, the optimized simulations successfully preserve the inherent macroscopic temporal evolution characteristics of the fluid in a 10-qubit simulation, achieving high correlation coefficients of $r$=0.933, $r$=0.941, and $r$=0.977 for density, X-momentum, and Y-momentum distributions respectively. Furthermore, we also analyzed the relationship between the algorithm truncation error and the hardware cumulative noise when the qubit number is extended to a higher level. This study proves that rationally adjusting truncation thresholds can establish an equilibrium point, preventing the hardware cumulative error from rapidly approaching 100% at the 20-30 qubit scale, providing a feasible engineering pathway for simulating complex fluid systems on real quantum devices in the future.

AI.tldr: Proposes an approximate operator optimization scheme to reduce circuit depth in quantum fluid Hamiltonian simulation from O(n²) to O(n log n) or O(n) by eliminating redundant two-qubit gates, enabling practical simulation on near-term quantum hardware despite deterministic truncation errors.
AI.motivation: Address bottleneck issues of hardware resource limitation and decoherence error in quantum fluid Hamiltonian simulation caused by standard quantum Fourier transform and momentum operator evolution, which result in excessively deep circuits and excessive two-qubit gates.
AI.method: Proposes an approximate operator optimization scheme that reduces circuit depth by eliminating O(n²) redundant two-qubit entangling gates. Uses truncation of high-frequency qubit coupling terms and momentum truncation to simplify circuits while preserving essential physics.
AI.result: Successfully reduces analog circuit depth from O(n²) to O(n log n) or O(n). In 10-qubit simulation of 2D unsteady divergent flow, achieves high correlation coefficients: r=0.933 for density, r=0.941 for X-momentum, and r=0.977 for Y-momentum distributions. Shows that rational truncation thresholds can prevent hardware cumulative error from rapidly approaching 100% at 20-30 qubit scale.
AI.conclusion: The optimized scheme enables practical quantum simulation of complex fluid systems on near-term hardware by establishing equilibrium between truncation errors and hardware noise, providing a feasible engineering pathway for future quantum fluid simulations despite deterministic theoretical errors introduced by high-frequency term truncation.

title: Arrival-time distributions as a probe of the preferred foliation in relativistic Bohmian mechanics
summary: Relativistic extensions of de Broglie-Bohm theory postulate a preferred foliation of space-time, an additional structure essential for defining simultaneous configurations on Minkowski space-time, but conventionally believed to be empirically undetectable at quantum equilibrium. In this paper, we outline an experimental protocol for empirically detecting the preferred foliation, which is assumed to be flat for simplicity. Building on the arrival-time distributions for spin-1/2 particles predicted by Das and Dürr, we show that in an EPRB-type experiment with spacelike-separated spin and arrival-time measurements, the observed arrival-time statistics will depend crucially on the temporal order of these measurements relative to the preferred foliation of space-time. This dependence offers a potential experimental signature of the preferred foliation postulated by relativistic Bohmian models. Moreover, it implies the possibility of superluminal signaling.

AI.tldr: Relativistic Bohmian mechanics requires a preferred foliation of spacetime, traditionally thought undetectable. This paper proposes an experimental protocol using EPRB-type experiments with spin-1/2 particles to detect this foliation through arrival-time statistics.
