AI.motivation: Relativistic extensions of de Broglie-Bohm theory postulate a preferred foliation of spacetime, which is conventionally believed to be empirically undetectable at quantum equilibrium. The motivation is to challenge this conventional wisdom by developing an experimental approach to detect this fundamental structure.
AI.method: The paper builds on Das and Dürr's arrival-time distributions for spin-1/2 particles and proposes an EPRB-type experiment with spacelike-separated spin and arrival-time measurements. The key insight is that observed arrival-time statistics will depend on the temporal order of these measurements relative to the preferred foliation.
AI.result: The analysis shows that arrival-time statistics in such experiments crucially depend on the temporal order of measurements relative to the preferred foliation, providing a potential experimental signature for detecting this structure. Moreover, this dependence implies the possibility of superluminal signaling.
AI.conclusion: The preferred foliation postulated by relativistic Bohmian models may be empirically detectable through careful measurement of arrival-time statistics in EPRB-type experiments, challenging the conventional view that this structure is fundamentally unobservable.

title: Robustness Evaluation of Hybrid Quantum Neural Networks under Noise Models via System-Level Error Mitigation
summary: Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs) represent a promising direction within Quantum Machine Learning (QML), yet their realization on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices remains constrained by decoherence, gate imperfections, crosstalk, and readout errors. This study provides a systematic evaluation of noise effects and mitigation strategies in hybrid quantum neural networks (HQNNs). Zero-Noise Extrapolation (ZNE), Digital Dynamical Decoupling (DDD), and Layerwise Richardson Extrapolation (LRE) are integrated into end-to-end QNN training pipelines developed with PennyLane, simulated under Qiskit Aer noise models, and integrated with the Mitiq framework, while Probabilistic Error Cancellation (PEC) is evaluated separately under depolarizing noise due to its computational cost. Experiments conducted on the Iris dataset with five representative noise channels show that the impact of noise and the effect of mitigation are strongly dependent on the noise model and its strength. The model maintains comparatively strong performance under phase-flip and phase-damping noise, while substantial degradation is observed under high depolarizing and amplitude-damping noise. Across the evaluated mitigation methods, the observed benefits remain limited and noise-dependent: ZNE, DDD, and LRE generally follow the same degradation trends as the unmitigated baseline, while PEC shows limited gains only in the low-noise depolarizing regime. These findings highlight the need for context-specific mitigation strategies to improve the robustness of QNNs in practical NISQ settings.

AI.tldr: Systematic evaluation of noise effects and mitigation strategies in hybrid quantum neural networks reveals noise-dependent performance degradation and limited benefits from current mitigation techniques on NISQ devices.
AI.motivation: Quantum Neural Networks face significant challenges on NISQ devices due to various noise sources (decoherence, gate imperfections, crosstalk, readout errors), requiring systematic evaluation of noise effects and mitigation strategies to improve robustness.
AI.method: Integrated Zero-Noise Extrapolation (ZNE), Digital Dynamical Decoupling (DDD), and Layerwise Richardson Extrapolation (LRE) into end-to-end QNN training pipelines using PennyLane, simulated under Qiskit Aer noise models with Mitiq framework. Evaluated Probabilistic Error Cancellation (PEC) separately for depolarizing noise due to computational cost. Conducted experiments on Iris dataset with five representative noise channels.
AI.result: Noise impact and mitigation effectiveness strongly depend on noise model and strength. Models maintain relatively strong performance under phase-flip and phase-damping noise but degrade substantially under high depolarizing and amplitude-damping noise. Mitigation benefits are limited and noise-dependent: ZNE, DDD, and LRE follow similar degradation trends as unmitigated baseline, while PEC shows limited gains only in low-noise depolarizing regime.
AI.conclusion: Current noise mitigation techniques provide limited benefits for QNNs on NISQ devices, with effectiveness heavily dependent on specific noise characteristics. The findings highlight the need for context-specific mitigation strategies to improve QNN robustness in practical quantum computing settings.

title: Thermal vapor quantum battery based on collective atomic spins
summary: Quantum batteries harness non-classical resources, such as quantum coherence and entanglement, to surpass the performance limits of classical energy-storage devices. Here we realize a room-temperature quantum battery based on a collective atomic spin ensemble in a thermal alkali-metal vapor, containing approximately $10^{12}$ $^{87}$Rb atoms with coherence times exceeding 110 ms. We operationally determine the battery capacity by directly measuring the extremal internal energies accessible under unitary evolution. This tomography-free protocol agrees closely with the conventional state-based definition and verifies the decomposition of capacity into coherent and incoherent contributions. We further show that quantum coherence can substantially enhance the storage capability independently of level populations, and experimentally establish quantitative relations linking battery capacity to von Neumann, Tsallis and linear entropies. By introducing a controlled dephasing channel with a magnetic-field gradient, we observe a monotonic reduction of capacity with coherence loss and track the corresponding evolution of the entropy-capacity relations. Our results identify thermal atomic spin ensembles as a scalable platform for quantum batteries and connect macroscopic quantum energy storage with operational quantum thermodynamics.

AI.tldr: Experimental realization of a room-temperature quantum battery using a collective atomic spin ensemble in thermal alkali-metal vapor, demonstrating quantum coherence enhancement of energy storage capacity and establishing entropy-capacity relations.
AI.motivation: To harness quantum resources (coherence and entanglement) to surpass classical energy-storage limits and connect macroscopic quantum energy storage with operational quantum thermodynamics.
AI.method: Used a collective atomic spin ensemble of ~10^12 87Rb atoms in thermal vapor with coherence times >110 ms. Implemented tomography-free protocol to operationally determine battery capacity via direct measurement of extremal internal energies under unitary evolution. Introduced controlled dephasing channel with magnetic-field gradient to study coherence effects.
AI.result: Verified decomposition of capacity into coherent and incoherent contributions. Demonstrated quantum coherence substantially enhances storage capability independent of level populations. Established quantitative relations linking battery capacity to von Neumann, Tsallis and linear entropies. Observed monotonic capacity reduction with coherence loss via controlled dephasing.
AI.conclusion: Thermal atomic spin ensembles provide a scalable platform for quantum batteries, enabling macroscopic quantum energy storage and connecting quantum thermodynamics with practical energy storage applications.

title: Surpassing thermal-state limit in thermometry via non-completely positive quantum encoding
summary: Conventional quantum thermometry assumes completely positive (CP) encoding maps, where the probe is initially uncorrelated with the environment. We consider realistic scenarios with initial probe-environment correlations leading to physically realizable non-completely positive (NCP) encoding, and show how such encodings can significantly impact temperature estimation of the environment. We first consider pure entangled probe-environment initial states (Type-I NCP encoding) and analytically show that for probes and environments of equal but arbitrary dimension, the maximum achievable precision matches the thermal-state bound, as in the CP case. However, upon relaxing the constraint of pure probe-environment states and considering general correlated initial states (Type-II NCP encoding), we demonstrate that the estimation precision can surpass the thermal-state limit. This establishes a clear advantage of NCP encoding in enhancing thermometric performance. We illustrate the results using qubit probes interacting with qubit environments via XY interactions.

AI.tldr: NCP encoding from initial probe-environment correlations can enhance quantum thermometry precision beyond thermal-state limits, with Type-II NCP encoding showing superior performance over Type-I.
AI.motivation: Conventional quantum thermometry assumes CP encoding with uncorrelated initial probe-environment states, but realistic scenarios involve initial correlations leading to NCP encoding that may impact temperature estimation.
AI.method: Analyze two types of NCP encoding: Type-I with pure entangled probe-environment initial states, and Type-II with general correlated initial states. Use analytical proofs for equal-dimension probes/environments and illustrate with qubit systems interacting via XY interactions.
AI.result: Type-I NCP encoding achieves precision matching the thermal-state bound (same as CP case), while Type-II NCP encoding surpasses the thermal-state limit, demonstrating clear advantage of NCP encoding for enhanced thermometric performance.
AI.conclusion: Initial probe-environment correlations leading to NCP encoding can significantly improve quantum temperature estimation, with general correlated states (Type-II) providing superior precision enhancement over pure entangled states (Type-I).

title: Randomized Subsystem Descent for Fermion-to-Qubit Mapping
summary: We propose a versatile and efficient algorithmic framework for optimizing fermion-to-qubit mappings by generalizing the idea of randomized block coordinate descent. Our greedy approach, termed Randomized Subsystem Descent, iteratively samples a tractable subsystem from the full Hamiltonian, performs optimization within the subsystem under a given metric, and then reintegrates the updated subsystem into the global operator. Restricting the optimization to a subsystem at each iteration ensures computational efficiency, bypassing the dimensional bottlenecks that usually hinder global search heuristics. We benchmark our algorithm on one- and two-dimensional lattice hopping models, the Hubbard model with up to $16 \times 16$ sites, alongside a collection of molecular electronic-structure Hamiltonians with up to 54 modes and more than 180,000 Pauli strings. Across all benchmarks, our method consistently provides appreciable reduction in (weighted) Pauli weight, suggesting that Randomized Subsystem Descent is a practical and scalable framework for lowering the resource overhead of finding hardware-efficient Hamiltonian encodings.

AI.tldr: Randomized Subsystem Descent (RSD) is a greedy algorithm for optimizing fermion-to-qubit mappings by iteratively sampling tractable subsystems, optimizing locally, and reintegrating updates to reduce Pauli weight efficiently.
AI.motivation: Current fermion-to-qubit mapping optimization methods face computational bottlenecks due to the exponential scaling of search space with system size, making global optimization infeasible for large Hamiltonians. There's a need for scalable algorithms that can efficiently reduce Pauli weight for hardware-efficient quantum simulations.
AI.method: Randomized Subsystem Descent generalizes randomized block coordinate descent by: 1) sampling a tractable subsystem from the full Hamiltonian, 2) performing optimization within the subsystem under a given metric, 3) reintegrating the updated subsystem into the global operator. This subsystem restriction ensures computational efficiency while avoiding dimensional bottlenecks of global search heuristics.
AI.result: The algorithm was benchmarked on: 1D/2D lattice hopping models, Hubbard model up to 16×16 sites, and molecular electronic-structure Hamiltonians with up to 54 modes and >180,000 Pauli strings. Across all benchmarks, RSD consistently provided appreciable reduction in (weighted) Pauli weight, demonstrating practical scalability.
AI.conclusion: Randomized Subsystem Descent is a practical and scalable framework for lowering resource overhead in finding hardware-efficient Hamiltonian encodings, offering an efficient alternative to global optimization methods that suffer from dimensional bottlenecks.

title: Quantum Spectroscopy with Undetected Photons for Biomolecular Sensing in the Mid-Infrared
summary: We investigate quantum spectroscopy with undetected photons for protein detection in the mid-infrared spectral region. Classical Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy of protein samples (bovine serum albumin and N-terminal pro-brain natriuretic peptide) is used as reference to define the sample's mid-infrared absorption, which is then embedded in a numerical model of a double-pass quantum interferometer. We analyse parameters that influence visibility of the interference pattern formed by the signal beams, including the length of nonlinear crystal, sample length and mirror-sample distance. This leads us to a practical quantum spectrometer design with optimal image contrast at the specific amide I-II spectral bands. The simulated visibility spectra reproduce nearly identically the protein absorption features in the mid-IR and reveal temperature-induced changes to the protein secondary structure. Overall, this provides practical design rules for future quantum bio-spectroscopy applications that use only visible wavelength sources and detectors.

AI.tldr: Quantum spectroscopy with undetected photons enables protein detection in mid-IR using visible light sources/detectors, with simulated design achieving optimal contrast at amide bands.
AI.motivation: To develop practical quantum bio-spectroscopy applications that can detect proteins in the mid-infrared spectral region using only visible wavelength sources and detectors, overcoming limitations of conventional mid-IR instrumentation.
AI.method: Used classical FTIR spectroscopy of protein samples (BSA and NT-proBNP) as reference, embedded sample's mid-IR absorption in numerical model of double-pass quantum interferometer, analyzed parameters affecting interference pattern visibility (crystal length, sample length, mirror-sample distance).
AI.result: Developed practical quantum spectrometer design with optimal image contrast at specific amide I-II spectral bands; simulated visibility spectra reproduce protein absorption features nearly identically and reveal temperature-induced changes to protein secondary structure.
AI.conclusion: Provides practical design rules for future quantum bio-spectroscopy applications that enable mid-infrared protein detection using only visible wavelength sources and detectors, offering a promising alternative to conventional mid-IR spectroscopy.

title: Repeated weak measurements: watching quantum correlations evolve
comment: 17 pages including the SI; 4 figures plus 4 figures in SI
summary: Experimental access to many-body quantum systems is often limited by measurement backaction, and key dynamical properties are typically obtained by perturbing a system and measuring its response. Here we replace this active paradigm with a minimally invasive protocol based on a pair of weak quantum measurements that leverages measurement backaction as a strength. By correlating time-separated measurements with the first detecting fluctuations -- of any sort -- and the second tracking their time evolution, our method directly measures dynamical correlation functions without external perturbation. We demonstrate this technique in an atomic Bose-Einstein condensate using phase-contrast imaging to obtain the two-time density-density correlation function known as the Van Hove function and, through its Fourier transform, the dynamical structure factor. Due to the role of spatial correlations in scattering, these quantities underpin neutron and X-ray scattering and atomic Bragg spectroscopy. This approach is broadly applicable, providing access to correlation functions between any pair of observables amenable to weak measurement, thereby going beyond the capabilities of conventional strong measurements. We further isolate the role of quantum backaction through Aharonov's post-selection-based quantum weak values.

AI.tldr: A minimally invasive protocol using pairs of weak quantum measurements directly measures dynamical correlation functions without external perturbation, demonstrated in atomic BEC to obtain Van Hove function and dynamical structure factor.
AI.motivation: Conventional measurement of many-body quantum systems suffers from measurement backaction and requires perturbing the system. The authors aim to develop a minimally invasive protocol that leverages measurement backaction as a strength rather than a limitation.
AI.method: A pair of time-separated weak quantum measurements: the first detects fluctuations (of any sort) and the second tracks their time evolution. This directly measures dynamical correlation functions without external perturbation. Demonstrated in atomic Bose-Einstein condensate using phase-contrast imaging to obtain density-density correlation functions.
AI.result: Successfully measured the two-time density-density correlation function (Van Hove function) and its Fourier transform (dynamical structure factor) in atomic BEC. The approach isolates quantum backaction through Aharonov's post-selection-based quantum weak values.
AI.conclusion: The protocol provides minimally invasive access to dynamical correlation functions between any pair of observables amenable to weak measurement, going beyond conventional strong measurements. It leverages measurement backaction as a strength and is broadly applicable to quantum systems.

title: Toward quantum interconnects featuring nanometer-to-picometer bandwidth compression and THz-range quantum frequency conversion
summary: The long-range transmission of quantum information relies on multiple interfaces between photons, acting as flying qubits, and localized memories, serving as repeaters, to mitigate transmission losses. Efficient, long-range transmission necessitates the use of short, picosecond-scale photons, which are markedly different from the narrowband, nanosecond-scale photons optimal for absorption by memory elements, typically operating at wavelengths far from telecom. In this article, we point toward designs capable of bridging these regimes, leveraging the interplay between sum-frequency generation-based quantum frequency conversion and resonant confinement in an integrated ring resonator.

AI.tldr: Integrated ring resonator design bridges picosecond telecom photons with nanosecond memory-compatible photons via sum-frequency generation quantum frequency conversion.
AI.motivation: Long-range quantum communication requires efficient interfaces between short picosecond telecom photons (for transmission) and narrowband nanosecond photons (for memory absorption), which operate at different wavelengths and timescales.
AI.method: Leverages interplay between sum-frequency generation-based quantum frequency conversion and resonant confinement in an integrated ring resonator to bridge the picosecond telecom regime with nanosecond memory-compatible regime.
AI.result: The paper proposes design approaches capable of bridging these disparate regimes, though specific experimental results are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Integrated photonic ring resonators with quantum frequency conversion offer a promising architecture for interfacing telecom transmission photons with quantum memory elements in long-range quantum networks.

title: Mutually-commuting von Neumann algebra models of quantum networks and violation of Bell-type inequalities
summary: Employing mutually-commuting von Neumann algebras to represent the algebra of observables on quantum systems provides a framework for studying quantum information theory in systems with infinite degrees of freedom and quantum field theory, yielding many profound results that differ from non-relativistic quantum systems. In this paper, we establish a mutually-commuting von Neumann algebra model of quantum networks with arbitrary structures. We derive Bell-type inequalities on this model, and determine various bounds for Bell-type inequalities based on the structure of underline von Neumann algebras, and identify the algebraic structural conditions required for their violation. The conditions on the algebraic structure of observables for maximal violation of Bell-type inequalities, which we discovered in the context of von Neumann algebra models, can in turn guide the search for measurements in the non-relativistic setting.

AI.tldr: Establishes a von Neumann algebra model for quantum networks, derives Bell-type inequalities with structural bounds, and identifies algebraic conditions for maximal violation.
AI.motivation: To extend quantum information theory to systems with infinite degrees of freedom and quantum field theory using mutually-commuting von Neumann algebras, and to study quantum networks with arbitrary structures within this framework.
AI.method: Develops a mutually-commuting von Neumann algebra model for quantum networks with arbitrary structures, derives Bell-type inequalities on this model, determines bounds based on von Neumann algebra structure, and identifies algebraic conditions for violation.
AI.result: Established von Neumann algebra model for quantum networks, derived Bell-type inequalities with structural bounds, identified algebraic conditions required for violation, and discovered conditions for maximal violation that can guide measurement selection in non-relativistic settings.
AI.conclusion: The von Neumann algebra framework provides a powerful approach for studying quantum networks and Bell inequalities in infinite-dimensional systems, with discovered algebraic conditions for maximal violation offering guidance for measurement selection in non-relativistic quantum systems.

title: Measurement-defined control of single-particle interference
summary: Interference is conventionally attributed to path-accumulated phase differences, with measurement treated as a passive readout. Here we demonstrate that single-particle interference is governed by the relative phase between the prepared quantum state and the detector-defined measurement basis -- a joint quantity that is operationally inaccessible in any conventional interferometer. Using coherently seeded entangled nonlinear biphoton sources, we show that independently scanning the pump phase difference, the seed phase difference, or the signal interferometric phase each produces identical sinusoidal fringes ($V\approx0.99$) -- a three-scan equivalence impossible in any two-mode interferometer. The fringe visibility is continuously controlled through the idler-state overlap, directly encoding quantum distinguishability without idler detection. The same measurement-defined interference law persists from the single-photon to the high-flux regime. The bright-dark collective-state structure demonstrated here unifies coherent population trapping and electromagnetically induced transparency in atomic $Λ$-systems, discrete photonic interference, and single-slit diffraction within a common framework differing only in dark-subspace dimensionality, establishing measurement-defined photonic modes as a fundamental resource for quantum photonics.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates that single-particle interference is governed by the relative phase between the prepared quantum state and the detector-defined measurement basis, not just path-accumulated phase differences, using entangled biphoton sources to show three-scan equivalence and continuous control of fringe visibility through idler-state overlap.
AI.motivation: To challenge the conventional view that interference is solely due to path-accumulated phase differences with passive measurement, and to establish that interference is governed by the relative phase between the prepared quantum state and the detector-defined measurement basis - a joint quantity operationally inaccessible in conventional interferometers.
AI.method: Using coherently seeded entangled nonlinear biphoton sources to independently scan three different phase parameters: pump phase difference, seed phase difference, and signal interferometric phase, while measuring fringe visibility and controlling it through idler-state overlap without idler detection.
AI.result: All three phase scans produce identical sinusoidal fringes with visibility V≈0.99, demonstrating three-scan equivalence impossible in conventional two-mode interferometers. Fringe visibility is continuously controlled through idler-state overlap, directly encoding quantum distinguishability. The same interference law persists from single-photon to high-flux regimes.
AI.conclusion: Measurement-defined interference establishes photonic modes as a fundamental resource, unifying coherent population trapping, electromagnetically induced transparency, discrete photonic interference, and single-slit diffraction within a common framework differing only in dark-subspace dimensionality.

title: Tight Trade-off Between Internal, Assisted, and External Entanglement
summary: We derive a tight and saturable monogamy relation for three-qubit pure states that bounds the sum of concurrence and concurrence of assistance by the entanglement with an external qubit. The bound decreases strictly with increasing external entanglement, establishing a precise trade-off between internal and environment-induced entanglement. Equivalent formulations in terms of negativity and its convex-roof extensions follow. Our result provides a unified and quantitative constraint on entanglement distribution in open multipartite quantum systems.

AI.tldr: Tight monogamy relation for 3-qubit pure states bounds sum of concurrence and concurrence of assistance by external qubit entanglement, showing strict trade-off between internal and environment-induced entanglement.
AI.motivation: To establish quantitative constraints on entanglement distribution in open multipartite quantum systems by deriving precise trade-off relationships between internal entanglement and entanglement induced by external environment.
AI.method: Derivation of tight and saturable monogamy relation for three-qubit pure states using concurrence and concurrence of assistance, with extensions to negativity and its convex-roof extensions.
AI.result: Obtained bound that decreases strictly with increasing external entanglement, establishing precise trade-off between internal and environment-induced entanglement in three-qubit systems.
AI.conclusion: Provides unified quantitative constraint on entanglement distribution in open multipartite quantum systems, with implications for understanding entanglement sharing between system and environment.

title: Frequency upconversion of infrared signals via molecular optomechanical cavities
summary: Molecular optomechanical cavities have recently emerged as a promising platform for frequency upconversion, enabling the quantum coherent conversion of infrared signal into the visible range. In a recent work [F. Zou et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 132, 153602 (2024)], we proposed an amplification mechanism that can enhance the intensity of the upconverted infrared signals by a factor of 1000 or more within such a cavity under the ideal case without any noise. In this work, we employ the power spectrum method to investigate the noise added to the upconverted signal in a molecular optomechanical cavity along with the conversion efficiency from infrared signal into visible range. In the red-detuned regime, the anti-Stokes sideband achieves superior conversion efficiency relative to the Stokes sideband. Conversely, the Stokes sideband dominates under the blue-detuned condition, which amplifies the infrared signal. We further demonstrate the dependence of the added noise on the coupling strength and decay rates of the system. In particular, we find that when the infrared signal is amplified, the added noise approaches the quantum limit of one quantum.

AI.tldr: Analysis of noise and conversion efficiency in molecular optomechanical cavities for infrared-to-visible frequency upconversion, showing quantum-limited noise performance under amplification conditions.
AI.motivation: Previous work demonstrated significant amplification (1000×) of infrared signals in molecular optomechanical cavities under ideal noiseless conditions. This work investigates the practical noise characteristics and conversion efficiency of such upconversion systems to understand real-world performance limitations.
AI.method: Employed power spectrum method to analyze noise added to upconverted signals and conversion efficiency from infrared to visible range in molecular optomechanical cavities. Investigated different operational regimes (red-detuned vs blue-detuned) and parameter dependencies.
AI.result: Red-detuned regime favors anti-Stokes sideband with superior conversion efficiency, while blue-detuned regime favors Stokes sideband which amplifies infrared signals. Added noise approaches quantum limit of one quantum when infrared signal is amplified, and noise characteristics depend on coupling strength and system decay rates.
AI.conclusion: Molecular optomechanical cavities can achieve quantum-limited noise performance (approaching one quantum) during infrared signal amplification, making them promising for practical quantum coherent frequency upconversion applications despite noise considerations.

title: Numerical simulation methods for quantum sensing at parametric criticality
summary: Microwave photon detection is a key technology for low-temperature superconducting electronics and quantum information processing. A promising possibility is to use switching processes in parametric superconducting devices at criticality, which can be triggered by small perturbations. Here we demonstrate the unique sensing properties of the superconducting Kerr parametric resonator when operated in the proximity of the phase transition boundary. We utilize a semiclassical approximation to provide numerical and analytical results for the Heisenberg-Langevin and Fokker-Planck equations that describe the switching mechanism. We show that the probability of switching events is enhanced by probe input states with energies down to single quanta levels.

AI.tldr: Demonstration of superconducting Kerr parametric resonator as microwave photon detector operating near phase transition boundary, showing enhanced switching probability for single-photon-level inputs.
AI.motivation: Microwave photon detection is crucial for superconducting electronics and quantum information processing, requiring sensitive detectors that can respond to small perturbations like single photons.
AI.method: Utilize superconducting Kerr parametric resonator operated near phase transition boundary; apply semiclassical approximation to analyze switching mechanism via Heisenberg-Langevin and Fokker-Planck equations; numerical and analytical approaches.
AI.result: Demonstrated unique sensing properties of Kerr parametric resonator near criticality; showed switching probability is enhanced by probe input states with energies down to single quanta levels (single photons).
AI.conclusion: Superconducting Kerr parametric resonator operating near phase transition boundary provides promising approach for sensitive microwave photon detection, capable of responding to single-photon-level inputs through enhanced switching probability at criticality.

title: Bound entanglement detection in $4 \otimes 4$ systems via generalized Choi maps
summary: We construct a family of positive but not completely positive linear maps acting on four dimensional space. We employ these maps to detect bound entanglement in high dimensional quantum systems.

AI.tldr: Construction of positive but not completely positive linear maps on 4D space used to detect bound entanglement in high-dimensional quantum systems
AI.motivation: To develop new tools for detecting bound entanglement in high-dimensional quantum systems, which is challenging due to the subtle nature of bound entangled states that cannot be distilled into pure entangled states
AI.method: Construct a family of positive but not completely positive linear maps acting on four-dimensional space, then apply these maps as entanglement witnesses to detect bound entanglement
AI.result: Successfully constructed specific positive but not completely positive maps that can identify bound entangled states in high-dimensional quantum systems that would otherwise be undetectable by standard entanglement criteria
AI.conclusion: The constructed family of maps provides an effective tool for detecting bound entanglement in high-dimensional quantum systems, advancing the understanding and characterization of quantum entanglement beyond distillable states

title: State-Averaged Quantum Algorithms for Multiconfigurational Surface Chemistry: A Benchmark on Rh@TiO2(110)
summary:
Accurate modeling of surface catalytic processes often requires methods capable of describing strong correlation, charge transfer, and multiple closely lying electronic states. While density functional theory remains widely used, its limitations for localized electronic states motivate the use of wavefunction-based approaches and, more recently, quantum computing algorithms. However, the performance of quantum ansätze in chemically motivated, multistate settings remains largely unexplored.
  Here, we benchmark state-averaged factorized unitary coupled cluster with singles and doubles (SA-fUCCSD) and the adaptive, problem-tailored ansatz (SA-ADAPT) using an embedded cluster model of NO adsorption on Rh-doped TiO2(110). The system exhibits pronounced multiconfigurational character and multiple state crossings, providing a stringent test. State-averaged CASSCF serves as a reference, and the quantum ansätze are evaluated as solvers for the corresponding CASCI problem within a fixed orbital basis.
  We find that SA-fUCCSD improves with increasing circuit depth but requires many parameters and shows sensitivity to initialization. In contrast, SA-ADAPT achieves near-CASSCF accuracy with significantly fewer operators. A modified operator selection scheme, incorporating multiple operators per iteration, substantially accelerates convergence.
  Our results demonstrate the efficiency of adaptive ansätze for multistate problems and establish a controlled benchmark for quantum algorithms in chemically motivated systems beyond minimal models.

AI.tldr: Benchmark of quantum ansätze (SA-fUCCSD and SA-ADAPT) for multistate catalytic systems using NO adsorption on Rh-doped TiO2(110) as test case, showing adaptive ansätze achieve near-CASSCF accuracy with fewer operators.
AI.motivation: Surface catalytic processes require methods for strong correlation, charge transfer, and multiple electronic states; DFT has limitations for localized states, motivating wavefunction-based approaches and quantum computing algorithms, but quantum ansätze performance in multistate chemical systems remains unexplored.
AI.method: Benchmarked state-averaged factorized unitary coupled cluster with singles and doubles (SA-fUCCSD) and adaptive problem-tailored ansatz (SA-ADAPT) using embedded cluster model of NO adsorption on Rh-doped TiO2(110). State-averaged CASSCF served as reference, and quantum ansätze were evaluated as solvers for CASCI problem within fixed orbital basis.
AI.result: SA-fUCCSD improves with circuit depth but requires many parameters and shows sensitivity to initialization. SA-ADAPT achieves near-CASSCF accuracy with significantly fewer operators. Modified operator selection scheme with multiple operators per iteration substantially accelerates convergence.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive ansätze are efficient for multistate problems, establishing controlled benchmark for quantum algorithms in chemically motivated systems beyond minimal models.

title: Exponential quantum space advantage for Shannon entropy estimation in data streams
summary: Near-term quantum devices with limited qubits motivate the study of space-bounded quantum computation in the data stream model. We show that Shannon entropy estimation exhibits an exponential separation between quantum and classical space complexity in this setting. Technically, we develop a two-stage quantum streaming algorithm based on a quantum procedure with an explicitly constructed oracle derived from the streaming input. This algorithm achieves logarithmic space complexity in the accuracy parameter, whereas any classical streaming algorithm requires polynomial space. In sharp contrast, existing results for Shannon entropy estimation in the quantum query model achieve only a quadratic speedup. Our work establishes a natural problem with practical applications in computer networking that admits an exponential quantum space advantage, revealing a fundamental gap between quantum query complexity and streaming space complexity.

AI.tldr: Quantum streaming algorithm achieves exponential space advantage over classical for Shannon entropy estimation
AI.motivation: Near-term quantum devices with limited qubits motivate space-bounded quantum computation in the data stream model, seeking problems with practical applications that demonstrate quantum advantage
AI.method: Developed a two-stage quantum streaming algorithm based on a quantum procedure with an explicitly constructed oracle derived from the streaming input
AI.result: Quantum algorithm achieves logarithmic space complexity in accuracy parameter, while any classical streaming algorithm requires polynomial space - exponential separation
AI.conclusion: Establishes Shannon entropy estimation as a natural problem with practical applications that admits exponential quantum space advantage, revealing fundamental gap between quantum query complexity and streaming space complexity

title: Decoherence in Waveguide Quantum Electrodynamics using Matrix Product States
summary: We present a matrix product state (MPS) method for including decoherence processes in calculations involving waveguide quantum electrodynamics (waveguide QED) using density matrices. The approach is based on collision quantum optics, where the many-body state of the waveguide is represented as discrete time bins, which are efficiently represented using an MPS chain. Our method is a generalization of previous MPS methods, and we demonstrate how one can efficiently expand to density matrices, allowing for the inclusion of various loss processes in the form of Lindblad terms in the Liouvillian superoperator responsible for the relevant dissipation dynamics. As an application of the theory, we study various waveguide QED systems and the influence of emitter pure dephasing (which is one of the most important processes in real systems) on the light-matter interactions, including a two-level system (TLS) in a semi-infinite waveguide with time-delayed feedback, two spatially separated TLSs with finite delays, and finally the scattering of few-photon Fock pulses on a TLS. In addition to emitter pure dephasing, we also show how to include off-chip radiative decay, and show how it differs qualitatively from pure dephasing.

AI.tldr: MPS method for modeling decoherence in waveguide QED using density matrices and collision quantum optics approach with discrete time bins.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient computational method for including decoherence processes (like pure dephasing) in waveguide QED systems, which are crucial for modeling real experimental systems but challenging to incorporate in existing MPS approaches.
AI.method: Generalizes previous MPS methods using collision quantum optics with discrete time bins, efficiently representing waveguide states as MPS chains, and expanding to density matrices to include Lindblad terms for dissipation dynamics.
AI.result: Demonstrated application to various waveguide QED systems: TLS in semi-infinite waveguide with time-delayed feedback, two spatially separated TLSs with finite delays, and scattering of few-photon Fock pulses on TLS, showing effects of pure dephasing and off-chip radiative decay.
AI.conclusion: The method provides an efficient framework for modeling decoherence in waveguide QED, enabling study of realistic loss processes and revealing qualitative differences between pure dephasing and radiative decay in light-matter interactions.

title: Shannon and Rényi entropies of molecular densities: insights into extensivity and the incomplete description of electron correlation
summary: In this work, we investigate the reliability of information-theoretic measures based on the electron-density and shape-function, specifically Shannon and Rényi entropies, as descriptors of electronic correlation. By establishing a rigorous decomposition of these entropic measures into additive and nonadditive contributions, supported on a Mulliken-like atomic partition of molecules, we systematically analyze the asymptotic behavior of the entropies at the infinite-internuclear-distance limit to assess the problem of static correlation and extensivity. Our algebraic and numerical analysis reveals several flaws in the use of these density-based descriptors. We demonstrate that for minimal-basis and different theoretical levels, the Shannon and Rényi entropies fail to encode the amount of static correlation conveyed by the underlying wavefunction. Conversely, shape-function Shannon entropies and Rényi entropies (for $α\neq 1$) violate extensivity. In larger basis sets, uncorrelated Hartree-Fock densities consistently overestimate entropy compared to sufficiently correlated (e.g., full-valence-CAS) densities. Moreover, the entropies for insufficiently correlated methods violate extensivity. These findings indicate that electron-density-based measures are insufficient for capturing static correlation, suggesting that robust entropic descriptors should be constructed from higher-dimensional Hilbert-space objects.

AI.tldr: Shannon and Rényi entropies based on electron density and shape function fail as reliable descriptors of electronic correlation, violating extensivity and inadequately capturing static correlation.
AI.motivation: To investigate the reliability of information-theoretic measures (Shannon and Rényi entropies) based on electron density and shape function as descriptors of electronic correlation, particularly assessing their ability to capture static correlation and maintain extensivity properties.
AI.method: Established rigorous decomposition of entropic measures into additive and nonadditive contributions using Mulliken-like atomic partition of molecules. Conducted systematic analysis of asymptotic behavior at infinite-internuclear-distance limit to assess static correlation and extensivity. Performed algebraic and numerical analysis across minimal-basis and different theoretical levels, comparing Hartree-Fock densities with correlated (full-valence-CAS) densities.
AI.result: Shannon and Rényi entropies fail to encode static correlation conveyed by underlying wavefunction. Shape-function Shannon entropies and Rényi entropies (for α≠1) violate extensivity. In larger basis sets, uncorrelated Hartree-Fock densities consistently overestimate entropy compared to sufficiently correlated densities. Entropies for insufficiently correlated methods violate extensivity.
AI.conclusion: Electron-density-based measures are insufficient for capturing static correlation. Robust entropic descriptors should be constructed from higher-dimensional Hilbert-space objects rather than relying solely on electron density or shape function.

title: Generation of energy-time entangled triphotons in a six-level cold atomic system
summary: Multiphoton entangled states are pivotal resources for implementing optical quantum information protocols. Recently, energy-time-entangled triphotons have been observed in hot atomic ensembles. However, in these protocols, the complex fifth-order nonlinear susceptibility entailed by four- or five-level systems limits our understanding of triphoton generation. Here, to directly capture the generation mechanism of triphotons and their associated optical properties, we investigate the generation of energy-time-entangled triphotons in a six-level cold atomic ensemble. The fifth-order nonlinear susceptibility indicates the existence of two sets of spontaneous six-wave mixing in the system. Notably, triphoton generation in this system is subject to stringent timing constraints. Collectively, these characteristics give rise to threefold coincidence counts, which -- dominated by the fifth-order nonlinear susceptibility -- exhibit asymmetrically damped Rabi oscillations in the two-dimensional time domain. Furthermore, we analytically derive that the temporal correlation properties of conditional two-photon states are preserved -- a unique feature of $W$-class tripartite entanglement. These results not only lay the groundwork for the experimental preparation of triphotons using six-level systems but also provide key support for understanding the generation mechanism of triphotons involving more complex fifth-order nonlinear susceptibilities.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates energy-time-entangled triphoton generation in a six-level cold atomic ensemble, revealing that the process involves two sets of spontaneous six-wave mixing and exhibits unique timing constraints and asymmetrically damped Rabi oscillations in coincidence counts.
AI.motivation: Previous observations of energy-time-entangled triphotons in hot atomic ensembles were limited by complex fifth-order nonlinear susceptibilities in four- or five-level systems, hindering understanding of triphoton generation mechanisms and optical properties.
AI.method: The researchers investigate triphoton generation in a six-level cold atomic ensemble, analyzing the fifth-order nonlinear susceptibility to reveal two sets of spontaneous six-wave mixing processes and studying timing constraints and coincidence count patterns.
AI.result: Triphoton generation exhibits stringent timing constraints and threefold coincidence counts dominated by fifth-order nonlinear susceptibility, showing asymmetrically damped Rabi oscillations in the two-dimensional time domain. The temporal correlation properties of conditional two-photon states are preserved, indicating W-class tripartite entanglement.
AI.conclusion: The study provides foundational understanding for experimental preparation of triphotons using six-level systems and offers key insights into generation mechanisms involving complex fifth-order nonlinear susceptibilities, with implications for optical quantum information protocols.

title: Dissipative dynamics and superradiant countinuous time crystal in a Rydberg-dressed Dicke system
summary: The interplay between many-body interactions and controlled dissipation provides a rich framework for exploring nonequilibrium quantum phases. In this work, we explore an open Dicke model including Rydberg-dressed interactions in a driven-dissipative cavity and unveil its unique nonequilibrium dynamics therein. We find that Rydberg-dressed interactions generate an additional critical coupling, which alters the stability of fixed points and hence determines fruitful dynamical phase transitions. Beyond the mean-field limit, we demonstrate that our system supports a superradiant continuous time crystal (CTC) phase, proving CTC can exist in an interacting spin-1/2 system. By bridging driven-dissipative quantum cavity and interacting atomic systems, our Rydberg-dressed Dicke system offers measurable signatures from the cavity emission photons, making it experimentally feasible as a versatile platform for exploring dynamical phase transitions and macroscopic temporal order in open quantum matter.

AI.tldr: Rydberg-dressed Dicke model in driven-dissipative cavity reveals new critical coupling from interactions, enabling superradiant continuous time crystal phase in spin-1/2 system with measurable cavity photon signatures.
AI.motivation: To explore nonequilibrium quantum phases by combining many-body interactions with controlled dissipation, specifically bridging driven-dissipative quantum cavities with interacting atomic systems to study dynamical phase transitions and macroscopic temporal order.
AI.method: Study an open Dicke model with Rydberg-dressed interactions in a driven-dissipative cavity, analyzing both mean-field dynamics and beyond-mean-field effects to identify phase transitions and time-crystalline behavior.
AI.result: Rydberg-dressed interactions generate an additional critical coupling that alters fixed-point stability and enables rich dynamical phase transitions. The system supports a superradiant continuous time crystal (CTC) phase, demonstrating CTC existence in an interacting spin-1/2 system.
AI.conclusion: The Rydberg-dressed Dicke system provides an experimentally feasible platform with measurable cavity photon signatures for exploring dynamical phase transitions and macroscopic temporal order in open quantum matter.

title: Fundamentals and Applications of Hybrid Electroand Opto-mechanical system coupled to Superconducting Qubit: A Short Review
summary: Superconducting qubits, realized by incorporating Josephson junctions into superconducting circuits, behave as artificial atoms with anharmonic energy spectra and can be precisely controlled and measured using microwave cavities within the framework of circuit quantum electrodynamics (cQED). Since its emergence in the early 2000s, cQED has established superconducting qubits as leading candidates for scalable quantum devices and has enabled the exploration of hybrid quantum systems that integrate disparate physical platformsThis review surveys superconducting hybrid quantum electromechanical systems in which mechanical resonators are coupled to superconducting qubits, with a focus on two widely used qubit platforms: the transmon and the fluxonium. We provide an overview of the underlying coupling mechanisms arising from interactions through the phase and charge degrees of freedom of the qubit, and discuss how these mechanisms give rise to both longitudinal and transverse qubit-mechanical interactions. We further review extensions of electromechanical platforms to electro-optomechanical architectures, in which optical cavities are integrated to enable coherent interfacing between superconducting circuits and optical photons. This review aims to present a unified framework and perspective on qubit-mechanical and qubit-mechanical-optical hybrid systems in superconducting quantum technologies and applications related to sensors.

AI.tldr: Review of superconducting hybrid quantum electromechanical systems focusing on transmon and fluxonium qubits coupled to mechanical resonators, covering coupling mechanisms and extensions to electro-optomechanical architectures for quantum sensing applications.
AI.motivation: To survey and provide a unified framework for understanding superconducting hybrid quantum electromechanical systems, particularly focusing on how mechanical resonators couple to superconducting qubits (transmon and fluxonium), and to explore extensions to electro-optomechanical architectures for quantum sensing applications.
AI.method: Review paper methodology: comprehensive survey of existing research on superconducting hybrid quantum electromechanical systems, focusing on coupling mechanisms through phase and charge degrees of freedom, analysis of longitudinal and transverse qubit-mechanical interactions, and examination of electro-optomechanical architectures integrating optical cavities.
AI.result: Provides systematic overview of coupling mechanisms in superconducting hybrid quantum electromechanical systems, detailing how phase and charge interactions lead to both longitudinal and transverse qubit-mechanical couplings, and demonstrates how electro-optomechanical architectures enable coherent interfacing between superconducting circuits and optical photons.
AI.conclusion: Superconducting hybrid quantum electromechanical systems, particularly those using transmon and fluxonium qubits coupled to mechanical resonators, represent promising platforms for quantum technologies and sensing applications, with electro-optomechanical extensions enabling coherent optical interfaces for enhanced functionality and broader applications.

title: Davies-Morris-Shore Framework for Multilevel Quantum Batteries: Dark and Funnel States in Interacting Qutrit Systems
summary: Dark and subradiant states have emerged as a promising resource for stabilizing open quantum batteries against dissipation, but existing studies are largely limited to qubit ensembles and symmetry-based constructions. Here we introduce a systematic, thermodynamically consistent framework for identifying long-lived energy storage states in interacting multilevel quantum batteries, combining the Davies master equation with a Morris-Shore (MS)-type decomposition of dissipative coupling blocks. Focusing on a minimal model of two interacting qutrits coupled to a common bath, we analytically construct dark, bright, and funnel states-excited states that decay exclusively into protected manifolds. We also derive quantitative robustness conditions governed by the ratio of interaction strength to anharmonicity. We show that multilevel ladder structure and exchange interactions enable energetic storage states beyond the qubit case. Numerical simulations confirm that these states exhibit long-lived energy storage under realistic dissipation. Finally, we show that high-energy funnel states provide a natural design target for multilevel quantum batteries, as their decay pathways are highly structured and directed toward protected manifolds. Knowledge of these pathways offers a principled basis for developing future protection and control strategies in superconducting multilevel platforms.

AI.tldr: Systematic framework identifies long-lived energy storage states in multilevel quantum batteries using dark/subradiant states, Morris-Shore decomposition, and thermodynamic consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing studies on dark/subradiant states for quantum battery stabilization are limited to qubit ensembles and symmetry-based constructions, lacking systematic approaches for multilevel systems.
AI.method: Combines Davies master equation with Morris-Shore decomposition of dissipative coupling blocks to analytically construct dark, bright, and funnel states in interacting multilevel systems. Focuses on two interacting qutrits coupled to common bath.
AI.result: Derived quantitative robustness conditions governed by interaction strength to anharmonicity ratio. Showed multilevel ladder structure and exchange interactions enable energetic storage states beyond qubit case. Numerical simulations confirm long-lived energy storage under realistic dissipation.
AI.conclusion: High-energy funnel states provide natural design target for multilevel quantum batteries due to structured decay pathways toward protected manifolds. Framework offers principled basis for protection/control strategies in superconducting multilevel platforms.

title: Quantangle-SAT: A Quantum SAT Solver Based on Entanglement and Equivalence Checking
summary: Satisfiability (SAT) is a central problem in computer science, and advances in SAT-solving algorithms have a far-reaching impact across many fields. Recent works have proposed quantum SAT solvers based on Grover's algorithm, a quantum search technique. However, Grover-based approaches face a key limitation: they typically require prior knowledge of the number of satisfying assignments of the target Boolean formula. This information is unavailable in most practical settings. Quantum counting can be used to estimate this quantity, but it incurs a computational overhead that is several orders of magnitude higher than Grover search. In this paper, we propose a novel quantum SAT solver based on entanglement and equivalence checking. Our method does not assume prior knowledge of the number of solutions and is computationally more efficient than quantum counting. Although the worst case time complexity is inevitably exponential, we prove that the expected time complexity of our approach is only constant time O(1) over random Boolean functions. Experimental results also support our theoretical claim.

AI.tldr: Novel quantum SAT solver using entanglement and equivalence checking that doesn't require prior knowledge of solution count, with O(1) expected time complexity for random Boolean functions.
AI.motivation: Existing quantum SAT solvers based on Grover's algorithm require prior knowledge of the number of satisfying assignments, which is unavailable in practice. Quantum counting can estimate this but incurs significant computational overhead.
AI.method: Proposes a quantum SAT solver based on entanglement and equivalence checking that doesn't assume prior knowledge of the number of solutions. The approach is computationally more efficient than quantum counting methods.
AI.result: Proves that the expected time complexity is constant time O(1) over random Boolean functions, despite worst-case exponential complexity. Experimental results support the theoretical claims.
AI.conclusion: The proposed quantum SAT solver overcomes the key limitation of Grover-based approaches by eliminating the need for prior knowledge of solution count while maintaining computational efficiency, with promising constant expected time complexity for random functions.

title: Equivalence of Local Dynamical Hidden-Variable Models to Static Bell Locality
summary: Physical intuition suggests that local dynamical evolution or measurement-induced disturbances of hidden variables might bypass Bell's theorem. We establish a generalized transition-kernel framework encompassing arbitrary local dynamics and rigorously prove that any strictly local dynamical model is mathematically equivalent to a static Bell model. We show that attempts to circumvent this equivalence -- including historical temporal models and recent macroscopic pilot-wave hydrodynamic analogs -- inevitably introduce explicit nonlocality (parameter dependence) or forfeit measurement independence. Our results establish a general boundary: nonlocal statistical correlations cannot be generated by purely local dynamical processes.

AI.tldr: Local dynamical models cannot generate Bell-violating correlations without introducing nonlocality or sacrificing measurement independence.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether local dynamical evolution or measurement-induced disturbances of hidden variables could potentially bypass Bell's theorem and explain quantum correlations through purely local processes.
AI.method: Developed a generalized transition-kernel framework encompassing arbitrary local dynamics, then rigorously proved mathematical equivalence between any strictly local dynamical model and static Bell models. Analyzed historical temporal models and recent hydrodynamic analogs within this framework.
AI.result: Proved that any strictly local dynamical model is mathematically equivalent to a static Bell model. Showed that attempts to circumvent this equivalence inevitably introduce explicit nonlocality (parameter dependence) or forfeit measurement independence. Established that nonlocal statistical correlations cannot be generated by purely local dynamical processes.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a general boundary: purely local dynamical processes cannot generate the nonlocal statistical correlations observed in quantum mechanics. Any successful hidden-variable model must either incorporate nonlocality or sacrifice measurement independence, confirming the fundamental constraints of Bell's theorem.

title: Block-encodings as programming abstractions: The Eclipse Qrisp BlockEncoding Interface
summary: Block-encoding is a foundational technique in modern quantum algorithms, enabling the implementation of non-unitary operations by embedding them into larger unitary matrices. While theoretically powerful and essential for advanced protocols like Quantum Singular Value Transformation (QSVT) and Quantum Signal Processing (QSP), the generation of compilable implementations of block-encodings poses a formidable challenge. This work presents the BlockEncoding interface within the Eclipse Qrisp framework, establishing block-encodings as a high-level programming abstraction accessible to a broad scientific audience. Serving as both a technical framework introduction and a hands-on tutorial, this paper explicitly details key underlying concepts abstracted away by the interface, such as block-encoding construction and qubitization, and their practical integration into methods like the Childs-Kothari-Somma (CKS) algorithm. We outline the interface's software architecture, encompassing constructors, core utilities, arithmetic composition, and algorithmic applications such as matrix inversion, polynomial filtering, and Hamiltonian simulation. Through code examples, we demonstrate how this interface simplifies both the practical realization of advanced quantum algorithms and their associated resource estimation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the BlockEncoding interface in the Eclipse Qrisp framework, making block-encoding techniques accessible as high-level programming abstractions for implementing advanced quantum algorithms like QSVT and QSP.
AI.motivation: Block-encoding is fundamental for modern quantum algorithms but presents significant implementation challenges. The authors aim to make these techniques accessible to a broader scientific audience by providing a high-level programming abstraction that simplifies practical implementation and resource estimation.
AI.method: The paper presents the BlockEncoding interface within Eclipse Qrisp framework, detailing its software architecture including constructors, core utilities, arithmetic composition, and algorithmic applications. It provides explicit details on underlying concepts like block-encoding construction, qubitization, and integration into algorithms like CKS, supported by code examples.
AI.result: The BlockEncoding interface successfully establishes block-encodings as accessible programming abstractions, simplifying the practical realization of advanced quantum algorithms such as matrix inversion, polynomial filtering, and Hamiltonian simulation, while facilitating resource estimation.
AI.conclusion: The BlockEncoding interface bridges the gap between theoretical block-encoding techniques and practical implementation, making advanced quantum algorithms more accessible to researchers and enabling easier development and resource analysis of quantum applications.

title: Quantum theory for phonon lasing and non-classical state generation in mixed-species and single trapped ions
summary: In this article we present a comprehensive theoretical investigation of phonon lasing with mixed-species trapped ions, as demonstrated in [T. Behrle, Phys. Rev. Lett. 131 (2023)], employing both a semi-classical mean-field description and a full quantum theory. We derive an analytic expression for the second-order coherence function, confirming the experimental observation of the system's lasing behaviour above threshold. Building on the successful implementation of the two-ion lasing scheme, we propose a novel approach for achieving phonon lasing with a single trapped ion, offering significant experimental advantages and making the implementation of multiple phonon lasers within a single setup feasible. Furthermore, we explore lasing in a squeezed basis and in different regimes of the Lamb-Dicke approximation, highlighting the potential to produce non-classical states with promising applications in precision sensing. Our analysis of a sensing protocol based on squeezed states, using experimentally feasible parameters, shows a sensitivity enhancement of up to two orders of magnitude.

AI.tldr: Theoretical investigation of phonon lasing with trapped ions, analyzing two-ion experiments, proposing single-ion lasing, and exploring squeezed states for enhanced quantum sensing.
AI.motivation: To provide comprehensive theoretical understanding of phonon lasing with trapped ions, building on experimental demonstrations, and to propose novel approaches for improved experimental implementations and quantum sensing applications.
AI.method: Employed both semi-classical mean-field description and full quantum theory to analyze phonon lasing; derived analytic expression for second-order coherence function; proposed single-ion lasing scheme; explored lasing in squeezed basis and different Lamb-Dicke regimes.
AI.result: Confirmed experimental observation of lasing behavior above threshold; demonstrated feasibility of single-ion phonon lasing; showed potential for producing non-classical states; achieved sensitivity enhancement of up to two orders of magnitude in squeezed-state sensing protocol.
AI.conclusion: Theoretical framework successfully explains experimental phonon lasing, proposes practical single-ion implementation, and demonstrates significant quantum sensing advantages through squeezed states, enabling multiple phonon lasers in single setups with enhanced precision.

title: Perfect quantum strategies for quantum magic rectangular games: a complete structural characterization
summary: Quantum magic rectangular games provide a natural setting for studying perfect quantum strategies and their underlying structure. However, existing analyses are largely case-specific and do not reveal a unifying characterization. In this work, we give a complete characterization of all perfect quantum strategies for quantum magic rectangular games, in the form of necessary and sufficient conditions on the shared state and measurement operators. Our approach identifies a structural set of constraints that any perfect strategy must satisfy, thereby providing a unified framework for understanding these games. As an illustration, our characterization shows that even in the $3 \times 3$ case, two pairs of bell states are not structurally enforced for perfect quantum strategies. More broadly, our results offer new insights into the structure of quantum correlations underlying perfect nonlocal strategies.

AI.tldr: Complete characterization of perfect quantum strategies for quantum magic rectangular games, providing necessary and sufficient conditions on shared states and measurements.
AI.motivation: Existing analyses of quantum magic rectangular games are case-specific and lack unifying characterization, creating a need for a comprehensive framework to understand perfect quantum strategies and their underlying structure.
AI.method: Developed a structural set of constraints that any perfect quantum strategy must satisfy, identifying necessary and sufficient conditions on shared states and measurement operators for perfect strategies in quantum magic rectangular games.
AI.result: Complete characterization of all perfect quantum strategies, showing that even in the 3×3 case, two pairs of Bell states are not structurally enforced for perfect strategies, providing new insights into quantum correlation structures.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a unified framework for understanding quantum magic rectangular games, revealing fundamental structural constraints on perfect quantum strategies and offering broader insights into quantum correlations underlying nonlocal strategies.

title: Optomechanical Detection of Individual Gas Collisions
summary: We experimentally demonstrate the detection of momentum transfers from individual collisions of Kr, Xe, and SF$_6$ with an optically levitated nanoparticle, finding good agreement with theoretical expectations. The observed event rates accurately measure the gas partial pressures, while the spectral shape provides a sensitive probe of the surface properties of the nanoparticle, including its temperature. The reconstruction of impulse signals as small as 200 keV/$c$ further establishes that levitated optomechanical sensors can reach the sensitivity required for precision measurements of fundamental particle interactions, and demonstrates a proof-of-principle for a primary pressure sensor based on the detection of individual gas particle collisions.

AI.tldr: Experimental demonstration of detecting individual gas particle collisions with optically levitated nanoparticles, enabling gas pressure measurement and surface property analysis with high momentum sensitivity.
AI.motivation: To develop ultra-sensitive optomechanical sensors capable of detecting individual gas particle collisions, enabling precision measurements of fundamental particle interactions and creating a new type of primary pressure sensor.
AI.method: Used optically levitated nanoparticles to detect momentum transfers from individual collisions with Kr, Xe, and SF6 gas molecules, analyzing event rates and spectral shapes of the impulse signals.
AI.result: Successfully detected individual gas collisions with momentum transfers as small as 200 keV/c, achieving good agreement with theoretical predictions. Event rates accurately measured gas partial pressures, while spectral shapes provided sensitive probes of nanoparticle surface properties including temperature.
AI.conclusion: Levitated optomechanical sensors can achieve the sensitivity required for precision measurements of fundamental particle interactions and demonstrate proof-of-principle for a primary pressure sensor based on individual gas particle collision detection.

title: Engineered broadband Purcell protection using a shared $Π$-filter for multiplexed superconducting qubits
summary: We propose a broadband Purcell-protection scheme based on a single shared filter integrated directly into the feedline, enabling simultaneous protection of multiple qubits in a compact architecture with minimal hardware overhead. The filter consists of two open-ended stubs connected by an in-line transmission line, forming a $Π$ geometry, and operates via engineered passive microwave interference that suppresses the real part of the environmental admittance over a wide frequency window. Circuit simulations and finite-element modeling show strong suppression of transmission within the target band (the qubit's frequencies) while preserving the readout and reset modes of the multiplexed architecture. For realistic device parameters, the proposed design yields Purcell-limited relaxation times exceeding $1$ ms over a frequency span of approximately $1.5$ GHz, which can be further extended with straightforward modifications of the design. Our results establish the $Π$-filter as a compact and scalable solution for broadband impedance engineering in superconducting quantum circuits, compatible with standard dispersive readout protocols.

AI.tldr: A broadband Purcell-protection scheme using a single Π-filter integrated into the feedline to simultaneously protect multiple qubits with minimal hardware overhead, achieving >1 ms Purcell-limited relaxation times over ~1.5 GHz bandwidth.
AI.motivation: To address the need for simultaneous protection of multiple qubits from Purcell decay in superconducting quantum circuits while maintaining compact architecture, minimal hardware overhead, and compatibility with standard dispersive readout protocols.
AI.method: A Π-filter consisting of two open-ended stubs connected by an in-line transmission line, integrated directly into the feedline. The filter operates via engineered passive microwave interference that suppresses the real part of environmental admittance over a wide frequency window. Design validated through circuit simulations and finite-element modeling.
AI.result: Strong suppression of transmission within the target band (qubit frequencies) while preserving readout and reset modes. For realistic device parameters, Purcell-limited relaxation times exceed 1 ms over approximately 1.5 GHz frequency span, extendable with design modifications.
AI.conclusion: The Π-filter establishes a compact and scalable solution for broadband impedance engineering in superconducting quantum circuits, compatible with standard dispersive readout protocols and enabling simultaneous protection of multiple qubits with minimal hardware overhead.

title: Security Risks of VOA-Induced Luminescence in Chip-Based quantum key distribution
summary: Integrated photonics is widely regarded as a key enabler for scalable quantum key distribution (QKD), offering compactness, stability, and compatibility with semiconductor fabrication. Despite rapid advances in chip-based QKD, the implementation security of integrated photonic components remains insufficiently understood. Here we present the first systematic study of an implementation-level security vulnerability associated with p-n junction-based variable optical attenuators (VOAs), a ubiquitous component in integrated QKD transmitters. We theoretically and experimentally demonstrate that electrically biased p-n junction VOAs emit spontaneous luminescence. Using a single-photon-sensitive spectral measurement technique, we identify the emission wavelength to be centered around 1107 nm, well separated from the C-band quantum signals. This spectral separation gives rise to a previously unrecognized wavelength-resolved side channel, enabling potential wavelength-splitting attacks without directly disturbing the encoded quantum states. By incorporating the measured luminescence into a quantitative security analysis, we show that even extremely weak emission can lead to non-negligible information leakage. Our findings reveal a fundamental and previously overlooked security risk in photonic integrated QKD systems and highlight the necessity of security-aware device design for future integrated quantum communication technologies.

AI.tldr: First systematic study of security vulnerability in p-n junction VOAs used in integrated QKD transmitters, revealing spontaneous luminescence at 1107 nm that creates wavelength-resolved side channel for potential attacks.
AI.motivation: Implementation security of integrated photonic components in QKD systems remains insufficiently understood despite rapid advances in chip-based QKD. There's a need to identify and analyze potential security vulnerabilities in ubiquitous components like variable optical attenuators.
AI.method: Theoretical and experimental demonstration of spontaneous luminescence from electrically biased p-n junction VOAs using single-photon-sensitive spectral measurement technique. Incorporation of measured luminescence into quantitative security analysis to evaluate information leakage.
AI.result: Identified emission wavelength centered around 1107 nm, well separated from C-band quantum signals, creating a previously unrecognized wavelength-resolved side channel. Showed that even extremely weak emission can lead to non-negligible information leakage, enabling potential wavelength-splitting attacks without disturbing encoded quantum states.
AI.conclusion: Reveals fundamental and previously overlooked security risk in photonic integrated QKD systems, highlighting necessity of security-aware device design for future integrated quantum communication technologies.

title: Classical counterparts of shortcuts to adiabaticity in nonlinear dissipative Lagrangian systems
summary: Shortcuts to adiabaticity (STA) were first developed in quantum dynamics to realize rapid transformations with suppressed residual excitations. Here we show how the same idea can be implemented in classical nonlinear dissipative Lagrangian systems. Using a coupled $r$-$θ$ manipulator as an illustrative model, we perform inverse engineering on the Euler-Lagrange equations with Rayleigh dissipation by prescribing endpoint-stationary trajectories, obtaining the corresponding force and torque profiles and quantifying how geometric coupling amplifies errors and residual energy. We further compare smooth STA protocols with actuator-bounded time-optimal solutions and with proportional-integral-derivative tracking, which highlights a trade-off among smoothness, speed, and robustness. Finally, we introduce a single-shot correction based on one mid-course measurement to reduce the effect of early deviations while keeping the inputs nearly smooth. These results provide a practical bridge between quantum STA concepts and their classical counterparts.

AI.tldr: Classical implementation of shortcuts to adiabaticity (STA) in nonlinear dissipative Lagrangian systems, demonstrated on a coupled manipulator with analysis of error amplification, protocol comparisons, and mid-course correction.
AI.motivation: To bridge quantum STA concepts to classical nonlinear dissipative systems, enabling rapid transformations with suppressed residual excitations in classical mechanical systems.
AI.method: Inverse engineering on Euler-Lagrange equations with Rayleigh dissipation using a coupled r-θ manipulator model, prescribing endpoint-stationary trajectories to obtain force/torque profiles. Comparison of smooth STA protocols with time-optimal solutions and PID tracking. Introduction of single-shot mid-course correction.
AI.result: Demonstrated classical STA implementation, quantified geometric coupling's error amplification effects, revealed trade-offs among smoothness, speed, and robustness, and showed mid-course correction reduces early deviation effects while maintaining input smoothness.
AI.conclusion: Provides practical bridge between quantum STA concepts and classical counterparts, enabling rapid transformations with suppressed excitations in nonlinear dissipative Lagrangian systems.

title: Recurrence analysis of quantum many-body dynamics
summary: Observables of out-of-equilibrium quantum many-body systems display complex temporal behavior that encodes the underlying physical mechanisms but typically resists straightforward interpretations. We introduce recurrence analysis - a nonlinear time-series analysis framework long established for classical dynamical systems - to investigate correlated quantum many-body dynamics. Recurrence plots provide a qualitative fingerprint of simulated or experimental data, while recurrence quantification analysis extracts corresponding numerical descriptors. Applying this framework to quenches from the paramagnetic ground state in the one-dimensional transverse-field Ising model, we observe a clear progression in the recurrence plots of two-site correlations: nearly periodic patterns in the deeply ferromagnetic phase give way to multiscale temporal structures at criticality. Recurrence quantifiers further recover the critical field strength without prior knowledge of the model, establishing recurrence analysis as a versatile tool for characterizing quantum many-body dynamics, including unsupervised detection of quantum phase transitions.

AI.tldr: Recurrence analysis, a classical nonlinear time-series method, is adapted to study quantum many-body dynamics, enabling visualization via recurrence plots and quantification via numerical descriptors, with applications to detecting quantum phase transitions in the transverse-field Ising model.
