AI.motivation: Out-of-equilibrium quantum many-body systems exhibit complex temporal behavior that is difficult to interpret using conventional methods, necessitating new analysis frameworks to characterize and understand these dynamics.
AI.method: Introduces recurrence analysis - a nonlinear time-series analysis framework from classical dynamical systems - to quantum many-body dynamics. Uses recurrence plots for qualitative visualization and recurrence quantification analysis for numerical descriptors. Applies this to quenches from paramagnetic ground states in the 1D transverse-field Ising model, analyzing two-site correlations.
AI.result: Recurrence plots show clear progression: nearly periodic patterns in deeply ferromagnetic phase transition to multiscale temporal structures at criticality. Recurrence quantifiers successfully recover the critical field strength without prior model knowledge, demonstrating unsupervised detection of quantum phase transitions.
AI.conclusion: Recurrence analysis is established as a versatile tool for characterizing quantum many-body dynamics, providing both qualitative fingerprints and quantitative descriptors, with particular utility for unsupervised detection of quantum phase transitions in out-of-equilibrium systems.

title: Disorder-induced non-Gaussian states in large ensembles of cavity-coupled molecules
summary: We analyze vibrational dynamics in a toy model for polaritonic chemistry under collective electronic strong coupling. In a Holstein-Tavis-Cummings model, incoherently excited by a photon, we show that disorder leads to non-Gaussian states of vibrational modes on short time scales at the single-molecule level. Using exact matrix product state simulations, we demonstrate that this effect can remain robust for larger molecule numbers, implying that nuclear wave packets cannot be effectively described by thermal states. Furthermore, we compare simulations of the exact quantum dynamics with semiclassical approximations. We find that the Ehrenfest approximation can only well reproduce ensemble-averaged observables for very large system sizes. Also simulations in the truncated Wigner approximation fail to capture the non-Gaussian effects. Our work highlights the importance of disorder and genuine quantum effects in cavity-modified nuclear dynamics in polaritonic chemistry.

AI.tldr: Disorder in polaritonic chemistry leads to non-Gaussian vibrational states that resist semiclassical approximations, highlighting quantum effects in cavity-modified nuclear dynamics.
AI.motivation: To understand how disorder affects vibrational dynamics in polaritonic chemistry under collective electronic strong coupling, particularly whether nuclear wave packets can be described by thermal states or require quantum treatment.
AI.method: Used Holstein-Tavis-Cummings model with incoherent photon excitation, performed exact matrix product state simulations for quantum dynamics, and compared with semiclassical approximations (Ehrenfest and truncated Wigner approximation).
AI.result: Disorder leads to non-Gaussian vibrational states at single-molecule level; effect remains robust for larger molecule numbers; Ehrenfest approximation only works for ensemble averages in large systems; truncated Wigner fails to capture non-Gaussian effects.
AI.conclusion: Disorder and genuine quantum effects are crucial in cavity-modified nuclear dynamics in polaritonic chemistry, as nuclear wave packets cannot be effectively described by thermal states or semiclassical approximations.

title: Random-State Generation and Preparation Complexity in Rydberg Atom Arrays
summary: Rydberg atom arrays are powerful platforms for studying quantum many-body systems. We consider the Rydberg-Ising Hamiltonian on periodic chains and numerically study ensembles of states generated by random global pulse sequences subject to hardware constraints and fixed evolution times. We compare the statistical properties of such states with those of Haar-random states within the relevant lattice symmetry sector. In the strong-interaction regime (short interatomic distance), the dynamics is governed by an effective blockade that restricts Hilbert-space exploration and limits entanglement growth. In this regime, level-spacing statistics of reduced density matrices are close to random-matrix predictions, while the distribution of measurement probabilities deviates from Porter-Thomas behavior. For weaker interactions (larger interatomic distance), the system approaches Haar-like statistics at long times, as reflected in entanglement entropy, entanglement spectrum statistics, and the distribution of measurement probabilities. At intermediate interactions, this behavior is observed on experimentally relevant timescales. Motivated by this observation, we investigate whether generic symmetric quantum states can be efficiently prepared using quantum optimal control in this regime. Employing target states drawn from an ensemble with a broad entropy distribution, we observe high fidelities (infidelities between $10^{-5}$ and $3\times 10^{-2}$ for 9 spins). The fidelity, however, decreases with the entanglement entropy of the target state, demonstrating that highly entangled states are intrinsically harder to prepare under realistic constraints.

AI.tldr: Rydberg atom arrays with random pulse sequences show different statistical behaviors depending on interaction strength: strong interactions limit entanglement and deviate from random matrix predictions, while weaker interactions approach Haar-random statistics, enabling efficient quantum optimal control for preparing generic symmetric states.
AI.motivation: To understand how Rydberg atom arrays under random global pulse sequences can generate quantum states with statistical properties comparable to Haar-random states, and to investigate whether such systems can be used for efficient preparation of generic symmetric quantum states via optimal control.
AI.method: Numerical study of Rydberg-Ising Hamiltonian on periodic chains with random global pulse sequences subject to hardware constraints and fixed evolution times; comparison with Haar-random states within lattice symmetry sector; analysis of statistical properties including level-spacing statistics, entanglement entropy, entanglement spectrum statistics, and measurement probability distributions; quantum optimal control applied to prepare target states drawn from ensemble with broad entropy distribution.
AI.result: Strong interactions (short interatomic distance) show effective blockade limiting Hilbert-space exploration and entanglement growth, with level-spacing statistics close to random-matrix predictions but measurement probability distributions deviating from Porter-Thomas. Weaker interactions (larger interatomic distance) approach Haar-like statistics at long times. At intermediate interactions, this behavior occurs on experimentally relevant timescales. Quantum optimal control achieves high fidelities (infidelities 10^-5 to 3×10^-2 for 9 spins), but fidelity decreases with target state entanglement entropy.
AI.conclusion: Rydberg atom arrays with appropriate interaction strengths can generate states with Haar-like statistics, enabling efficient preparation of generic symmetric quantum states via optimal control, though preparation fidelity is limited by the entanglement entropy of target states under realistic constraints.

title: Scaling of Quantum Resources for Simulating a Long-Range System
summary: We simulate a long-range extended Ising model in one dimension using a hybrid quantum algorithm, namely Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE). In this quantum simulation, we investigate how quantum resources scale with system size and interaction strength. Three structure-aware ansatze incorporating nearest-neighbor (NN), next-nearest-neighbor (NNN), and next-next-nearest-neighbor (NNNN) entangling blocks are constructed by mimicking the string operators in the Hamiltonian. We show that energy fidelity alone is not a good indicator for finding the ground state of our model. To overcome this problem, we introduce an additional criterion based on pairwise logarithmic negativity as a more reliable way to find the actual ground state by the VQE. We find that the interaction range parameter alpha primarily governs the minimum number of ansatz layers required, rather than proximity to the quantum critical point. In particular, we show that in the non-local regime (alpha <= 1), the NNN and NNNN ansatze reduce the layer scaling rate by factors of 2.5x and 3.8x relative to NN in all phases, including the critical point. The total number of two-qubit gates required for reliable simulation grows quadratically with system size for all three ansatze. This is consistent with the theoretical prediction, as the number of non-local terms in the Hamiltonian also grows quadratically with the system size. In the local regime, however, the number of required two-qubit gates grows linearly with system size. In contrast, in the quasi-local regime, the required number of two-qubit gates for the quantum simulation is more subtle and depends on the phase of the Hamiltonian.

AI.tldr: VQE simulation of 1D long-range Ising model shows energy fidelity insufficient for ground state identification; pairwise logarithmic negativity introduced as better criterion. Interaction range α governs ansatz layer requirements, with NNN/NNNN ansatze reducing layer scaling by 2.5-3.8× in non-local regime. Two-qubit gate scaling varies: quadratic in non-local regime, linear in local regime, phase-dependent in quasi-local regime.
AI.motivation: To investigate quantum resource scaling for simulating long-range extended Ising models using VQE, addressing limitations of energy fidelity as ground state indicator and exploring how interaction range affects ansatz requirements.
AI.method: Hybrid quantum algorithm using VQE with structure-aware ansatze incorporating NN, NNN, and NNNN entangling blocks mimicking Hamiltonian string operators. Introduced pairwise logarithmic negativity as additional criterion beyond energy fidelity for ground state identification.
AI.result: Energy fidelity alone insufficient for ground state identification; pairwise logarithmic negativity provides reliable alternative. Interaction range α primarily determines minimum ansatz layers required. In non-local regime (α≤1), NNN and NNNN ansatze reduce layer scaling by 2.5× and 3.8× relative to NN across all phases. Two-qubit gate scaling: quadratic with system size in non-local regime, linear in local regime, phase-dependent in quasi-local regime.
AI.conclusion: Structure-aware ansatze significantly reduce quantum resource requirements for long-range Ising model simulation. Pairwise logarithmic negativity is crucial for reliable ground state identification. Interaction range α, not proximity to critical point, governs ansatz complexity. Gate scaling transitions from linear to quadratic based on interaction locality, with quasi-local regime showing phase-dependent behavior.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Maximizing Quantum Fisher Information in Time-Dependent Many-Body Systems
summary: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) sets the ultimate precision limit for parameter estimation and is therefore a central quantity in quantum metrology. In time-dependent many-body systems, however, maximizing QFI is a highly non-trivial task due to the combined effects of non-commutativity, control complexity, and the exponential growth of the Hilbert space. In this work, we present a physics-informed neural network (PINN) framework to address this problem through the learning of counter-diabatic quantum dynamics. Our approach combines a variational PINN formulation with a Magnus-expansion treatment of time-ordered evolution, enabling the adiabatic gauge potential and the scheduling function to be inferred directly from the underlying physics while enforcing the Euler-Lagrange structure of the protocol. The method is applied to several families of driven spin Hamiltonians, including nearest-neighbor, dipolar, and trapped-ion-inspired interactions, for systems of up to six qubits. The numerical results show that the proposed framework systematically improves over reference solutions based only on the Euler-Lagrange condition, yielding high normalized QFI together with favorable fidelity and extremal-balance metrics while preserving small phsical residuals. The analysis further shows that learning the scheduling function provides a clear performance advantage in most cases, and reveals non-trivial finite-size effects, with $q=3$ emerging as a particularly challenging regime. Although scalability remains limited by the exponential growth of the operator space and by automatic-differentiation costs, the results demonstrate that PINNs constitute a viable and physically grounded route for learning metrologically optimal control strategies in interacting quantum systems.

AI.tldr: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) learn counter-diabatic quantum dynamics to maximize Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) in time-dependent many-body systems, outperforming Euler-Lagrange-only solutions for up to 6-qubit spin systems.
AI.motivation: Maximizing Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) in time-dependent many-body systems is challenging due to non-commutativity, control complexity, and exponential Hilbert space growth. A physics-grounded approach is needed to find metrologically optimal control strategies.
AI.method: Combines variational PINN formulation with Magnus-expansion treatment of time-ordered evolution to learn counter-diabatic quantum dynamics. The framework infers adiabatic gauge potential and scheduling function directly from underlying physics while enforcing Euler-Lagrange protocol structure.
AI.result: Applied to driven spin Hamiltonians (nearest-neighbor, dipolar, trapped-ion-inspired) for up to 6 qubits. Systematically improves over Euler-Lagrange-only solutions, achieving high normalized QFI with favorable fidelity and extremal-balance metrics while maintaining small physical residuals. Learning scheduling function provides performance advantage; reveals non-trivial finite-size effects with q=3 as particularly challenging regime.
AI.conclusion: PINNs constitute a viable and physically grounded route for learning metrologically optimal control strategies in interacting quantum systems, though scalability is limited by exponential operator space growth and automatic-differentiation costs.

title: AtomTwin.jl: a physics-native digital twin framework for neutral-atom quantum processors
summary: AtomTwin.jl is an open-source Julia package for developing and simulating quantum protocols, hardware configurations and building digital twins for neutral-atom quantum processors and related atomic quantum devices. AtomTwin operates between mathematical models and physical devices; modeling atoms, optical tweezers, laser fields, atomic motion, interactions, and noise processes natively from physical geometry and parameters, without requiring users to define Hamiltonians manually. The package provides hardware-level instruction sequences, high-performance solvers for coupled quantum and classical dynamics, and a ready-to-use model for ytterbium-171 atoms in an extensible framework designed to accommodate a greater variety of atomic species and hardware components in the future. This paper describes the software architecture, performance benchmarks against existing toolboxes, and a demonstrated end-to-end application: preparation of a logical Bell state in the $[[4,2,2]]$ error-detecting code with four $^{171}$Yb atoms in moveable tweezers.

AI.tldr: AtomTwin.jl is an open-source Julia package for simulating neutral-atom quantum processors, providing hardware-level modeling of atoms, optical tweezers, laser fields, and noise processes without requiring manual Hamiltonian definition.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between mathematical models and physical devices for neutral-atom quantum processors, enabling development of quantum protocols, hardware configurations, and digital twins without requiring users to manually define complex Hamiltonians.
AI.method: Developed an extensible Julia framework that models atoms, optical tweezers, laser fields, atomic motion, interactions, and noise processes natively from physical geometry and parameters. Provides hardware-level instruction sequences, high-performance solvers for coupled quantum and classical dynamics, and includes a ready-to-use model for ytterbium-171 atoms.
AI.result: The paper presents software architecture details, performance benchmarks against existing toolboxes, and demonstrates an end-to-end application: preparation of a logical Bell state in the [[4,2,2]] error-detecting code with four 171Yb atoms in moveable tweezers.
AI.conclusion: AtomTwin.jl successfully provides a comprehensive simulation framework for neutral-atom quantum processors that operates directly from physical parameters, enabling realistic modeling of quantum protocols and hardware configurations while maintaining extensibility for future atomic species and hardware components.

title: Hamiltonian dynamics from pure dissipation
summary: The fundamental difference between closed and open quantum dynamics lies in their environmental interaction: closed systems are perfectly isolated and evolve reversibly under unitary Hamiltonian dynamics, whereas open systems continuously couple to an external bath, resulting in irreversible dissipation and information loss. In this work, we show internal Hamiltonian dynamics can be "faked`` via external pure dissipation, i.e., Lindbladians without a coherent Hamiltonian part. More concretely, we show that, in a GKSL representation with zero explicit Hamiltonian term but nontraceless jump operators, bounded-norm dissipative generators can approximate Hamiltonian dynamics within $ε$ error in diamond norm using $\mathcal{O}(t^2/ε)$ evolution time. We further prove that for time-independent dynamics this $\mathcal{O}(t^2/ε)$ scaling is in the worst case, necessary and optimal from a geometric perspective, which captures the fundamental decoherence cost for catching up with the speed of Hamiltonian dynamics. Our construction leads to various implications, including the BQP-completeness of purely dissipative dynamics even before reaching approximate equilibrium, a Zeno-adjacent state-independent freezing effect, the no super-quadratic fast-forwarding theorem of a class of purely dissipative dynamics, and reducing Lindbladian simulation cost via gauge changing.

AI.tldr: Purely dissipative Lindbladians (without Hamiltonian terms) can simulate Hamiltonian dynamics with O(t²/ε) evolution time, proving optimal scaling and revealing fundamental decoherence costs.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether purely dissipative quantum dynamics (Lindbladians without Hamiltonian terms) can effectively simulate coherent Hamiltonian dynamics, bridging the gap between closed and open quantum systems.
AI.method: Using GKSL representation with zero explicit Hamiltonian term but nontraceless jump operators, constructing bounded-norm dissipative generators that approximate Hamiltonian dynamics within ε error in diamond norm.
AI.result: Achieved O(t²/ε) evolution time scaling for Hamiltonian simulation via pure dissipation, proved this scaling is worst-case optimal, and derived various implications including BQP-completeness of purely dissipative dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Hamiltonian dynamics can be effectively "faked" by purely dissipative processes with fundamental O(t²/ε) decoherence cost, revealing deep connections between coherent and dissipative quantum evolution.

title: Dissipative Preparation of Correlated Quantum States in Dipolar Rydberg Arrays
summary: Preparing correlated quantum states is essential for emerging technologies, but remains challenging in many-body systems. Here we propose a dissipative protocol that engineers nonreciprocal, energy-selective transitions to steer dipolar quantum systems toward desired many-body states. This is realized by introducing two types of controllable dissipative auxiliary atoms that act as nonreciprocal excitation and de-excitation channels, respectively, enabling a directional walk in Hilbert space. This approach enables stabilization of states across the many-body spectrum, not limited to the ground state and requiring no \textit{a priori} knowledge of the Hamiltonian. Our approach is designed for neutral atoms in dipolar Rydberg arrays, but applies broadly to setups with similar capabilities, providing a flexible and scalable framework for state preparation in programmable platforms.

AI.tldr: A dissipative protocol using nonreciprocal, energy-selective transitions to steer dipolar quantum systems toward desired many-body states via controllable auxiliary atoms acting as directional channels.
AI.motivation: Preparing correlated quantum states is essential for emerging technologies but remains challenging in many-body systems, requiring new approaches beyond ground state preparation.
AI.method: Introduces two types of controllable dissipative auxiliary atoms that act as nonreciprocal excitation and de-excitation channels, enabling directional walk in Hilbert space through energy-selective transitions.
AI.result: Enables stabilization of states across the many-body spectrum, not limited to ground states, without requiring a priori knowledge of the Hamiltonian. Designed for neutral atoms in dipolar Rydberg arrays but broadly applicable to similar setups.
AI.conclusion: Provides a flexible and scalable framework for state preparation in programmable quantum platforms using dissipative engineering with nonreciprocal transitions.
